paragraphs.text: A GOD-PLEASING LIFE have reinforced it, and thus made up your mind. I predict to you that you will find complete peace, and that you will no longer be troubled by such thoughts as “My life is not going anywhere...! am not doing anything useful.” There is just one other thing; it is necessary to keep the heart in check, because it takes pride in such nonsense. True, it is no good without the heart, for what kind of life is it without the heart? All the same, it is not good to give it free reign. It is blind, and without strict guidance one immediately falls into a ditch!
May the Lord bless you! 17 A GOD-PLEASING LIFE Contributions to the heavenly treasury. A God-pleasing life.
Dreams ofthe progressives about the universal goodness ofman and theirfalsehood.
I am very glad that you took to heart what was written in the last letter, and that you agree to behave in just such a manner.
God gave us this life so that we would have time to prepare for the next one. This one is short, but the next one has no end.

Although this life is short, during its course one may lay in provisions for all of eternity. Each good work goes toward this end like a small contribution; from all such contributions comes the overall capital, the interest of which will determine the upkeep of the saver for all of eternity. He who makes larger contributions will have a richer upkeep, while he who makes smaller contributions will have a lesser upkeep. The Lord will render to each one according to his works. 91

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE We must presently devote all our concern to making a few more contributions to that account. This concern is neither complicated nor difficult, as the Lord Himself confirms when He says For Ady yoke is ecisyy and Ady burden is light (Matthew 1 1 :30). I have explained this to you with respect to the thoughts that have troubled you in order to assuage your sorrow, as if you lived without being useful and were to spend your entire life without being useful, if you were to continue living as you are living now. For the entire order of Christian life is thus: Believe in God, in the worshipful Trinity that saves us in the Lord Jesus Christ through the benevolence of the Holy Spirit and, receiving the beneficial powers through the Divine mysteries of the Holy Church, live according to the commandments of the Gospel, being inspired with the hope that God, for the smallest feasible labor of ours, for the sake of faith in the Lord Savior and obedience to Him, will not deprive us of heavenly blessings.

This I am adding intentionally, so that you see in what spirit we must perform a real Christian’s works. For some people say that good works are necessary, while others say that faith is necessary. But both are needed; faith must be combined with works, and works with faith.

All the same, attention must be concentrated principally on carrying out the commandments, W^e already believe, what else is needed? Carry out the commandments, for faith without works is dead. ^ Also, thankfulness to the Lord: that it suited Him to determine the value of our works, not by their magnitude or greatness, but by our inward disposition when carrying them out, having at the same time given us a multiplicity of opportunities for performing good works according to His will, so that, if we pay attention to ourselves, we may at every moment perform God-pleasing works. There is no need to go abroad for 1 . But wilt thou know, O vain man, thatfaith without works is deads' (James 2 : 20) 92

A GOD-PLEASING LIFE this, as the progressives do. Instead, just look around yourself each day and each hour; on whatever you see the seal of the commandment, carry it out immediately, in the belief that God Himself this very hour rec^uires this work of you, and nothing else.

Work at becoming even more strongly fixed in such thought. As soon as you have done so, peace will begin pouring into your heart from the belief that each moment you are working for the Lord. This beginning embraces everything.
Even when you are told to darn your youngest brother’s sock, and you do it for the sake of the Lord’s commandment to listen and help, this will be numbered among the God-pleasing works.
Thus each step, each word, even each movement and glance, everything may direct one to walk in God’s will and, consequently, to move each moment toward the ultimate goal.

The progressives have in mind all mankind or at least all of its people lumped together. The fact is, however, that “mankind, or the people” does not exist as a person for whom you could do something right now. It consists of individual persons: By doing something for one person, we are doing it within the general mass of humanity. If each one of us did what was possible to do for whoever was standing right in front of our eyes, instead of goggling at the community of mankind, then all people, in aggregate, would at each moment be doing that which is needed by those in need, and, by satisfying their needs, would establish the welfare of all mankind, which is made up of haves and have-nots, the weak and the strong. But those who keep thoughts of the welfare of all mankind inattentively let slip by that which is in front of their eyes. Because they do not have the opportunity to perform a general work, and let slip by the opportunity to perform a particular work, they accomplish nothing towards the main purpose of life. 93

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE I was told of such an instance in St. Petersburg. There was some sort of meeting of young people who were advocates of universal welfare—this was at the very height of progressive raving. One gentleman was making an impassioned speech about love for mankind and the people. Everyone was enraptured. But when he returned home, his servant did not open the door quickly enough—he had not seen him coming. The servant did not give him a candle quickly enough, something had happened to his pipe, and it was a little cold in his room.

Our philanthropist could not stand this, and finally, he sharply reprimanded his servant. The latter answered something back, and the former struck him in the chest. And so here is our fine fellow, who was overflowing with love toward mankind in one place, who could not behave properly toward even one person at home. Also at the very height of progressivist raving, there were pretty girls who threw themselves into the work of bookbinding establishments, who often left their mothers without a crust of bread. All the same, they imagined that they were in some way moving forward and establishing the happiness of mankind. All troubles come from a mental outlook that is too broad. It is better to humbly cast your eyes down toward your feet, and to figure out which step to take where. This is the truest path.
This is the second time I have told you about all this, in order that all of it become more firmly entrenched in your mind.
I am guarding you from the obfuscation that progressivist dreams direct at the soul. 94

THE ONE THING NEEDFUL The significance ofspiritual needs in the order ofthe other aspects ofhuman life. The one thing needful. The supremacy of the spirit as the natural harmony ofall needs which are able to give peace and rest. The absence ofthis peace in mankind.
Universal vanity and the downfall ofthe spirit. The seed of this confusion is received together with one's birth.
Now there is nothing to prevent us from turning our minds to a solution of the remaining problem. To reacquaint ourselves with the subject, however, let us briefly recall what has been said already.

We agreed that man has three (or five, including the intermediate) levels of life, the spiritual, intellectual and corporeal; that each one of them has its aggregate of needs, those natural and peculiar to man; that these needs are not all of equal worth, but instead, some are higher, and others lower; and that the balanced satisfaction of them gives man peace. Spiritual needs are above all, and when they are satisfied, then even though the others are not satisfied, peace exists; but when the spiritual needs are not satisfied, then, even though all other needs are richly satisfied, there is no peace. That is why the satisfaction of them is called the one thing needful. ^X^hen the spiritual needs are met, they teach a person to harmonize with those needs the satisfaction of the other needs, so that neither the needs of the intellect nor the needs of the body interfere with the spiritual life, but, instead, aid it. Then within a person is established complete harmony of all motions and revelations of his life. There is a harmony of thoughts. 95

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE feelings, desires, undertakings, relationships, pleasures. And this is Paradise! Contrary to this, when the spirit is not satisfied, and this one thing needful is forgotten, then each of these other needs runs off in a different direction, each one demands fulfillment of its own needs, and their muddle and cry, like noise at a bazaar, deafen the poor person, and he runs around like a madman trying to satisfy them. But he never has peace, because when one need is being satisfied, the others are not, and, not considering themselves to be inferior to that need which is being honored with fulfillment, persistently demand their own satisfaction, just as children make demands of their mother when she is feeding one child, and the other five are crying. That is why there is noise, racket, agitation on all sides and disorder in everything inside such a person. This is just how the turbulent zone that we have been discussing in general comes into being, and it even further disturbs and disorders everything that is within. Such a person is never satisfied with anything, always has difficulties, stands about wearily, and having looked about himself is bewildered where to begin, and so he goes dizzily round and round. This is the vanity and downfall of the soul!
This is also the starting point of the question, just how is one to be? What must be done, in order that the spiritual aspect be made supreme in us, and, by ruling everything, put our lives in order?

I will tell you right now that this question would be pointless if the life peculiar to us developed as it is supposed to. For both the bodily and intellectual needs are natural to us, just as are the spiritual needs; and the satisfaction of them cannot in and of itself create disorder and confusion in our lives anymore than does the satisfaction of the spiritual needs. Develop them all in harmony and in natural mutual subordination, and then human life would flow like a charm! Plant a seed, water it, keep it in the warmth it needs, and it will put out a sprout, and then a stem, 96

THE ONE THING NEEDFUL and a leaf, and finally a beautiful flower. That is how it would be with a person. Let him open himself in the natural order, and you would always have an excellent person as the result. But why is it, then, that we do not see this excellence in ourselves or others? How do our lives get distorted?

Recall how you felt after you were immersed for a little while in that whirlpool or turbulent life. Indeed, you experienced a lot of inner confusion. If this were to be repeated a few times, if you did not take some precautions, you would be caught up in that very same whirlpool—and you would have that life which is vain, and the downfall of the spirit. Thus, could we not explain our disorder by saying that it is caused by disorderly people, that we are attracted to them, and we take on their disorderliness. You have just now come under the influence of this disorderliness, because your upbringing was under the regulating influence ofyour devout family and relatives. Others, however, whose families and relatives live in turbulence and confusion, take on this confusion from childhood and grow up in it. Perhaps this is the reason why our lives are diverted from their natural course, and unnatural confusion enters within them.
No, this does not explain the matter, for then the question still remains: How did confusion enter into this group of people?

Why does it exist in families and society? What is outside of a man is a manifestation of that which is within him. Disorder enters into the family and society from man. There is nothing within him which will not be manifested eventually outside of him. Then the outer gives back to the inner that which it has received from the inner. Once again the question arises: How did this confusion enter into man? Again, I would say the outer turbulence influences the inside, and reproduces itself there, because the seed of it and predisposition to it is already within. 97

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE You were confused and could not Find peace. Why? You were silent about it, but I did not tell you, because it was not the right time. Now I will tell you. During those two days, when you were immersed in the turbulent life, you were carried away, your heart sympathized, if not with everything, then with many things. It was from these sympathies that turbulence and confusion arose within you. When you came to yourself, you saw something new within yourself. Your conscience reported to you that this new thing was your doing, and that it was improper, and it began to punish you. Heeding this voice, you then considered everything carefully and correctly appraised that way of life. After this, if you should happen to fall again into this confusion, and if, as I have told you more than once, you keep your heart detached, not letting it have its will, then it will begin to break with its sympathies, and you will not experience the anguish which you experienced. I am reminding you of this, however, in passing. Because what I am talking about is how you, who have lived in a different order up to this point, could have sympathy for disorder. Think about it, and you will find that the sympathy exists, and had existed previously within, but it was hidden, and now has come outside. Outside influences do not create anything new, but merely act on what is already within. I will tell you an example. I have seen lepers.

Their children are born healthy and normal-looking, and they grow up healthy. But the time comes when the leprosy manifests itself in them, and they begin to be like their parents and all other lepers. Where did the leprosy come from? From within!
And how did it get there? It was there at birth.

From this it will be simple for you to make the conclusion that, if we see within ourselves disorderliness and confusion — thoughts, feelings, desires, undertakings, acts—and if we swirl around in them, it is because the seed of all of this has entered our nature, and this seed of disorderliness, being unleashed 98
ANCESTRAL SIN along with the unleashing of our forces and needs, introduces confusion within us and causes our lives in this way to be confused and turbulent.

Think over now, if you will, where this seed comes from. I Think this over, if you will,” because we can in no other way find a satisfactory answer to the question which has already been repeated several times: How is one to be? \^hoever wishes to successfully treat a disease must find its cause. Without that, the cure cannot have a correct course and success.
That is what must be discussed at this point. 19 ANCESTRAL SIN Ancestral sin as the source ofinner confusion and disorder. The possibility oftreatment ofthe condition ofa man harmed by sin.

Although I wrote you to think over the problem of where the seed of inner confusion and disorderliness comes from, and I myself promised to discuss it, indeed it is already known, and you know where it comes from. There is no reason to think it over, but merely to recall what we believe about this.

We believe that such seed was not introduced into our nature by the Creator during man’s creation. Instead, it was introduced after the creation, by the power of the fall of our ancestofs who, having transgressed the commandment, damaged and disordered our nature, which was transferred as damaged and disordered to their descendants, that is, to all mankind. Thus it has come down even to us and to you, and serves as the reason for the confusion and disorderliness within us, from which comes all disorder outside, and in the personal life of each person, as well as in family life and life in society. 99

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Why this disorderliness? Although the seed of it is received by us at birth, it is not natural to us. It does not belong to human nature in such a way that without it a human being is not a human being; on the contrary, without it, a human being becomes a true human being. It is our disease; and only by eliminating it are we able to be healthy in the way in which we are supposed to be by nature, as it was given to us from the hand of the Creator. I recall your desire to come to the level of human dignity.” Treat the aforementioned disease within yourself, and you will come to it.

Recall that our inner confusion and disorderliness are innate, but that they are not natural; they are not an essential part of our nature. Instead, they are crowded among our natural parts, disordering each one in its own activity, and in its mutual relationships with other parts. If this were natural, then their presence would not cause the suffering and tortures which we experience from them. That which is natural is what constitutes joy in life, but that which is unnatural oppresses it and tortures it. On the other hand, if it were natural, then, if someone were to be free and pure from it, then he would not be a human being. But we know a Man, W^ho was completely pure from it, and Who was all the same a human being. We also know, that everyone who puts Him on receives the power to purify himself, even as He is pure (1 John 3:3), and to become like Him.

Recall, I say, that this disorderliness is innate within us, but that it is not natural; recall, and maintain a firm conviction about it. This conviction will support within you a strong desire for curing this disease. If it is not natural, then this means that there is the possibility for it to be cured. By having such hope, who would not be inspired and cure himself as a result? Our nature in pure form is good. The angels themselves look on it with love and wonder, when it appears as such. Wouldn t we lOO

THE NATURE OF MAN also like to see it as such—and inwardly too, besides—in others and in ourselves? Truly, all our happiness and wellbeing consists in our deliverance from this disease. For, when it no longer exists, what would prevent us from being in a blessed state, and to feel ourselves as such? Contrary to this, if this disease is natural, then it cannot be cured. Thus it would remain always, no matter how hard you worked to rid yourself of it. If you accept this thought, you will lose heart, and say to yourself: this is how it is. For this is that woeful despair, which, once it has been introduced into people, they have given themselves over to lasciviousness, to work all uncleanness (Ephesians 4:19).

I shall repeat again: Maintain the conviction that our disorderliness is not natural to us, and do not listen to those who say, “It is no use talking about it, because that is just how we are made, and you cannot do anything about it.” That is not how we are made, and if we undertake to cure ourselves, then we will be able to do something about it.
I am telling you all this to inspire you to undertake this and so that you will not subject yourself again to such torments as you experienced, but instead cut off the very possibility of such.
It should be considered how this may be achieved. 20 .

THE DISTURBANCE IN THE NATURE OF MAN Continuation. Clarification ofthe disturbance which occurred in the nature ofman by the sin oftheforefather. Communion with Godfunctions as the source ofthe dominion ofthe spirit over the soul and body. Through the violation ofthe commandments man separated himselffrom God and lost dominion over the soul and lOI
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE body and becciTTie subjected to the doTTiinion ofthe passions.
The depiction ofman laid waste by a passionatefall.

I shall continue. What exactly happened to us as a result of the transgression of our forebears? Our nature remained the same, the parts and forces of our substance remained the same, with the same laws and needs. Our consciousness, however, did not freely set out in the same direction. The passions disturbed the mutual correlation of our parts and forces, and, by violating their original order, introduced disorder into the overall activity and life of man, engendering from themselves a special class of destructive forces. These passions are not natural to us, but have such power as to control all of our forces as it suits them. That is what happened.

God created man to be happy namely in Him, through living interaction with Him. For this. He breathed into his face the breath of His life, that is, the spirit, as has been mentioned already. The essential attributes of the spirit are consciousness and freedom, while the essential movements of it are confession of God the Creator, Providence and Requiter; with a feeling of complete dependence upon Him; a feeling that everything is expressed with a loving view towards God, in unceasing attention to Him and reverential fear before Him; with the desire to create always that which is pleasing before Him in accordance with the statutes of the conscience; and with renunciation of everything, in order to taste the One God and to live and rejoice with Him alone. Man has been given consciousness and freedom in the spirit, not so that he may become conceited and do as he pleases, but so that, having acknowledged that everything he has is from God, he may live in God, and freely and consciously head toward that one goal. When he is so disposed, then he abides in God, and God abides in him. When God abides in a man. He gives his spirit the power to control the 102

THE NATURE OF MAN soul and body, and also everything that is outside of him. This was the original condition of man. God appeared to our forebears and confirmed all of this by His Divine word, after ordering them to know Him alone, to serve Him alone, to walk in His will alone. So that they would not get confused when considering how to carry out all of this. He gave them a small commandment: Do not taste of the fruit of one of the trees, which He called the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
That is how our forebears began to live and to rejoice in Paradise.

A spirit, which fell primarily because of pride, envied them and knocked them from the path after inciting them to break the small commandment that was given them. He flatteringly suggested that by tasting the forbidden fruit, they would taste something so good that they just could not imagine; they would become like gods. They believed, and. ..they tasted. The matter of tasting, perhaps, is not significant; what is harmful is that they believed, without knowing who it was they believed. Perhaps even that would not have been so bad, if the evil spirit had not poured such horribly offensive thoughts and feelings toward God into them, like poison. He convinced them that God forbade them to eat from the tree because by doing so, they too, would become gods. That is what they believed. Having believed this, they could not help but have blasphemous thoughts about God, such as the idea that He envied them and treated them without benevolence. Having accepted such thoughts, they could not avoid certain bad feelings toward Him or selfwilled decrees: “We ourselves will take what Thou dost not want to allow us to have. This is what He is like, and we thought that He was so good! Well, we will just have to fix things up for ourselves in defiance of Him!” This was what was lodged into their hearts about God. Such thoughts and feelings were horribly bl asphemous! It is these thoughts and feelings which signify the falling away from God and the inimical rebellion against 103

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Him. Something else also happened within them, which is ascribed to the evil spirit: “I shall set my throne above the clouds and I shall be equal to the Most High.” This was not just an idle thought, but a hostile decree.

Thus, by taking upon itself the arranging of its own lot, man’s consciousness became conceited, and freedom became self-willing. The complete falling away from God was accomplished with a certain aversion and hostile revolt against Him.

It is for this reason that God abandoned such transgressors, and the living union was disrupted. God is everywhere and maintains everything, but He enters into free creatures only when they surrender themselves to Him. ^X^hen they are self-absorbed.

He does not violate their self-rule; He continues to keep and maintain them, but does not enter inside them. Thus our forebears were also left alone. If they had repented sooner, perhaps God would have returned to them, but they were stubborn, and even with obvious evidence, neither Adam nor Eve would acknowledge that they were guilty. Judgment followed, and with it the punishment of expulsion from Paradise.

Then they came to their senses, but it was too late. They had to bear the punishment laid upon them, as did our entire race after them. Thanks be to the all-merciful God, that although He turned from us. He did not discard us, and arranged a marvelous way for reunion with Him.

I have digressed a little far here. XC^e must fix our attention on what happened within man. Indeed! The spirit had authority over the soul and body, because it was in living communion with God, and from Him it received divine power. When the living communion with God was disrupted, the flow of Divine power was also disrupted. The spirit, left by itself, was not able to rule the soul and body, but was drawn to and itself captured by them. The intellect ruled over man, and through the intellect, corporeality, and man became intellectual and car104
THE NATURE OF MAN nal. Although the spirit was the same, it was without authority.

It announces its existence at times through the fear of God, at other times through the alarms ofthe conscience, at other times still through the dissatisfaction with anything created. These manifestations are not paid any heed, but are merely taken into consideration, all care being turned to the arranging of one’s life here, to which the intellect is assigned, a life more material, because life here is conducted with a body, and everything that is corporeally tangible seems necessary.

When this subversion of order in the interrelationships of the parts of our nature took place, man could no longer see things in their true perspective, and he could not keep his needs, desires and feelings in their proper place. These needs, desires and feelings were thrown into confusion, and disorder became their characteristic feature. But this condition, which is of course evil, would have been even tolerable, if the passions had not otherwise entered and tyrannized man. Have you seen how wrath racks an angry man like a fever? How envy permeates someone who is envious until the poor man is green? How a mournful man is so consumed by grief that he is reduced to skin and bones? All the passions are like this. They entered inside us along with selfishness. As soon as our forebear inwardly uttered “I myself,” selfishness took root inside him, this poison and this satanic seed. From this then developed the horde of passions; pride, envy, hatred, grief, despondency, greed and sensuality, with all of their numerous and multi-faceted consequences. Having become inbred, they agitate to an even greater degree a condition that was already perturbed without them.

That is what the illness consists of: The spirit became conceited and self-willed. Because of this it lost its authority, and fell under the rule of the soul and body and everything outward. From this came the confusion of the intellectual-physical needs and desires, and especially the infinitude of such needs 105

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and desires. This infinitude is imparted to them by the spirit from itself. In and of themselves, these needs are finite and not turbulent. The fact that they seethe without limit is because the spirit rages in them, and the spirit by nature has boundless energy. From this comes gluttony, drunkenness, avarice and many other things to which man does not think to set a limit.
But the main illness is the passions, the alien tyrants.
Now you must make a simple induction: ^JCHiat is necessary for restoring everything in us back to its original order? I will write you about this next time, and you think it over in the meantime.

THE NECESSITY OF UNION WITH GOD The necessity ofunion with Godfor salvation. Man himselfis not capable ofdoing this. The Spirit ofGod accomplishes this in us on account ofthe Redemption offered by the Son ofGod.

YC^hat is your reply to the question of what is necessary for restoring everything in us to what it was previously, to the original order? No matter what you came up with, I will continue with my own thoughts.

Since we went down the hill, we must turn around and go back up it again. How did the illness, acting in opposition to us, enter, and how may it be expelled? We have fallen away from God; what is required to be reunited with Him? We have fallen away, doubting God^s word; we must reestablish complete faith in this word. Having lost trust of and faith in God, we made the fatal decision: I myselF it is necessary to destroy that ^*I myself.” Once this occurred, our spirit lost its proper authority to rule over the soul and body, and conversely io6

UNION WITH GOD fell under the yoke of slavery to them; authority must be restored to the spirit. W^hen the authority of the spirit was disrupted, the needs of the soul and body dispersed in different directions, and within our desires there came about confusion; all of these needs must be once again brought into unity, and in them must be established an order of mutual subordination.
Along with the fatal “I myself,” a pack of fears, like wild beasts which prey on us, has encroached on the circle of our lives; these fears must be driven out.

See how much there is to be done. Already by the great number and the importance of these necessary items you may conclude that we ourselves cannot manage what is above all a matter concerning us. It is especially impossible for us to hope to rectify this matter of utmost importance to us on our own, because we have not established the primary point, without which there is no starting anything else, namely, reunion with God, which can in no way be in our power, ^^e may wish for it and seek it, but to arrange it—this is a matter which is out of our hands. Who can encroach on God’s realm, or who can himself lay the path to Him? ^X^ho is strong enough to take from God what he desires, the more so to compel God to be in us, so that we may also be in Him, and especially since all this has already been given us, but received with contempt, and the face of God met in a belittling manner with disbelief and His authority defied by self-will? You say, I will repent and I do repent. It is not up to you, however, to provide the conditions.

Perhaps repentance does indeed have its place in the matter, but only when God Himself decrees it and agrees to accept it. In and of itself, what use is it?! You end up hurting yourself badly, what good is that ?
Thus, reunion with God is not in our power, and the conditions and form of its accomplishment, and everything relating to it from this end, are out of our hands. At the same 107

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE time, it is indeed important to know that once you accomplish this reunion, everything else will start moving by itself. Instantly the spirit will gather strength, subordinate the soul and body to itself, put in order the needs and desires and expel the passions. How could this take place on its own? I am speaking like this because I want you to know that there is nothing for us to bang our heads over as to how we are to reunite with God.

There is nothing to beat your head against, nothing for you to devise. Rather, since it has pleased God to establish a law and order for this reunion, rush to accept it with complete faith and make use of it with warm gratitude. Thanks be to God the lover of mankind! Everything for reunion has already been accomplished, established and explained. Accept it and use it.

I will not go into great detail as to how all of this has been accomplished; I will tell you what is most important. For the restoration of our spirit and its reunion with God, it is necessary that the Divine Spirit descend to it and revive it. In order to open the way to the descent of the Divine Spirit, the Only-Begotten Son of God came to earth, was incarnated, suffered, died on the cross, was resurrected, and ascended into heaven. It is in this respect that the Son of God Himself turned His work over to the Holy Spirit when, after telling His disciples about His going to the Father and having filled them with sorrow. He said to them to comfort themt / tellyou the truth, it is expedient for you thut I go away: for if I go not cLwuyy the Comforter will not come unto you (John 16:7). Even before this the holy Evangelist John the Theologian, in explaining the Lord’s words He that believeth on me... out ofhis belly shallflow rivers ofliving water^ added But this spoke He ofthe Spirit, which they that believe on Him should receive: for the Holy Spirit was not yetgiven; becauseJesus was notyetglorified (John 7:38,39). It was appropriate to give praise to the Son of God, who humbled Himself in the Incarnation and suffered, in order that the Holy io8
UNION WITH GOD Spirit would come and dwell with those who believed in Him.

He came and began to abide, and His abiding in believers was so imperative, that the other Apostle inquired of them with wonder: Know you not that you are the temple of God, and that the Spirit ofGod dwells in you? (I Cor. 3:16) There is everything in its entirety for you. The Son of God was incarnated, suffered in the flesh, died on the Cross, rose again, ascended into Heaven, and sent the Holy Spirit from the Father. The Holy Spirit, Who may be received by those who believe in the Son, abides with them and carries out in them that about which the Son prayed: As Thou, Father, art in Me, and I in Thee, that they also may be one in Us Qohn 17:21).

Just how does He accomplish this? All those who believe in the Son of God are combined in spirit, which, being revived, is reunited with God. This is called the new birth from God, which malces believers the children of God through grace, as St. John the Evangelist says: hs many as received Him, to them gave Hepower to become the sons ofGod, even to them that believe on His name: which were born, not of blood... but of God (John 1:12,13). And the law of spiritual life ofJesus Christ came into being. Except a man be born ofwater and ofthe Spirit, he cannot enter into the Kingdom of God; for only that which is born ofthe Spirit is spirit (John 3:5,6).

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and together with God the Father and God the Son is established within us. Just how is this dwelling prepared?

The Divine Spirit acts secretly upon our spirit and sets it in motion. Our spirit, set in motion, recollects within itself its natural Divine knowledge that God exists, maintains everything, and is the Rewarder. The consciousness of this gives rise to a feeling of complete dependence on God, and enkindles the fear of God. These and other things are stirred up by the conscience, that witness and judge of our affairs and feelings, among which is rarely to be found anything which God would look upon with kindness. The conscience, alarmed by both the fear of God and the feeling of complete dependence upon God, furnishes the person with a feeling of the hopelessness of his situation: “Where shall I go? Without the Gospel, the awakening of our spirit would be ruinous, or would unavoidably plunge us into despair. Come beneath the protection of the Cross and be saved. The Son of God, ^X^ho was incarnate, died on the Cross to cleanse our sins. However, not everything is accomplished by the Divine Spirit alone. We are expected to do something, and this “something is of no small importance. The Divine Spirit arouses, the good news indicates where to begin. This is from God.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE further actions of grace, which bring him then into the realm of the saved. Take note of this next point, if you will. It must always be present in each spirit, when one is on the side of those being saved. It consists of the following actions: Once grace has aroused the feeling of the urgency of the situation, and the good news has indicated the way out of it, to the feeling of hopelessness must be added the consciousness that the person himself is guilty of everything and repents of it; he must believe in the efficacy of the form of salvation offered to him, desire to be saved by this very form of salvation, and acquire good cheer in it. He must express complete willingness to do with complete sincerity everything that will be indicated to him, as a condition of salvation. When all of this has taken place in our spirit, there remains only to take part in the mysteries, and Divine communion is accomplished.

To summarize: Repent, desire salvation in the Lord, and acquire good cheer in it. These actions are calming; they occur inwardly and give contentment through their local manifestation. The final action—the willingness to do everything which

TRUE SPIRITUAL ZEAL is required—is the real active force in salvation, ^ insofar as it depends upon us; it is the source of saving activity and the life that is saved. This willingness, while it is expressed by us alone, is weak; but when Divine grace establishes itself within us, then it becomes all- powerful, knows no impediment, and overcomes everything. It is the unquenchable zeal for pleasing God and complete sincerity in fulfillment of the Divine will, in the presence of complete faith in the Lord and trust in salvation in Him Alone. It fulfills the Divine eternal purpose; should be holy and without blame before Him in love (Ephesians 1:4), for which the Lord makes us zealous ofgood works (Titus 2:1 4).

I have intentionally directed your attention to this zeal, so that you become zealous. For once it is a part of us, then Divine grace for its part will not omit doing everything with abundance. Thus, when there exists such zeal in the realm of faith and the search for salvation, then everything exists, and the accomplishing of our salvation is undoubtedly in motion and heading towards its goal. When there is no zeal, then there is nothing at all; he who is not zealous for salvation is a non-participant in salvation.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE discussing, that active, live, fervent, untiring zeal that is the sign our spirit has been rehabilitated and restored to its former power through reunion with God by means of the grace and action of the Holy Spirit. We do have our outbursts of zeal, but they are only outbursts, and then they are extinguished. The zeal which is always fervent, constant and untiring zeal, exists only when our spirit is filled with grace by the Holy Spirit. Thus, when you have such zeal, it means also that your spirit has been restored, and—do not quench it—it takes in hand both the mind and body, all the requirements of their nature, all their domestic and civil cares, and it directs them toward one thing: pleasing God and salvation. Do you have such zeal, or not.

I suppose it is very difficult for you to answer this, and not just on account of modesty. I shall come to your aid. Do not confuse this zeal with other kinds of zeal. Spiritual zeal is completely given over to pleasing God and the salvation of the soul. It is full of the fear of God, and it continually keeps its attention on Him, taking care in every way not to allow anything in the thoughts, feelings, words, or deeds that is not pleasing to God, as directed by the conscience that it keeps as pure as a mirror. It guards its heart from any attachment to anything, except for God and Divine things, and by its hopes it moves into another world, having cut off all earthly cares. It does not shun that which is needed for temporal life, but everything relating to the here and now is for it an attachment, and there is really only one important thing, pleasing God and salvation.
Do you see this? Consider this well, if you will, and measure yourself against it.

Also keep in mind that there is intellectual zeal. This is very powerful, but it is completely given over to the affairs of temporal life. There are those who are so zealous for science that they do not sleep; those who run around the entire world for art; those who do not spare any energy in their zeal for industry ii6

TRUE SPIRITUAL ZEAL and trade; others who are zealous in domestic or civic affairs. All this would be neither here nor there; but the trouble is, when one of these kinds of zeal captures the attention and energy of a person to a great extent, then it extinguishes spiritual zeal.

The zeal of the soul in its best manifestations, such as given above, is cold toward the spiritual and repels any wish to occupy itself with that which satisfies spiritual zeal. There is nothing to be said about other kinds ofzeal, as they are contrary to spiritual. For there is also the zeal of vanity (for one’s dress and the like) and the zeal of evil—impure and sinful, directed toward the satisfaction of the passions. These latter sorts do not just cool spiritual zeal, but are completely adverse to it, and they both suppress it within themselves, if it should somehow begin to stir within the conscience, and also pursue and persecute it inside others. There are many different kinds of zeal! Look within yourself, don’t you have one of these kinds of zeal, too?

Spiritual zeal does not drive out intellectual zeal for science, art, domestic or civil concerns. It merely moderates and regulates it, directing it toward spiritual views, not allowing it to become zealous about anything which is counter to itself. It banishes only the vain and the passionate, and does not allow them to appear in the sphere of life of the man, of whom it has taken possession. In only one instance does it not allow even intellectual zeal, namely, when it sees that the actions and pursuits for the satisfaction of such a zeal—science, art, craftsmanship, family life, civil service and occupation—do not give full range to it (spiritual zeal), but crowd it out, cool it and extinguish it. Then it completely removes the person, whom it possesses, from all those parts of life which are ruled by intellectual zeal, and carries him off to a place where he may occupy himself with only that which satisfies it—spiritual zeal. Have you guessed what I am talking about here?

RENEWAL AND SELF-CLEANSING New Year s Greetings. The necessity ofrenewal and self-cleansing. This produces in us spiritual zeal.

I wished you a Happy New Year in passing, and then it occurred to me to give you a special wish for it. W^hat, specifically.^ That you become new. New in what way.^ In the way that a new dress is new: Everything is in its place, there is not the slightest little spot or wrinkle; it billows with freshness. That is what I wish for your soul to be like. You, in body, like the dress that we are talking about, fresh from the needle, will be completely new. Our soul, however, even upon its arrival in the world, brings the old; if this oldness is not removed from it, then it will remain old, without having tasted the new. Vanity and passions—that is what is old in the soul. Drive these out, and you will become new.
I will tell you a legend, and it will become clear to you what this is all about. A certain man lived somewhere far away in the desert. His internal organs became diseased, either his lungs, his

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE heart, or his liver, or maybe all of them at once. The pain made him feel as if he were going to die. He could not expect human help from anywhere, and so he turned to God with the most intense prayer. The Lord heard him. One night after falling asleep he saw a vision. Two angels came with knives, cut him open, took out the ailing parts, cleaned them, washed them and anointed them with something. Then they put everything back in its place, sprinkled something over him, and everything healed, as if he had not even been cut open. When he awakened, the elder got up entirely healthy, as if he had never been ill; he was new like a youth in the flower of his strength. Do you see what I wish for you? That everything be purified in your soul, that everything that is diseased and old be cast away, that everything conducive to health and renewal be put in its place and sprinkled with living water. And that it become new, fresh, full of bright and joyous life.

I told this story to someone who labors over himself When he heard it, he responded with enthusiasm, “Oh, if only the angels would come and do for my soul what they did for the body of the elder!” In this response you can hear both a prayer and also a desire to shift that which is supposed to be done through one s own labors to someone else. For such a crafty thought is not alien to our heart; we know that we are to be nothing but good, but to have to labor to achieve this—^we despair. The angels are not going to come to purify and heal our heart. There exist such things as instructions and advice from angels, but everything must be done by oneself. All means are provided; that is, tools and surgeon s instruments. Take them and cut yourself wherever necessary without feeling any pity for yourself No one else can do this for you. God Himself does not enter the sanctuary of the soul forcefully; instead. He requests to enter gently.
I shall not ask whether you are arranging for your own purification, or whether you will turn this over to others. You 120

I would like to share some thoughts concerning you which came to mind on the Feast of the Lord’s Baptism.^ I was about 1 . Theophany.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE to pose a question just for you: What do you think of your baptism? Was it merely a ceremony, or did it confer something on you? Then I thought it would be much better if I answered this myself. Here is my reply.

Baptism gives us what nothing else on earth can give us: It utiites utid cotnbines with out tiuturul Divitic gruc6» Thus, a person comes away from the baptismal font like an article from some workshop. As an example, take a bell in which silver has been added to copper. A similar copper bell without silver may outwardly resemble the bell with silver, but their composition is different, their sound is different, and they are regarded differently with respect to quality and value. That is the difference between a person who has been baptized, and one who has not. The difference is that the grace of the Holy Spirit combines with the baptism, since the person receiving baptism is being baptized with water and, along with it, the Holy Spirit. By appearance, he is the same person as the unbaptized person, but in actual fact, in their composition, they are quite different.

Thus, during Holy Baptism, some new element, a supernatural one, is added to our composition, and it will remain inside us hidden and secretly acting. We receive baptism as children, and although we do not know what is happening within us, the grace nonetheless combines with us and then begins to act within us apart from our consciousness, according to the singular goodness of God, and for the sake of the faith of our godparents and parents. While we are growing up, we are not able to distinguish within ourselves what is from our nature and what is from the grace which has been present in us from our very baptism, not in idleness, but acting in its own particular manner. In the early days, however, when those who were brought to the faith and baptized were adults, the action of grace noted within them was immediately percepti122

THE HIDDEN ACTION OF GRACE ble to them and apparent to others. Especially striking were the changes in moral character and life, when, for example, an angry person would become mild, a proud person humble, a stingy one generous, a profligate one celibate, and the like.

Something similar undoubtedly happened with us at the baptismal font. However, having reached maturity, and seeing the good things within ourselves, we often get conceited about them, or we ascribe them to ourselves. Instead, all that is good within us should be attributed to the grace of God, which remakes everything that is natural within us, and gives much directly from itself.

Grace acts inside us secretly from childhood. It is renewed within us by the communion of the Holy Mysteries of the Body and Blood of Christ. The environment in which we grow up, if it is believing and pious, gives full range to the action of grace inside us through Christian upbringing. We grow out of childhood and adolescence under conditions entirely unlike those under which the non-baptized leave them. I suppose that it is pleasant for you to grasp the idea and to believe that you are much favored. Do not deny this, but give thanks once again.

Grace acts secretly, but often it bursts out in the open, in holy outbursts and deeds. If you would chafe yourself a little more often as you go along in your Christian life, you would experience the action of grace more often. There is fire inside wood, but it is hidden. Rub a piece ofwood against another piece, and you get smoke; then fire appears. There is electricity in amber and sealing wax, but it is not visible. Rub a piece, and immediately you will notice the attraction of small particles and even some sparking. The telegraph is also silent, but as soon as the device begins to produce friction, electricity immediately awakens, and acts swiftly.
So you see, favored daughter of God, what it is all about!
Chafe yourself as necessary, as you go about the Christian life. 123

THE ACTIVITY OF GRACE Continuation concerning the hidden action ofthe grace of Baptism in man. An explanation ofthe action ofgrace by comparison to the action ofleaven in dough andfre in iron.

I suppose that you remember and still keep in mind that a Christian is not an ordinary person, being formed instead from nature and grace. I would like to clarify something for you at this point: Those who are saved, that is, those who will enter the eternal Kingdom of God, are only those in whom grace dwells; not secretly, but openly, permeating our entire essence and becoming even outwardly visible, absorbing, as it were, our entire nature.
Heed the word of the Savior! He says that the Kingdom of God is like when a woman takes leaven and adds it to dough.

THE ACTIVITY OF GRACE The dough, once it has received the leaven, does not rise all at once; it will do so in its own time. The leaven within it permeates the dough little by little, and the entire dough becomes leavened.

Bread baked from this is light, aromatic, delicious. It is exactly the same thing with grace which has been added to our nature: it does not permeate everything all at once; instead, it does so little by little. Then, once it has permeated everything, one’s entire nature is filled with grace. Everything which the person then does takes on a special character. Although by appearance the actions are the very same as other similar actions, they have a special aroma, a special taste, and a special sound. God accepts only these actions as being especially pleasing to Him.

I will make another comparison in my explanation of how grace, when it is given room to act, permeates our entire nature, and then becomes outwardly visible for all who are able to see. It is like fire when it permeates iron: it is not only inside the iron but on the outside as well, and its fiery force is observable to everyone. So it is with grace, when it has permeated our nature: it becomes visibly perceptible to all. All those entering into contact with such a grace- filled person feel an unusual force present within him, which manifests itself in different ways. ^XTen such a person begins to speak about something spiritual, everything emanates from him as brightly as the midday sun, and his words go directly to the soul, authoritatively forming corresponding feelings and dispositions within. Even if he does not speak, he exudes a warmth which touches everything, and a certain force goes out, which stimulates moral energy and engenders readiness for every kind of spiritual action and exploit. ^ 1 • The Russian word is podvtg, which in the spiritual context is a deed or act undertaken as a spiritual obedience. THE SPIRITUAL LIFE We receive the grace of God through Holy Baptism in our childhood. From that moment, it begins to act within us and carry out its action, in the hope that when we reach adulthood, we ourselves, through the decision of our own will, will undertake zealously to do everything for our salvation that is in our power to do. W^hen a family is devout and raises its children in the Christian way of life, grace itself creates peace within them, and they grow up to be gentle, humble, obedient, kind, God-pleasing and filled with the fear of God. Examples of this are to be seen everywhere.

I would point to you as one of them, if I were not afraid that you would get a high opinion ofyourself, although thus far there is not a lot to think of about yourself, because nothing is as of yet the result of anything you have done. Everything visible in you is good, but it is from someone else; it has been given to you and cultivated within you. You are now faced with the following task: You must love all of this good and put it in your heart and keep it, then you must increase it and multiply it. It is true that you have a good foundation and have been on the right path, but up to this point none of this has been as a result of your own will and free decision. And if you do not begin making your own efforts in this matter now, none of what you learned from your upbringing will be firm, and when you encounter unpleasant circumstances it will desert you, leaving behind only a pleasant—or even unpleasant—memory.

Do you recall what you experienced when you were plunged into the whirlpool ofworldly life? YC^ho tormented you then and distressed your heart? Everything good in you has been kindled by the grace of God, from a pious family upbringing. Why then did your experiences bother you? Because you allowed yourself to conceive a special sympathy for these chaotic ways. Although you did not tell me about this, it is self-evident from the sorrow which tormented you later. Without this sympathy, there would 126

THE ACTIVITY OF GRACE not have been torment. I wrote you that if necessity were to force you to be plunged into the same whirlpool again, not to attach your heart to anything that you see or hear there. If you do not become attached, you will not experience any torments.

Well, I do not know whether you are there, and, if you are, whether you are doing as I have told you. See for yourself. For you are your own mistress; you are entering the age of independent action. I say to you confidentially that either you will turn out to be just as empty, vain and passionate a person as everyone else, or, you could turn out to be neither one thing nor the other.

The first kind of person is the result when you wholeheartedly plunge into that sort of life-style. Then you forget all past good, holiness, spiritual things, and come to have an adversarial relationship toward them. Knowing you, I am sure that this will not happen to you. However, I cannot rule out the possibility of the second case, that you could turn out to be neither one thing nor the other—neither spiritual nor worldly, neither a Christian nor a pagan. This could happen even though you keep the pious ways in which you grew up, if you do not also guard your heart from sympathy for worldly ways. Remember, I have in mind “sympathy” and not “participation,” which is sometimes necessary, as you say. This sympathy would not divorce you completely from your previous upbringing, but would cool you to it, and you would be dragged into pious ways by habit alone, and not by choice of heart. Essentially, you would be neither in the worldly realm, nor in the true spiritual one—you would be neither one thing nor the other.

What would be in store for you as the result? The same thing to which the Lord in the Revelation has already condemned one of the angels of the churches: / know thy worksy that thou art neither cold nor hot'. I would thou wert cold or hot. So then because thou art lukewarruy and neither cold nor hoty I will spue thee out ofMy mouth (Rev. 3:15-16). One must be fervent toward God 127

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and all that is Divine, but cold toward everything secular and worldly. If you are neither cold toward the secular, nor fervent toward the Divine, but are instead lukewarm and half-cold toward both one and the other, then you will be cast out by God. How should one be? You should choose the holy, Godpleasing spiritual life with your heart, right now, as you are entering the period of independent life. Everything I have been saying leads to this. Will you choose? Get rid ofyour troubles — do not be indifferent, being neither one thing nor the other.
May the Lord bless you and bring you to reason!
INNER CONCENTRATION Cotitinudtion about zeal and vesolve to live according to grace.
Inner concentration. The illumination ofthe covering ofthe soul. The stages ofthis illumination.

I am very pleased by your desire to be reborn. You have been torn by the desire; may the Lord bless you, bring your desire to decisiveness, and then put it into action. This process, at least superficially, is not so complex or lengthy, but in reality it is a life-long labor. How one reaches the point of decisiveness will be discussed later. Right now I will speak a little on the purpose. A conscious, clear-cut purpose at the beginning of an undertaking keeps the energy of our forces at the proper level to see it through to its completion.

Last time, I wrote to you that it is necessary for us of our own will to give scope to the Divine grace received by us at Baptism, so that it, like leaven, will permeate our entire essence in all its parts. Then I added that it begins to act immediately from the time of Baptism, and if it does not meet with any 128

INNER CONCENTRATION interference, it produces everything possible within the soul by itself, in the hope that, once a person has reached maturity, his soul through its own free choice will choose those elements of a Christian, God-pleasing life that have already been instilled by grace. When someone who has reached maturity chooses grace through his own consciousness and free choice, then grace itself will instruct him how to come to terms with himself and re-do everything within himself, and it will not leave him until this work is carried to its conclusion, as long as the person does not begin to oppose it by his self-will. You are now standing at that moment of life when the choice of whether to choose the side of grace, or the side opposing it, will be put to your self-will and freedom of choice. Let us suppose that you have selected the first option, and that you have brought your life, through labor, to the blessed conclusion of a grace-filled life. What do you think your internal being would look like then? Like a radiant star, beaming bright rays everywhere. That is what!

Remember, you said you could not control your thoughts, and then you wrote how I was ruining you with what I was saying, that everything had been better for you previously, but once you began to scrutinize yourself under my direction, you saw only disorder; your thoughts, feelings and desires were all in disarray, and you had no strength to put them in any kind of order. Here is the solution to the problem of why it is that way: You have no center. And you have no center because you have still not decided through your own consciousness and free choice which way you will go. Up to this point, the grace of God has instilled in you a feasible order, and this order has been and still is within you. From this point, however, the grace of God will not act on its own, but will instead await your decision.
If you, through your own choice and decision, do not choose grace, then it will abandon you completely, and leave you in the hands of your self-will. You will be shunted off to the opposite 129

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE side, and maybe your heart will even choose it; but do not expect that your inner disorder will abate by taking this direction. No, an even greater confusion and disorder will be created. Inner regulation begins only when you choose the side of grace, and make the ways of life in the spirit of grace the inviolable rule of your life. From that moment, as the decision is forming inside you, a center will also form within you, a powerful center, which will begin more and more forcefully to draw you toward itself.

In this center will be grace, which has taken hold of your consciousness and free will (or, your consciousness and free will, combined with grace). This is the same thing which was previously called the resurrection or restoration of the spirit. Then the grace of God will begin to draw toward this center all of the other forces of your nature, both intellectual and spiritual, and govern their entire action, retaining within them that which is good, and destroying that which is bad. This drawing of everything to one center and directing of all to one goal is the inner rebirth which you have so fervently desired. Once this rebirth has been accomplished, then everything both great and small will proceed from this one center, and within you will be established the most perfect harmony, and the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will overshadow the inner temple ofyour nature. May the God of peace be with you! What a delightful and most blessed condition. You were torn by desire for something which is entirely worthy of your desire.

Stop and think a minute about what we said concerning the subtle element that is present everywhere, permeating everything, and which is of the same substance as our souls. ^0(/hen our soul is outside of grace, its covering is either as gloomy as the darkest night, as when someone indulges his passions and serves them; or it is grey like an obscure fog, as when someone who is not too given over to the passions sdll lives in vanity. Under the action of grace, however, not only is the soul permeated by 130

INNER CONCENTRATION grace, but its covering gradually grows brighter, just as gloomy weather gradually clears up. W^hen the soul is entirely permeated by grace, then its covering also becomes entirely bright. Just as everything within is drawn toward one thing in this case, so does the brightness of the covering arise from that very same center, following spiritual actions. This is radiance. A person who has received grace shines inwardly as a star, not just with a spiritual light, but also with a material one.

This inner brightness in such people often bursts out and becomes visible to others. When I was in St. Petersburg in the forties, I heard about this from certain people, and I wanted very much to see it with my own eyes. I happened to receive a monk in whom perceptible actions of grace had already begun to manifest themselves. We began speaking about spiritual things.

The more he entered within himself and the more his thought deepened, the brighter his face became, until it became as white as snow, with his eyes gleaming. It is said that Father Seraphim of Sarov quite often displayed this light, especially when at prayer in full view of everyone. There are many stories of similar manifestations in the accounts of the Fathers. For example, it is written how one of them, when standing at prayer, would raise his hands toward the sky, and bands of light would begin flowing from his fingers, and these bands of light would scatter over a wide area. Concerning another one, it is said that his disciple came to him for something and knocked at his door.

There was no answer, so he jumped up to peek in the aperture, and saw that the starets was standing completely engulfed in fire, like a pillar of light. There are many such accounts. I was still a little boy when I heard something similar about St. Tikhon. The Transfiguration of the Lord, when Fie appeared entirely illumined with light, is one of those instances.
Such radiance, during the transition to the other life, reveals itself naturally, for then the coarse body wanes, and does not

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE prevent the radiance from being seen by everyone. I have already reminded you of how St. Anthony the Great, while sitting and talking with his disciples, turned his eyes to heaven, and after he had gazed for a little while, said “I saw a pillar of light rising from earth to heaven. This was the soul of Ammon soaring to the Lord.” There are many such sightings which have been recorded. It goes without question that only those in whom there dwells the grace of God will enter Heaven, grace that has been received and which has begun its action to some degree, even if it has not had time to permeate one’s entire being. Recall the parable of the ten virgins.^ The five foolish ones did not enter the chamber because they had no fire in their lamps. This means that, after receiving grace, as everyone does, they did not take the trouble to kindle it within themselves, and they did not labor over themselves in order to give grace the scope to act fully within them. They extinguished grace, and so no light appeared: for there is no way for this to occur within us other than from grace.

Imagine how grief-stricken they were, when they stood at the door itself and were turned away. They could even hear the voice of the bridegroom; but He was not calling to them. He was driving them away. Lord have mercy! What an exceedingly bitter lot! Turn your thought to this parable a little more often, and attend with all care, so that no matter what happens, you will achieve your goal, and nothing will happen to you like what happened in the parable.
EXAMPLES OF THE FREE RESOLVE TO LIVE ACCORDING TO GRACE ExpLznution ofthefree resolve to live uccording to grcice by means ofparables and examples.
I am rushing off a few more lines to send you in addition to what has already been said on the same subject.

At the Divine Judgement, those who have received grace and who have not allowed it to act within themselves will first of all have the gift of grace taken away, and then they will be plunged into hell. This was revealed by the Savior in the parable of the talent (Luke 19i 11-27). Each of the servants has been given a talent; grace is given to each person equally. One of the servants acquires ten more talents; a second servant acquires five more. A third servant acquires nothing; he says that he wrapped his talent in a napkin and set it aside. This means that the first servant worked more than the others in order to be imbued with grace; the second worked half as hard by comparison, but the third servant neglected the gift, not bothering in the least to kindle grace within himself. The reward is then given out in accordance with the labors expended on acquiring grace, or with the inner enlightenment of one’s self under the action of grace.
The last servant did nothing in this regard; from him was taken even that which he had, which was given to him so generously at the beginning.

Do you see how this matter goes, and how it ends? We, the baptized, have all received a talent, which is the grace of the Holy Spirit. This talent, as I have already mentioned, acts within us on its own at first, until we have grown up. As we 133

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE approach maturity, grace, although it is ready at any time to act within us, does not act. It waits until we freely and willingly incline to it, when we ourselves begin to desire its full action within us and begin to seek it. As soon as we begin seeking, grace immediately resumes its work within us, rousing us, directing and strengthening us. Our imbuing with grace comes about in proportion to our seeking and to the labor expended in this search. If we do not seek and do not labor toward this particular goal in this frame of mind, grace will not begin to act within us on its own against our will, as if by force. God gave man freedom, and He does not want to violate this; He does not want to enter into a person against his will and act within him. When a person of his own accord wants to be subject to Divine action, only then does God begin to act within him through grace. If God were to have His own way, everyone would become holy in a single instant. One Divine instant, and everyone would change. But the law is such already that man must himself begin to desire and to seek, and then grace will not desert him, as long as he continues to trust in it.

Recall the Savior’s parable about the treasure hidden in the field and the merchant who is seeking goodly pearls (Matthew 1 3 : 44- 46) . A man found a treasure hidden in a field, and he went and sold all that he had and bought that field. Of course, he then dug up the treasure and became rich, although this is not mentioned in the parable. This field is our soul; the treasure, which is hidden inside of it, is grace, which is placed in it through Holy Baptism. The man s finding of the treasure in the parable signifies the moment when the Christian recognizes that within him is concealed such a treasure, to which nothing can compare—the grace of the Holy Spirit. He sold everything; this means he gave up everything he had that was dear to him to obtain the treasure, that is, to arouse and bring to the open the grace that is concealed within. 134

LIVE ACCORDING TO GRACE The other merchant dealt in precious stones. He heard there was a pearl to which there was nothing else equal, but the price for which no one in the local area seemed to know (I am adding this). Wishing to acquire the pearl, he sold everything he had and bought it. And of course, he became rich. This pearl ofgreat price is the image of the Divine grace which is concealed within us, and which is unknown to us, until we acknowledge it. He who acknowledges it becomes convinced at the same time that there is nothing more valuable. Thus, with complete selflessness he gives up everything and strives to kindle and ignite that grace within himself From these parables you may see what exactly is expected of us. It is expected that we 1) acknowledge the presence of the gift of grace within us; 2) comprehend that the value ofthe grace for us is so great, that it is more precious than life itself, so that without it life is not even life; 3) desire with all our strength to adapt this grace to ourselves^ and adapt ourselves to it, or, to put it another way, desire to imbue our entire nature with it, and to become enlightened and sanctified; 4) resolve to achieve this through the matter itself, and then 5) carry this decision into reality, putting everything else aside, or, having removed one's heart from everything, give it over to the full action of Divine grace. When these five acts have taken place within us, then comes the beginning of our internal rebirth, after which, if we continue to act in the same spirit unflaggingly, inner rebirth and illumination will grow quickly or slowly, depending on our labor, but most importantly, depending on our forgetfulness of self.

Remember how I was telling you about that governess who made a drastic change.^ All this happened for her. Once she had begun, she did not look back, but instead went further and 1 . Who had already reposed as Mother Magdalene at the Eletsk convent.
Her story appeared in the 1876 Strannik {Pilgrim). 135

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE fiirther, higher and higher. And she attained the fiery enkindling of grace. Divine grace makes no distinction as to who a person was before desiring it, but awaits only the desire. It begins its action immediately, as in the case of the Great Martyr St. Katherine who was pure, and in that of St. Mary of Egypt, who was formerly profligate. How many of these are there on this path today in Russia, known and unknown, both men and women, both widows and virgins? What blessed and most blessed souls they are! Blessed Augustine^ for a long time was kept in the bonds of an unspiritual life, although he knew about the spiritual life and desired it. What was it that helped him to break these bonds? A story he heard about how St. Anthony the Great, an unlearned man of ordinary stock, shone with grace.

Upon hearing the story, he cried out, “These simple ones are above us, leaving us behind with all of our learning and our importance among other people. And from this moment I mastered myself, and with ardor I set on that very same path along which St. Anthony walked.” How is it to be for you and me? Will we make excuses and keep putting things off day after day?! May the Lord bestow His enlightening blessing upon you.
