PATIENCE AND CONSTANCY Resolve to live according to grace must not be limited merely to desire but must be accompanied by a readiness for labors and warfare, by a yearning to unfailingly attain what one desires.
Having resolved this , one must immediately begin this task and continue with patience and constancy.
Not everything has been said yet. I shall undertake to write something more in addition to the above. The importance of the subject we are discussing compels this.
The radiance of the soul which Divine grace permeates completely, like fire permeates iron, is an attractive condition.

Having heard this, everyone is ready to rush off with the desire of obtaining it. I suppose you have such an impulse. But this impulse, although it signifies that the soul knows how to choose for the better, does not express everything which is required here. You might rush headlong and suddenly stop; then nothing will have come ofyour impulse. No, the impulse alone is not enough: you need to examine the matter thoroughly and make a firm and unyielding decision, being aware of all the labors, obstacles and difficulties which lie ahead, so that you may stand up to them with courageous inspiration until the end of your life.

The search for the means by which Divine grace permeates our entire essence is the same as the search for the Kingdom of God, or the enkindling of zeal for the salvation of the soul, or the falling in love with and choosing of the One Thing above all. There are various names and expressions, but the goal is one.
I chose the clearer aspect. No matter what you call it, the object 137

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE in and of itself is supremely desirable; therefore it is impossible not to desire it. Ask anyone, Do you want to go to Paradise, to the Heavenly Kingdom?” He will answer you immediately,“Yes, of course I do.” But then tell him, YC^ell, you must do this and that,” and his heart will sink. Everyone wants to go to Heaven, but not everyone has the desire to labor toward this end. I am saying this because it is not enough merely to desire. You need to resolve firmly to achieve without fail that which you desire.
You also need to resolve firmly to begin the process itself of labor toward this. In order to make this clearer for you, I will tell you how desires usually reach the point of decision.
We think about many subjects, and we plan many actions.

But we think about something for awhile, and then we forget it. This means that our heart was not in it, and so we did not remember it. If something is memorable, our heart is in it. If the heart is in something, it means that we like the subject. If we like something, then it is pleasant for us to think about, to keep in our thoughts, or to imagine. However, this may not stir any further desire to possess the thing, or if it is an action, to do it. We say that something is a good thing or a good deed, but it is up to us to fulfill it. We admire good things or good deeds, but we do not necessarily experience any inclination to reach out for them or toward them. In order to begin to desire something, and in order for the desired object have personal meaning for us, it is necessary that it be either beneficial to us, or essential, or something which we like so much that without it life would be boring or unhappy. Y(/^hen we see these aspects in some deed or thing, we cannot hold back our desire for it, and desire then forms inevitably. Let us suppose that we have desired something; is this all that is required? No. There are many desires which remain unfulfilled from lack of strength or concentration of energy in carrying them out. In order for a desire to be fulfilled, it is necessary to elevate it to the level of 138

PATIENCE AND CONSTANCY firm intention or decision, and it is necessary for the heart to say within itself, No matter what happens, I will obtain such-and-such a thing, or I will accomplish such-and-such a deed.” Once this has been pronounced within the heart, there immediately begins a thinking out of how to realize what has been decided. The means are considered; favorable conditions of time and place are devised; obstacles are foreseen, as well as measures for overcoming them; and there is a feasible overview of the entire course of the matter, from beginning to end. When all this has been mapped out in the heart, then it is completely ready to act.

Everything is ready for action, but the act itself still has not been realized; it must be begun, continued with constancy, patience, and corresponding diligence until it reaches its conclusion. Up to now, we have been describing the process as if we were preparing a lumber-cutting machine for its task: The steam is ready, the wood has been placed on it, and everything is set out as it should be. All that is left is to set it in motion, and work will be underway. This setting in motion is the crown of all previous preparation, as if nothing were to be added to the doing, except the accomplishing of the deed. Everything depends on this setting in motion. There still remains the most crucial act, in which all energy is concentrated, at the precise point of transformation of the desires into decisiveness and deed, when the decision has ripened and everything has been thought out for its accomplishment: beginning to do it. Do you think that this is so difficult, when everything has already been prepared.^ This, by the way, is the most difficult step. Everything up to the decision has occurred internally; now our internal action—the mental—must enter into the current of events and flow in line with the other ones. Just get the first step accomplished, and the condition itself, which already exists and into which the deed undertaken will enter, will begin to urge the 139
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE deed on to action in the spirit and order in which it was begun.

I am telling you all of this to point out what you still need to complete within yourself, so that the desire you had when you rushed headlong into spiritual matters will be transformed into action. So that your desire does not fail to bloom, it is necessary to carry it from the beginning to the point of decision.

This should not be a hasty decision, but a careful, deliberate, firm, rational, and most importantly, irrevocable decision. Then get down to work itself. First, everything must be accomplished inwardly through personal reflection, along with prayer to God to bring reason and enlightenment to the mind with respect to the extremely crucial matter at hand. The second thing will not be hard for you, judging by such customs as exist at your house.
May the Lord bless you, that the things set out above be accomplished within you!

THE INNER STATE ACCORDING TO MACARIUS THE GREAT An outline ofthe inner state enlightened by grace according to the words ofMacarius the Great. The means to arouse and strengthen one*s determination to lead a virtuous life.

I am impatient. I take up my pen, and am still just beginning to explain the very same thing to you, that is, the attractiveness of the condition of being filled with grace, in order to set you on the path to attaining it, learning it and being established in it. But this time, I will not offer you my own words, but those of that wise man of God, Macarius the Great, namely from his eighteenth homily. 140

THE INNER STATE “If anyone in the world is wealthy, and he has a hidden treasure, then he will acquire all that he wants through this treasure and wealth. So it is with those who have found and who already have the heavenly treasure (grace), for through this treasure they acquire every virtue, and accumulate even greater heavenly wealth for themselves. The Apostle says, have this treasure in earthen vessels (2 Cor. 4:7); that is, being still in the flesh, we were worthy to find in ourselves the above mentioned treasure, the sanctifying power of the Spirit.

He who has found and who has within himself this heavenly treasure of the Spirit is irreproachable, and accomplishes in purity every truth in accordance with the commandments and every virtuous deed without being compelled to do so, and without any difficulties. If we were to begin to implore God, we would begin seeking and asking that we too be given the treasure of His Spirit, and thus we would become irreproachable and abide in purity in all His commandments, carrying out every truth thoroughly and perfectly.

“It is necessary to force oneself to ask the Lord that He allow one to find and accept the treasure of the Spirit, to arrive at this condition without difficulty, and to carry out faultlessly and thoroughly all the commandments of the Lord with ease, which formerly could not be accomplished even with all one s strength.

This treasure is acquired through diligent seeking, having faith and patience in one’s labors throughout this seeking. It is necessary to ask God with sincere love and faith to allow us to find in our hearts His riches, in the power and efficacy of the spirit. ^J7hat it is like for those in whom Divine grace has begun noticeably to manifest its action is depicted by St. Macarius in the following way: Sometimes they are mirthful, as if they were at the king’s table, and rejoice with joy and inexpressible happiness. At other

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE times they are like a bride, soothed by divine tranquility in the company of her bridegroom. And sometimes, even though they have bodies, they feel the very same lightness and wingedness as do the incorporeal angels. Sometimes it is as if they are soothed with drink, being made merry and tranquil with the Spirit, in ecstasy from the divine spiritual mysteries. Sometimes the Spirit inflames them with such love that, if it were possible, they would make room in their hearts for every man, not distinguishing the bad from the good. Sometimes, in the humble wisdom of the Spirit, they so abase themselves in front of each person, that they consider themselves to be the last and the least of all men.

Sometimes their souls are tranquil in some great silence, in quiet and peace; sometimes they are instructed by grace in the understanding of something, in ineffable wisdom, in the knowledge of that which is impossible to express with speech. Sometimes they behave like ordinary people.” What a condition to long for! Here is yet another small sketch of the enlightenment by grace of the inner condition: ^^W^hen the soul aspires to the perfection of the Spirit, after it has been entirely cleansed of all the passions, and, after it has arrived at unity and dissolution with the Comforting Spirit in ineffable communion and being commingled with the Spirit, then it will be considered worthy of becoming spirit itself Then it will become completely light, all-seeing, completely joyful, completely calm, completely loving, completely merciful, completely filled with grace and goodness.” That is what the holy ascetics attained and were trying to attain! You will agree that it was and is worth struggling for.

Access to this realm is open to everyone. It is not some forbidden meadow or garden. Everyone has been promised these blessings, and the grace of the Holy Spirit in Baptism and Chrismation serves as the advance deposit toward their acquisition. We have only to rummage in ourselves, and to obtain this treasure. The 142

THE INNER STATE buried treasure is in our own garden, we have only to take up our shovel and start digging. Silver and gold will begin to sparkle with the very first thrusts of the spade, and then the entire treasure will come to view. There will be no measure to joy then.
Well then, why is my business with you at such an impasse?!

In the last letter I indicated to you the path that leads to persistence, but I did not mention which ideas in particular give rise to this decisiveness, and how it arrives at definitive effort. I will now briefly outline what is most important.

Even interest in a subject can stimulate energy, but in this case it is possible to put it aside until tomorrow. However, when one fully comprehends the extreme necessity and unavoidability of something on the one hand, and has the means for dealing with it on the other hand, then a decision is inevitably reached.

Here is an example for you: Some sedentary, lazy person is sitting in his room; you would not call out to him that there is a fire; but just let him see fire, and he will dash out of there. He is driven by spirit. That is exactly what we must do with ourselves, and, in the case of indecisiveness, it is necessary to enkindle misfortune around ourselves, that is, to convince ourselves that we must do such-and-such a thing, or we will perish eternally. As soon as this is consciously grasped, all of our moral energy arises in full force, and draws us inevitably to action. Take a little trouble on your own to discover how to do this with regard to what we are speaking about. I would make mention for my part that death could come any time. What would happen upon death? Perhaps what happened to the cunning servant; his talent was taken away from him—the gift of grace, and he was thrown into eternal darkness! Or perhaps what happened to the foolish virgins, to whom the door was locked and who heard, “I don’t know you!” Indeed, one or the other misfortune will be our lot, forever, if we do not enkindle grace within ourselves and if we are not enlightened by it. Place H3

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE yourself in a. situation such as those that have been explained, and I do not think that your indecisiveness, if you are indecisive, will be able to withstand it. This concept is most powerful.

Another ancient sage uttered. Remember the end ofyour life, and then you will never sin (Sirach 7*39). ^^ork at visualizing these explanations, and, once you are fully aware of them, do not weaken and do not lose sight of this consciousness. Read the book Vostani Spyay (Arise and Conquer) as an aid. You have already been given this book.

The availability of assistance is the second aspect. It provides courage and good hope for overcoming misfortune in the presence of the sense of doom, thus inspiring us to action. Were it not for this, the sense of impending doom would plunge us into despair. In the example given, were it not for the door ajar or the open window, there would be only one thing left for the man caught by the fire to do—tear out his hair. So it is with respect to our subject; in the total doom in which we are indeed placed (without grace it is impossible to escape being deprived of the Kingdom of Heaven)^ in the consciousness of such doom, all that would be left would be to fall into despair, were it not for the assistance which is available to us. But thanks be to the Lord, the means by which we may escape the inescapable doom beyond the grave is already prepared for us. Everything is prepared, we have it at hand, and it is even within us. All that is left is to get to work and act. Knowing all this, are we still going to continue to delay and put things off day after day?

With respect to you, I would add that you do not have to begin anything special. Live in that spirit in which you were brought up, and keep those pious customs which you note in your immediate family and relatives. The thrust of my entire discourse is this: That you wholeheartedly embrace this very way of life and that you of your own accord decide to live that way to the end. Your life up to this point really has not been yours. 144

INITIAL YEARNING You were guided in this way. It is very beneficial, but will not last, if you do not choose this particular life ofyour own accord, and set it for yourself as inviolable law. If you do not do this now, the evil spirit of worldly life will attract you, or you will end up as neither one thing nor the other, as I have already mentioned to you.

Think over all of this, for God’s sake, and do not delay in making your decisions. May the Lord bless you! 31 SUPPORTING THIS INITIAL YEARNING How to support the initialyearningfor the life ofvirtue. Spiritual reading and reflection. The recording ofgood thoughts. How to escape the wandering ofone's thoughts by reading andprayer.
Unceasing remembrance ofGod and death. Self-reproach.
Praise God! You write that you have a strong desire to draw closer to Him. Lord give the blessing! For my discourse has had no other purpose than that of inspiring you with that desire.

May you enkindle the fear of God within yourself. Know that when there is the fear of God it means that your spirit is alive and that Divine grace is acting within you. The first action of our spirit is the knowledge of God and the fear of God, and the primary gift of Divine grace is the fear of God. This fear of God is the beginning, the continuation and the completion of the path of salvation. He who has a live, active fear of God has within himself an inexhaustible strength which sets into motion all that is pleasing to God, and which also acts as a bold sentry, who guards one from the enemy’s attacks and from one’s own falling into dissipation. May the Lord help you to enkindle 145
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and then to maintain within yourself in full force the spirit of this fear of God!

That you devote a little time in the morning to the reading of spiritual books is a very good thing. But who there with you is reacting to this so unfavorably? If you were to persist in nothing else besides reading and prayer, then of course that would be out of order; but what is wrong with a little bit of reading and prayer?! As far as I know, all devout and God-fearing people do this. I believe your parents keep the very same rule.

This reading with prayer preceding strengthens the soul and gives it strength for the entire day. Doctors say you should not leave the house on an empty stomach. With respect to the soul, this is fulfilled by morning prayer and reading. The soul is fed by them, and sets out on the business of the day nourished.
Something came to mind that I would like to suggest to you.

Get a notebook,^ and in it write down the thoughts that come to you as you read the Gospel and other books in this manner: “The Lord says such and such in the Gospel; from this it is obvious that we must act in such and such a way; for me this is feasible in such and such instances; I will act thus; Lord help me!” This does not require much effort, but how much benefit comes from it! Act in this way. Your thought will come into focus and become inspired. The Spirit, moving in the Scriptures, will enter into your heart and heal it. This is balm for wounds!
What can be done about thoughts running about every which way during reading and prayer? No one is free from this.

There is no sin in this, but it is out of place. It is a sin when someone willfully develops within himself alien thoughts, but when they run off involuntarily, is there any fault? The fault comes about when somebody who has noticed straying thoughts continues to stray in them. It is necessary to do this: As soon as
1. Fr. Seraphim’s note: “The importance of keeping a journal.” 146
INITIAL YEARNING you notice your thought wandering off, immediately put it back in its place.

For diminishing the straying of thought during prayer, it is necessary to make an effort to pray with warm feeling. To do this, it is necessary to warm the soul before prayer with meditation and with bows. Learn to pray with your own prayer. For example, the essence of evening prayer is to thank God for the day and for everything that one has met in the course of it, both good and bad. For the wrong which has been done, one must repent and ask forgiveness, promising to make amends the following day; then one prays to God for protection during sleep. Say all of this to God from your mind and from your heart. The essence of morning prayer is to thank God for sleep and revitalizing, and to ask Him to help to do things throughout the day for His glory. Again, say this to Him with all your heart and mind. While you are at it, both in the morning and the evening, make known your vital needs to the Lord, both inner needs and outer ones, speaking to Him like a child: “See, Lord, my illness and weakness! Help me and heal me!” All this and similar things you may say to God in your own words, without using your prayerbook. Maybe this will be better. Try this, and if it works, you may put aside your prayerbook altogether; if it does not work, however, then you should pray with your prayerbook, or else you may be left entirely without prayer.

So that the words of the prayers in the prayerbook take on meaning and warm the heart, you should sit down in your free time, other than the time when you are at prayer, and think out thoroughly the content of the prayers, and become keenly aware of them. Then when you read the prayers during prayer-time, whether in the morning or evening, all those thoughts and feelings which came to you during meditation will be renewed, and they will collect your attention and warm your heart. Never read a prayer hurriedly. Another thing: Try to learn the prayers 147
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE by heart. This greatly aids undistracted prayer. A prayer must be learned just like anything else.

Learn to think of God not only when you are standing at prayer, but also at every hour and at every minute, for He is everywhere. From this peace will pour into your heart, giving strength for daily business and a regulating of affairs. Your present desire of drawing closer to God will be completely realized in this way. Just as someone standing in the sunshine is warm, so too is the person who always remembers God.

Add to remembrance of God the remembrance of death and of eternal bliss or damnation. These two remembrances will divert us from everything evil, even in thought, and direct us to everything good, not just for show, but in truth. Some think mistakenly that remembrance of death poisons life. It does not poison life, but instead teaches us to be careful and to abstain from everything that does poison life. If we were to remember death a little more, there would be less confusion in our lives, both personal and collective.

You reproach yourself for pride. Good, very good. Be on the lookout for its appearance and immediately cut it off. Pride likes to do everything for itself, but you are to do everything for the glory of God and the good of others, not thinking of yourself, not having pity for yourself. Indeed, the proud outwardly do most of the very same things that those who are not proud do, except that they have a different direction and different intentions in everything. Our job is to redirect these intentions from pride to self-reproach, and then direct our actions in line with this. This must be learned. Learn, learn.
You want me to scold you without mercy. Nothing would come of this. To me, you have been chaste and pure so far. It remains for me to wish that the Lord always keep you the way 148
PATH OF A VIRTUOUS LIFE you have seemed to me; if you are not like that in actual fact, may He vouchsafe to make you so.

GOVENIE: THE PATH OF A VIRTUOUS LIFE One who hcts vesolved to stand on the path oj^a vivtuous lije must consciouslyfast. Instvuctwn on pwpev ^ovenie. Ehe conduct of one preparingfor Confession and Communion in church.

Glory be to Thee, O Lord! The fast has drawn near. You have brought me pleasure by saying that you intend to begin fasting and attending Divine Services immediately.^ Do not change your intention. People usually fast and attend services during Holy Week, but do not put it off until then yourself 1 . Trans, note: In the opening paragraph to this letter, St. Theoph an uses the worcls^£7T^, (verb) govente (noun). The first is translated as 'Tast,” here referring to the Lenten fast, which is familiar to Christians in the West.

The second word is occasionally translated as fast,” but its meaning is actually more complex, implying customs associated with the fasting periods in the Orthodox Church that are today unknown in the Western churches.

Govenie, according to the Polnyy Tserkovno-Slavyanskiy Slovar {Complete Church Slavonic Dictionary), is “the reverential custom of the Greek- Russian Orthodox Church. According to it, on one of the weeks of any fast period (primarily that of Great Lent), the Christian abstains not only from rich foods, but also from the immoderate consumption of the most simple foods, if not for the entire week, then two or three days of it, before going to confession and to partake of the Holy ISdysteries; at the same time he participates in daily church services. It is cjuite interesting and helpful to examine the root of this word: In Sanskrit, go means speech, especially in the spiritual sense; hava means a sacrifice; gu means to sacrifice. Hence, judging from this one may think that, in ancient times, govenie consisted of prayers combined with sacrifices. For the sake of brevity, the term govenie will be used from this point on without translation. 149

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Those who fast and attend services during Holy Week include people who have already fasted, attended services and taken Communion during the preceding weeks who desire to take Communion a second time; those who are compelled by some sort of necessity; or those for whom it is desirable to hasten the end of the labor of the fast, for there are only three days to labor.
We will apply only the first case to you.
Lord give the blessing for you to perform govenie in a fitting manner. Each faster must do everything that you intend to do.

Fast, go to church, seclude yourself, read, think and study yourself; all of this is necessary. But all of these actions must be directed toward one goal: worthy Communion of the holy Mysteries of Christ. In order to commune worthily, the soul must be cleansed with repentance. All the various actions of govenie—going to church, prayer at home, fasting, and everything else, are undertaken in order to accomplish the repentance as it should be, with sincere contrition and a firm resolution to not offend the Lord anymore.

The first thing to do in making repentance is to go inside yourself. Earthly matters and cares and unrestrained wandering of thoughts all over the place do not allow us to go inside ourselves and to study ourselves. Therefore the faster, to the extent possible during the time of the fast, shuts off his cares, and instead of going about his usual business, stays at home.

This cutting off of cares is a matter of extreme importance in govenie. He who does not do so will certainly spend the fast haphazardly. You, too, must cut off your cares. No matter how few these cares are, they do exist and distract us. Set about govenie having laid aside all cares.
Let us suppose that you have cast everything aside and have gone to your room. What is there to do? It is possible to sit around in private doing nothing. You must take up occupations 150
PATH OF A VIRTUOUS LIFE that are conducive X.o govenie, W^hat kind of occupations? Prayer, reading, meditation.

Prayer is that which is in addition to prayer in church. That is already self-evident. You know how to pray in church, of course. However, do take it into consideration! Go to church willingly, as to the house of God, your own home; go there without being contemptuous or bored. Go to church not just to be at the service, but in order to pray from your heart. Pray with sincere warmth, with an outpouring before God, with feelings of contrition, humility and reverential fear and with diligent petitions for your vital spiritual needs. To succeed at this, you have to think it out ahead of time, and, having come to church, you must force yourself to it. Do not just consider attendance at the service to be useless; during the course of the service the heart is warmed and calls out warmly to God. That is the main thing. You must also listen to the service, and align your thoughts and feelings with it. Variation, as long as it is directed toward one goal and is not distracting, will maintain the attention’s nourishing and constructive exertion in an agreeable manner. Attend to what is being sung and read, especially during the petitions, for they are the summary of all of the needs for which we turn to God unashamedly.

Usually, however, the thoughts do stray. This is from a lack of prayerful feeling. However, you may deal with straying thoughts in this way: As soon as you notice your thoughts have left church, turn them back and do not ever allow yourself consciously to daydream or to stray in thought during govenie, or at any other time. When the thoughts stray unbeknownst to you, this is also a small sin; but when you begin wandering off in thought purposely while you are in church, this is a sin. The Lord is among those who are in church. He who does not think about the Lord in church, but who daydreams instead, is like a man who has gone to the king in order to ask him for something.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE who then begins to make faces and fidget in the king’s presence, without paying any attention to him. Perhaps you will not be completely successful in preventing the straying of thoughts while maintaining your effort; but it is possible and it is necessary to not allow yourself to daydream intentionally. With respect to the straying of thoughts there are these two rules: 1) As soon as you notice this straying, turn the thoughts back, and 2) do not consciously allow the thoughts to wander.

A remedy against the straying of thoughts is mental attention, attention to the fact that the Lord is before us and we are before Him. One must place the entire mind in this thought and not deviate from it. The attention is attached to the Lord by the fear of God and by the desire to please God. From these come warmth of the heart, which draws the attention to the One Lord. Work at stirring the heart, and you will see for yourself how it keeps the thought in check. It is necessary to force yourself Without labor and mental effort you will not attain anything spiritual. Prostrations help a lot in the warming of the heart. Do these a little more often, performing both bows from the waist and full prostrations to the ground.

May the Lord grant that you feel delight in being at church so that you will rush to it, just as people rush into a warm room from the cold. During the fast, the primary stimulator of action, the reason for which we perform govenie^ is worthy attendance at church. Other actions aid and assist this, but they will be discussed another time. 152
33 GOVENIE AND CONDUCT AT HOME Continuation concerning govenie. Conduct at home.

I shall continue our discussion of govenie. The person performing govenie knows only church and home. Suppose you have just come home; what is there? Even at home it is necessary to maintain a God-pleasing turning of heart and mind to the Lord with as much strength as possible. Hurry to your room right after church and greet it with several prostrations, reverently asking the Lord that you spend the time ahead of you alone at home in a way that will benefit the soul. Then you need to sit down and rest for a while. Do not let the thoughts stray even at this point, but say to yourself, not thinking about anything. Lord have mercy! Lord have mercy! Once you have rested, you need to do something; either pray or do some handicraft. W^hich handicraft you do does not matter; you already know what you like to do. It is impossible to occupy oneself with spiritual things all the time; you need to have some sort of enjoyable handicraft. However, do it when the soul is fatigued, and you are not capable of reading, thinking or praying to God. If your spiritual pursuits are going well, then you do not have to have a handicraft. It is meant for occupying time, which otherwise would be spent in idleness, always most pernicious, and all the more so govenie.

How is one to pray at home? You said it well, that one has to add a little to the usual prayer rule. Yes, that is necessary. It is better if you do not just read more prayers; instead, you should pray a little more without the prayerbook, expressing your vital spiritual needs to the Lord in your own words. In 153

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE the morning and evening, read no more than on ordinary days, but before the beginning of the recited prayer and afterward, pray your own prayers. In between the prayers that you read, insert your own prayer with prostrations, both from the waist and to the ground, and from a kneeling position. Importune the Lord, the Mother of God and your Angel Protector, expressing everything to Them in your own words, everything that you feel is of extreme necessity for you. Pray that They grant you to know yourself, and once you have this knowledge, that They furnish you with the desire and strength to remedy the irremediable, and, even more, that They fill the heart with the spirit of contrition and humility, these being a sacrifice to God, the most suitable to Efim. But do not make a rule that is too long and drawn out for you. It is better to stand at prayer a little more often and to make a few prostrations during the course of the day, doing this frequently, so that the entire day is filled with prostrations. Do not take your mind off of the Lord at all; either stand at prayer or do something else.

After prayer, do some reading with meditation. You need to read, not in order to pack your mind with diverse information and ideas, but to receive edification and to understand how best to accomplish those things which are necessary for us during these days oi govenie. For this one must read a little, but each item that is read must be brought to conscious feeling by devoting lengthy attention to it.

What should be read? Just spiritual books, of course. Of these, I cannot recommend anything to you more than the writings of Bishop Tikhon.^ There is the little book Arise and Conquer, a selection of articles by him that are conducive to repentance. There is another book about repentance and Communion, sermons on Great Lent and the preparatory week preceding it. I have heard that it is very helpful at this time; it
1. St. Tikhon of Zadonsk, Bishop ofVoronezh (1724-1783). 154
CONDUCT AT HOME discusses only repentance and Communion. I cannot find any better reading for you. These books have been given to you.

Unhurried, meditative reading ofsuitable books will inspire the soul more forcefully than anything else. Apply yourself to this. In the morning after prayer (matins is heard in the evening), devote everything to it until the Hours. It also serves as preparation for church prayer. After the Hours you may continue, as long as there is the desire and attention. If there comes a prayerftil urge during your reading, stand up and pray. Should you read with somebody or alone? It is better to do it alone, because this is more conducive to studying yourself and to continuing what you are reading for yourself. You must insert meditation into the reading, because reading alone will dissipate into daydreaming, and you will not be able to refocus on it.

So, you have done your reading, meditated, performed prostrations and all the studies appropriate for one observing govenie at home. However, you will not be able to keep your mind in such a state of continual exertion at all times. When you get tired, you may sit and work for a little while, as I have already mentioned to you.

You also said well that it is necessary to fast a little. Yes, it is necessary, but not too much. You already eat so little. It is necessary to have strength to stand in church and make prostrations at home. Do whatever you find is comfortable and convenient for you. It is necessary to let the body know that it is the cause for having to repent, and that it must therefore bear the labors govenie. It is also necessary to cut back the amount and restfulness of sleep a little. This item, it seems, will demand sacrifice of you. To the best of your ability, do not neglect this. Every form of deprivation is suitable during this time. 155

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE What about conversation? One may converse, not about trifling things, but about spiritual things. Instead of conversation, it would be better to set aside an hour for reading together. This would be very suitable in the evening. Nothing would be better, if one of you could give edifying accounts that manifest the power of repentance and Communion. For your readings together, you should select something from the Lives of the Saints.
That is enough for now. I will add whatever else is needed later. 34 PREPARATION FOR CONFESSION Continuation concerning govenie. Preparation for Confession. Reviewing one 's life.
We will continue our discussion of govenie.

Everything that has been said is the framework in which govenie is placed, or the outward rite and performance of it, which is usually observed by good fasters. Work a little to adhere to it, if you want to be a good faster. Do not just adopt a sad countenance, do not disfigure your face. Do everything with willingness, with a good and joyful disposition of spirit.

Spend your time in the same way that people who are going to a feast with the king spend time. Such people think and talk only about what the feast is going to be like, how they will meet with the king, what to say when he greets them, how to dress best and not smudge their face, and the like. Ahead of you, however, lies something incomparably better and higher: A feast, not with an earthly king, but with the Heavenly King. If

PREPARATION FOR CONFESSION you will take a little trouble to get yourself dressed and prepared in a way that will please the King, then you will be favored by Him. You will receive that which is priceless, and you will have unspeakable joy. ^ You said you would begin to fast; then fast! Lord give the blessing! Think, if you please, how you are going to dress. Get rid of your old dresses, new ones are necessary. If one of the old ones seems suitable, wash it, iron it, and make everything look like it is entirely new. By this I want to say that you need to reexamine yourself; acknowledge what is bad and throw it out, but keep the good, correct it and perfect it.
Let us go inside ourselves then, and begin to look over what is within.

The interference of any outsider in this matter is inappropriate and completely impossible. No one except for you yourself can go inside of yourself and analyze the workings of your conscience. You will want to do this yourself. I will give you only a few instructions. In the books which you were told to read, there is much in the way of instruction concerning this. I will tell you a few more things, however.

To examine yourself well, you must turn your attention to the three aspects of our active life. These aspects are 1) the actions, those isolated acts which are performed at a given time in a given place and under given circumstances; 2) the disposition of the heart and the characteristic tendencies which are concealed beneath the actions; and 3) the general character of life.

Our entire life is made up of an uninterrupted series of acts: thoughts, words, deeds; some giving way to others, with these in turn being followed by still others. Look over all these acts, each one separately; there is no way to define their moral value. Even
1. The preceding passage draws imagery from Matthew 22:1-14, the parable of the marriage feast. 157
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE if you took it into your head to review and reassess the acts you have accomplished in one day, you would not be able to do it.

Man is a creature of perpetual motion. How many times does he change his mind and re-do things from morning until evening! How many things does he commit from confession to confession! How is one to be then? There is no necessity to review and reassess everything separately. You have an unsleeping guard, your conscience. When something evil is done, it does not let it slip by, and no matter how you try to justify it to your conscience, it will not cease evaluating everything in its own way: This is evil, that is evil. Here then is your first step’.
Listen to your conscience and all those actions which it exposes to you, without any excuse, acknowledge as sinful and prepare to confess them.

We may call this the first and the last step; that is, the admission that one is undeniably guilty of what the conscience has revealed, and the decision to avoid it in the future. This would be sufficient, if it were possible to be certain that the conscience itself is correct in everything. It does happen that the conscience does not notice something because of some sort of confusion; it might forget something because of old age, and it may not consider something to be a sin out of ignorance and incomplete knowledge ofwhat actions are necessary for us. So it is a necessity here for the conscience to turn for assistance to the commandments of God that are depicted in the Word of God, and, in reviewing them, to find out if we have done anything contrary to any of them. By doing this, we may recall much which we have forgotten, and much of that which is recalled will be presented in a form other than how we interpreted it.

The Word of God becomes like a mirror. Just as each person, when looking in a mirror, sees a blemish or spot on his face or clothing, so the soul, when reading the Word of God and examining the commandments which are enumerated 158

PREPARATION FOR CONFESSION there, cannot help but see whether it is correct in all of these commandments or not. The conscience, enlightened by this Word of the living God, will tell this to the soul at once quite frankly. Here then is the second step for you. Go over the commandments and see whether you carry them out or not.

For example, the commandment enjoins us to offer charity every time someone asks for it. Look at yourself, do you always offer it, or not? Do you sometimes refuse it, not because of any important reason, but simply because you scorn the beggar? If this turns out to be the case, take note: It is 2. sin. The commandment says to forgive everyone everything, even that which is unpleasant and offensive. Look at yourself again, to see whether you are always deferential; haven’t you had disputes, heated words, and maybe even quarrels? If you can recall any, note once again. It is a sin, although the conscience does not always place such actions in this category. Again, you must place all your hope in God. Have you always done this? In the usual course of action you do not notice it. But when you meet with need, it immediately surfaces and becomes apparent on what your soul relies, whether on God or whether you forget about God and rely on something else. It is obvious that you must use all of your abilities in order to pull through difficult situations, for they too are from God, but final success can be hoped for only from God, which is why we pray to Him for help, and when something turns out well, we thank Him as the Sole Deliverer, disregarding our own abilities. Take a look, have you acted in this way? If not, take note once again: It is a sin. Do this with each commandment, and note which actions you have committed against which commandment. In this way you will make a detailed examination of your actions.
What is the best way to do this? Have you studied the catechism? In it each commandment is explained and it is shown which good deeds are required of us with each com159

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE mandment, and which sins are forbidden. Take this and review your actions with its assistance. I recall that at your house you have a book by Bishop Platon of Kostroma that tells how to give Confession and how to confess. In it is a very thorough enumeration of many questions, which are to be asked of those who are taking Confession. Perhaps by using this book it will be even easier for you to examine yourself.

I suppose you would like to study yourself in the proper way for the first time, and to determine what you are and what is in you. Work on reviewing yourself thoroughly according to these instructions. Later on, it will not be so complicated for you. But work on it a little for now.
I will cover what still remains for you to do after this the next time I write. 35 THE DISPOSITION OF ONE’S HEART Continuation. Reviewing the disposition ofone's heart.
Designating the chiefcharacter or spirit ofone's life.

To continue. The second aspect oflife is the disposition or the mood and inclination ofthe heart. Actions do not provide full knowledge of the self It is necessary to look more deeply into oneself and to examine what your heart is like, paying more attention to this than to actions. Suppose a man who is normally kind-hearted happens to behave stubbornly on a single occasion (say he fails to help somebody). Then you encounter another person who behaves in the same way, not just by chance, but because he is stingy by nature. On the surface, both actions are identical, but going by the inner disposition of the actors, there is a big difference between them. Acts are actions isolated i6o

DISPOSITION OF ONE’S HEART in time and in place, while dispositions signify permanent inclinations of the heart by which the character and way of life ^ person are defined, and from which come all his greatest desires and the direction for his acts. The good dispositions are called virtues, while the worst are called vices, depraved inclinations and passions.

Christ the Savior indicates which dispositions a Christian must have in his heart. These are humility, contrition, meekness, love of righteousness and truth, mercifulness, pureheartedness, love of peace, and patience. The Apostle St. Paul indicates the following blessed Christian dispositions of the heart, which are the fruits of the Holy Spirit: Love, joy, peace, longsujjering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance

5.22, 23). In another place he writes. Put on therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved, bowels of mercies, kindness, humbleness ofmind, meekness, longsuffering; Forbearing one another, andforgiving one another, ifany man have a quarrel against any: even as Christforgave you, so also do ye. And above all these things put on charity, which is the bond ofperfectness. And let the peace ofGod rule in your hearts, to the which also ye are called in one body; and beye thankful (Col. 3:12-15). In contrast to these dispositions are the vices or passions, the sources of all the evil deeds that ruin us. The main ones are pride, vanity, selfishness, intemperateness, anger, hatred, envy, idleness, desire for sensual pleasures, gloom, despair. Concerning these, the Apostle decreed that not only should Christians not possess them, but also that they should not mention them among themselves: Let it not be once named amongyou (Eph. 5:3).
You see how rigorous this is! Please examine yourself thoroughly to see if you have any bad inclinations and passions.
Everyone possesses each one of them to a small degree, but they are not deeply rooted or permanent. And everyone has one main passion around which all the others entwine themselves. You 161

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE must take care to seek this one out above all. Although because of your youth such a passion will not be obvious, there should be perceptible traces if you probe. After you have found it, assess the others; find the one that is closest to it, and the one that is farthest. And you will understand the structure of your heart.

What a valuable acquisition! Because after this, when you purge yourself of passions and bad inclinations, then it will be apparent to you where to direct your efforts and strength, namely, at your main passion. Then you will overcome it, and all the others will disperse in and of themselves. As in war, once the main enemy force has been shattered, you have only to pursue the others and rout them without any trouble. It is easy to correct deeds. Just do not do it. The heart, however, cannot be transformed and corrected all at once. A struggle lies ahead. In this struggle, if you do not know where to direct your blows, you will exhaust yourself thrashing about to no purpose, and you will not achieve any success. Take a little trouble!

The third aspect oflife is the spirit oflife. This is the most important as well as the most complex element. A malicious spirit is so subtle and so able to mask itself with a facade of kindness and opportuneness that one must have the sharpest spiritual vision in order to detect it. A good spirit, on the other hand, is obvious, because it has a unique purpose; that is, having put everything else aside, it lives for God. Contrary to this is the spirit that lives for itself (egoism). This type of spirit very often, if not always, adopts a secondary direction: living for the world.

Thus, if we suppose that the spirit of one’s life is indicated by who it is a person lives for, it should be easy for you to determine the spirit of your life. This is accomplished once you have determined who it is you live for, or, as you are still just starting out in life, who it is you most wish to live for, what it is you strive for in your heart, and to whom you will to 162

DISPOSITION OF ONE’S HEART dedicate your life the most. The spirit of your life, even though your life is still in an embryonic stage like a feeble baby bird, is defined by the characteristics of that to which you most incline.

He who lives for God has a God-fearing spirit that endeavors to please the One God. He who lives only for himself has a spirit that is self-pleasing, egoistical, selfish, carnal. He who lives for the world has a world-loving or vain spirit. Going by these characteristics, look and see what kind of spirit breathes in you.

Judging from the fact that you were filled with the desire to draw closer to God, it must be supposed that the main spirit of your life is good, the true spirit is good, as it should be. Again, judging from the fact that you reacted so adversely to the secular way of life in which there is the spirit of the world, it must be supposed that such a spirit has no place or power in you, although it may come over you if you are not careful. Whether or not you possess an egoistical spirit still remains under question. It seems to me that it is there, although to what extent I do not know. So what if it is present; since you have expressed the desire to draw closer to God, your soul does not belong to the world, and your egoistical spirit will soon dissipate if you give more and more rein to your heart for the kindling of desire for God. Please take this into consideration.

Thus you see what lies ahead for you to do; do it. At first glance, it appears to be an enormous task, but m actual fact it is simple and easy. Pray to God, begin the task, and you can determine everything in a single evening. You do not have to cross oceans, just examine what is within yourself. However, do not put this off until the evening before Confession. No, begin at this very moment, at the start of the fast. You will gradually discover everything in a thorough and clear manner. It will be difficult, perhaps, but only this one time. If you then begin to live according to the inclination of your good spirit, then life itself will lead you to a more complete self-knowledge. For why
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE is it that we do not know ourselves for the most part? Because we live in a careless manner.
Let us suppose that you have reexamined yourself thoroughly and that you have found many faults. What should you do next? What do you gain from such knowledge? I will write you about this next time.

What use can you make of the knowledge you have achieved of yourself? First of all, you should judge yourself in all your shortcomings, without making any excuses or justifications. At the Presanctified Liturgy, after the choir sings, “Lord I have cried unto thee,” the reader recites, “O let not my heart be inclined to any evil thing, let me not be involved in ungodly works.” This suggests to Christians to pray that God not allow them to be filled with craftiness to think up excuses for sins. Do not expect repentance from a person who thinks up such excuses; he who has no repentance cannot begin self-correction.

Consequently, the main thing is that to judge yourself without pity, you must bring yourself to the point where you can sincerely utter in your heart, “I am completely guilty.” When you come to say in your heart “I am guilty,” then you must apply the fear of the Divine Judgment. If your own conscience condemns you, then God Himself does not excuse 164

REPENTANCE AND COMMUNION you. And God does see you as guilty. If you are seen as such, then you are condemned as such. If you are condemned, then you are sentenced and a fitting punishment is decided upon.
This punishment will fall on you sooner or later, and it is already hanging over you when you are condemned by God.

How then should one be? A person would not know how he is to be if there were no Divine grace. A gracious God gives us the hope of forgiveness of guilt, ifwe repent with contrition and set forth a firm intention to flee past sins and not anger God by them. This is the essence of repentance.
Do not just be a passionless seeker of your shortcomings; mourn them, and sincerely regret that they were committed.
Mourning begets the humble resolution to flee shortcomings; while knowledge alone, although it is accompanied by the intention to be cautious, leads to pride, from which deliver us, O Lord!

Once you have made the decision to flee your faults, it is then necessary to plan and decide how to do this successfully, so that you can begin the actual healing of yourself from this moment on. Suppose, for example, that you get angry at something: Do not get angrier. Then decide the best way to correct yourself so that you do not get angry again. Do the same thing with everything else. You must do this now, before the fact: Decide how you are going to act in a particular instance so that you do not repeat a shortcoming. The best way to go about this is to write down your shortcomings as soon as you are aware of them, and then write out a prescription for how you intend to correct them. This will be your first general and completely corrective Confession. Put a little effort into doing this, for the sake of the Lord. You will see what control you accjuire over yourself, how powerfully you begin to restrain yourself, with the awareness that this way of acting and no other is the right one.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Along with mourning for one’s sins and resolving not to sin, it is necessary to add diligent prayer to the Lord so that He will grant His help in opposing sin, and also to believe that the Lord will not withhold such help. Christians must possess the firm conviction that the sins they mourn and confess and promise to flee from are forgiven them by the Lord s grace for the sake of His death on the Cross. They must also believe that at the same time the Divine blessing is being given for the avoidance of sin by virtue of that same death on the cross. This blessing is bestowed in the presence of a strong resolution not to sin and a clear, firm belief in Christ the Savior.

When you finish doing these things, you will be ready for Confession, and when you receive absolution for your sins at Confession, you will be ready for Holy Communion. When you are sincerely repentant and have a firm resolution to make amends, the Lord comes in His Holy Mysteries and enters into you, and He will be with you and you will be with Him. Oh, great and unutterable grace of God the All-Beneficent!

It has occurred to me to tell you something! Take the Lives of the Saints for the month of March and read the story of how blessed Theodora endured toll-houses.^ This story is included in the life of St. Basil the Younger for March 26. The life itself of that starets is great. Begin immediately with the story of Theodora; you can find where it begins from the notes in the margins.

Here is a short life of the elder: Basil the Younger lived in the desert not far from Constantinople. He was seized as a spy and after enduring tortures he was finally cast into the sea. God miraculously delivered him from drowning, and he came secretly to the city where a kind person gave him refuge. The elder again took up his ascetic labors as he had done in the desert, and

REPENTANCE AND COMMUNION during this time Theodora waited on him fervently. This holy woman died before the elder. Basil had another disciple, Gregory, a layman who was very God-fearing. Gregory wanted to know what Theodora had received for her devoted service to St. Basil. He asked the elder about this. The elder prayed, and then Gregory had a dream in which he saw Theodora in Paradise, in a place prepared for Basil; it was bright, very bright. Gregory asked Theodora how she had separated from her body, and how she had attained such a blessed place. The blessed Theodora replied with the story of how she had died and the toll-houses she then underwent. This is the story which I advise you to read with close attention. It is very instructive. It will guide you toward self-knowledge and instill within you a renewed conviction in the force of tearful repentance and Gonfession. Until you can obtain the book, I shall paraphrase the story for you.

St. Theodora underwent twenty toll-houses. 1) T\\q first \s in which one is tormented by the sins of idle, unruly, vulgar, and scandalous human word and speech; of blasphemy, ridicule, bawdy worldly songs, indecent exclamations, laughter, and raucous laughter. 2) The second toll-house is that of falsehoods, in which one is tormented by every sort of falsehood, especially perjury; the taking of God s name in vain; false witness; the breaking of vows made to God; confession of sins which have not actually occurred, and other similar kinds of falsehood. 3) The thirdly the one of judgment and slander, the defamation of one s neighbors, making fun of their shortcomings and sins. 4) ^\\Q. fiourth is that of gluttony, sensuality, satiation, feasting, drunkenness, violation of fasts. 5) The fiijih is that of sloth, in which one is tormented by all the days and hours spent in idleness, parasites and hirelings who do not deserve the wages they are being paid; those who are not joyful at church services on Sundays and feast days; those who are bored during Matins and Liturgy; those who are indifferent towards matters concern167

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE ing their salvation. 6) The sixth is different kinds of theft. 7) The seventh is love of money and stinginess. 8) The eighth is usury and every kind of exploitation. 9) The ninth is injustice, in which are tormented judges who make unjust judgments, acquitting the guilty, condemning the innocent, and those who withhold a worker’s pay or maintain incorrect weights and measures when buying and selling. 1 0) The tenth is envy, hatred, hatred of one’s brother and hatred of one’s friend. 11) The eleventh IS pride, vanity, conceit, contempt, boastfulness, failure to honor one’s parents, disobedience to authority. 12) The twelfth is anger and wrath. 1 3) The thirteenth is bearing grudges; holding anger in one’s heart against someone who is close; vindictiveness; and the repaying of evil for evil. 14) The fourteenth is murder, where it is not criminal acts that torment, but also any sort of wounding, such as striking somebody about the head and shoulders out of vexation; slapping somebody in the face; pushing somebody out of anger. 15) The fifteenth is sorcery, charms, the use of potions, incantations, and invocations of demons. 16), 17), 18) The sixteenth, seventeenth, and eighteenth are carnal sins. 19) The nineteenth is heresy, of incorrect knowledge of one’s faith; falling away from Orthodoxy; desecration of God and all that is holy. 20) The twentieth is lack of mercy, heartlessness, and the cutting off of one’s generosity toward the needs of the poor.

Blessed Theodora encountered the same things which every soul encounters. The Apostle called the demons the principalities of the air. They are evil and obtrusive: Would they let a soul pass, when it is to ascend to the Divine Throne, without attempting to seize it or at least to disturb it with their terrors?

How is one to be? For our great comfort the tears of repentance along with repentant deeds, especially that of almsgiving, wash away all sins. Blessed Theodora saw many times how the demons would bring scrolls in which her sins were recorded, and when i68

THE WILL OF GOD they would unroll these scrolls to expose her, there would be nothing inside. When she asked the angels who attended her why this was so, they explained to her that when somebody sincerely repents of his sins, fasts, prays, and does merciful deeds, his sins are washed away.

Do not desire to be worldly-wise, but take this story to heart, and undertake to correct all your imperfections in accordance with its teaching. 37 HARMONY WITH THE WILL OF GOD The awakening resolve to amend one’s life must be clearly distinguished as a yearning to act in regard to everything in harmony with the will ofGod. The peace and thejoy ofthe servants of God. A warning about an incomplete resolve to live according to God. One who is neither hot nor cold will be rejected by God.

I have not heard whether you have begun the fast. Well, when you do begin, it would be useful for you to read everything that I have written to you about fasting. In the meantime, I will add a few words to clarify several points, because the subject is too complex to explain in just a few lines.

Let us suppose that you have placed yourself in a frame of mind for fasting, or are observing at least the outward procedure of fasting, and have begun in the meantime to scrutinize yourself strictly in accordance with the instructions you have been given, so that having examined all these imperfections, you may begin to be diligent in all things.
We need a good definition of the phrase to be diligent. This is the heart of the matter; if a person is not cognizant of it, he 169
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE can make a gross error, believing in the meantime that he is doing everything correctly.

Let us begin to be diligent. In what way then are we bad now? What is wrong with our behavior? I think that even you have had the experience of not knowing what you are going to say at Confession, because it is not apparent what you are sinful in. How could you have come to think that way? From it being unclear as to what our lives must be like, and whether our deeds and our thoughts are directed toward that end. How could this be unclear when, in looking at your life in general, it strikes you that it, like everyone else’s life, is wicked and meaningless? Do we remain calm within ourselves, saying or only secretly feeling that there is still something missing?

Everything that has been written thus far has been in opposition to this so that you will not be afflicted by it. If you act in accordance with what has been instructed, then you will not even say, “What is missing?” Pay a little more attention to this.
That is what it is all about, as we have said more than once!

Our entire lives, in all of their parts and details, must be devoted to God. The general rule is that everything you do should be done according to Divine will and for the sake of pleasing God, in praise of His Most Holy Name. Thus, we should examine each act which occurs to see if it is in com pli ance with the Divine will and then perform it with the conviction that it is totally in compliance with it and is pleasing to God. A person who always acts with such discretion and in the clear consciousness of pleasing God with his actions cannot fail at the same time to acknowledge that his life is proceeding truthfully.

Although his acts are not brilliant or perfect, he permits nothing consciously in them that would offend God or would not be 1 . This is covered in detail in Bp. Theophan’s pamphlet Seven Wordsfor the Week Before Lenty Moscow, 1902.

THE WILL OF GOD pleasing to Him. This consciousness fills his heart with peaceful quiet from the tranquillity of the conscience, and with that spiritual joy which is borne of the feeling that he is not alien to God. For although he is not great, or distinguished, or famous, he is still His servant who tries in every way possible to please Him, directs all his efforts toward this, and believes that God Himself sees him as such.

This is how everyone’s moral life should be. How in actual fact is the greater part of it? We live carelessly. Our thoughts do not possess any conscious, independent directing of all actions, both great and small, toward pleasing God. Actions occur haphazardly; what happens is for the most part in accordance with procedures established because everyone acts in a particular way, with no thought as to the suitability of these actions to life’ s main purpose.

Recently I wrote you concerning the spirit of life, how it is either God-fearing, vain, or worldly. I forgot to add a fourth possibility: Neither one thing nor the other. ^ Don’t the majority of people breathe with this spirit? They seemingly have nothing against God, but they also have no deliberate intention of pleasing God. For example, they happen to drop into church; they drop in, they leave, they make no apology. W^hen they pray at home they make a bow or two, and that is it. They are satisfied. That is how they are in everything with respect to God.

While they are not obvious egotists, when it comes to the preservation of their own interests, in order to avoid making any self-sacrifice, they can always find a reason to avoid it. They also are not too blatantly vain, but have no objection to amusing themselves with worldly matters. People of this sort are rather common. They are indifferent to the matter of pleasing God and salvation; they are neither hot nor cold. God turns away from them and rejects them. 1 . See Letter 26 (at the end).

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE You have not belonged to this category up to this point? I do not think you have entirely. However, most of the things you have done have been because that is what everyone in your group does. Abandon what you have been. I assure you that if you do everything in good conscience that has been prescribed, you will cease to resemble such people in even a small way, and you will begin to do all things well and exceedingly well. It is not that the deeds will take on a special character; however, they will take on a special coloring, a different quality, a different beauty and seemliness. Lord give the blessing! I wish this for you with all my heart. Once you have begun to act in accordance with the will of God in everything, you will certainly be filled with inner peace, and feel genuine peace; clear, warm, joyous peace, which is paradise for the soul.

To make this come about, however, it is necessary to decide on such a life. To reach the point of decisiveness, it is necessary to feel the depravity of life, the disregard for what is important—the being neither one thing nor the other—and then to feel the delightful worthiness of the attentive life. And this is little. It is necessary to be contrite, for even if the time one has lived has been short, all the same, a part of that has been lived in vain. A contrite heart concerning one’s defects before God is the basis of the decision to be diligent toward Him in the future.
You must attend to all of this now. May the Lord help you!

May the Lord Who arranges the salvation of all who are saved through each one’s own set of circumstances also teach you how to put things aright, so that your life does not pass in vain, as neither one thing nor the other; instead, may it be God-pleasing and bring salvation to you and serve as the means for receiving the Heavenly Kingdom. 172

A CONSCIOUS RENEWAL OF THE VOWS GIVEN AT BAPTISM The necessary resolve ofone preparingfor Communion to dedicate himselfto God is to be considered as a conscious renewal ofthe vows given for us at Baptism.

I have still more to add. After reading everything that lies ahead ofyou, do not think, “Oh! How terrible! How am I going to be able to do all of this? If it were really like this, it would be impossible to get a solid grip on things because it is so critically important. But there is nothing particularly difficult or overwhelming here. Everything is simple. Begin gradually and you will be able to do everything. Even if you are not able to do everything as you would like, do what you are able to do.

The Lord is not strict about minor details. He values diligence and purpose. He will accept your effort to reexamine and remake yourself entirely as a finished deed. Just do not give up the effort, but keep going until you reach the end. The most important thing, that is, the firmly resolved intention of dedicating your entire self to the Lord must be constantly kindled, strengthened, and established within you once and for all. This is an essential condition for receiving the grace which is imparted in the Mysteries of Repentance and Holy Communion.

I will remind you of how our salvation was arranged, and you will see what the most important thing is for you now. We sinned through our forefathers. Divine benevolence took pity on us and arranged our salvation in the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God, Who became incarnate for the sake of man and for our salvation. He accomplished this salvation by His death on 173

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE the Cross and Resurrection, and the sending down of the Holy Spirit. Those who believe in Him and approach Him with repentance receive remission of sins for the sake of His death, and receive the grace of the Holy Spirit in the Mysteries for the sake of deciding to follow Him and His teaching in life . All this is accomplished by Baptism and Chrismation. One who has received grace begins a new life, having been born of the Holy Spirit. We received this new birth in our childhood. Our parents and godparents attested to faith for us. They have been entrusted with seeing that we live according to the Lord’s commandments.

It is for this reason that in these first Mysteries everything is done for us as if we had consciously expressed faith and made our vows, but obviously on the condition that when we grow up and come to self-knowledge, we ourselves willfully take upon ourselves the obligation which was expressed for us by others during Baptism. When someone does this, then the Divine grace, which has been operating up to this point alone and in secret, begins operating with freedom and often allows the person who has made the decision to sensibly feel and manifestly help in arranging his own salvation. This occurs for the most part during the fast.

This is what you are to do, then. You have previously fasted and done everything that is usually done at that time. But now you want to do this with more discretion, with full awareness of the matter. Going by this, I make clear to you that now you are to stand up so thatyou willpronounce those vows which were given for you by others. When you were baptized, satan and all his works and all his service (worldly life) were renounced; now you yourself must renounce all of these things. When you were baptized, everything was confirmed for you, that you would unite to Christ and serve Him and worship Him; now you yourself must proclaim this with all your heart and mind. 174
THE ENEMY LEADS ONE ASTRAY Enter into this with conviction and understand the determination that accompanies it. May the Lord be your helper, and also the Mother of God and your Guardian Angel!

May the grace of the All-Holy Spirit overshadow you in the Mysteries of Confession and Holy Communion, which you should approach with a blessed disposition and decision. 39 HOW THE ENEMY TRIES TO LEAD ONE ASTRAY Amendment oflife consists in the erecting anew not ofthe outward order hut in the spirit, with readiness to war with obstacles. Various means by which the enemy tries to lead astray those who stand on the path oftrue life.
Your last letter was a festive occasion for me! Your head has cleared and the decisions of your heart are adopting true and saving direction!

You have begun doing everything that is necessary. Lord give the blessing! All your intentions about your future new ways of life are good. However, so that you do not become lopsided in your enthusiasm, although this would be toward the right, ^ I rush this bit of advice.

Watch when you remake everything that you pay more attention to the inward things than to the outward. The outward things can be put aside for the time being as they are, with the exception of those things which by their very nature 1 . Temptation from the right is zeal without knowledge: over concern for correctness in externals of church life without proper attention to humility and compassion. The Lord accused the Pharisees of this saying, Ye pay tithe ofmint and anise. . . and have omitted the weightier matters. . . mercy, and faith. Matt. 23:23. 175
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE have a harmful action on the heart, making one vain, disturbing the thoughts, filling one with unnecessary desires, and the like.

Remaking, of course, must also touch on outward ways, but not so much in the form as in the spirit in which they need to be done. If you do this, then outwardly, everything will remain the same with a few exceptions, but the spirit will be completely different. The advantage of not breaking off outward ways too abruptly is that your change will not be too obvious to anyone.

Next, what you should have in your mind is something you already have, namely, do not think that the plan is simple to put into action. How many outer and inner obstacles there are! You are doing the right thing by preparing not to take the easy way, but to do battle. So! Get ready to struggle, and ask the Lord for everything, to grant you strength to endure everything hostile and hindering that you will encounter. Do not put your trust in yourself. Place all hope in the Lord, and His help will always be with you.

In preparing for battle, however, do not think that you will always be victorious. Often there will be only hardship that will bring nothing but affliction. You will often see that with each desire to be careful, slips and blunders steal by and creep in. Know ahead of time that this is the way things are. You will encounter them, but do not be afraid. You are seeing all of this now beforehand; do not expect the life ahead to be anything other than one in the midst of all kinds of hostility, worries and misfortunes.

Provide yourself with only one thing, strong courage: no matter what happens, stay with what you have begun. This alone must now be established and set for life by commitment and by steadfast perseverance. No matter how life goes, whatever successes and failures there are, you should give all of this over to God’s will. 176

THE ENEMY LEADS ONE ASTRAY It is evident from the experiences noted in the Lives of Saints that the Lord leads those who adhere to Him with warm love and who devote their lives to Him toward perfection in different ways. He will even allow the enemy to act in a hostile manner, not withholding in this, however. His helpful right hand. Everything is from God. But His ways are wondrous, and importantly, they are secret. Even the one who is being led perceives them only when looking back after the fact. How important then is the continual prayer. Save me by the way Thou knowesA Committing yourself into God’s hands with this prayer, the matter is entire and irrevocable.
The adversary will not sleep. God’s saints noted that he operates on those who are just beginning in two ways. Some he does not disturb at all and does not put up any opposition.

Those who do not encounter inward or outward impediments and who see that everything is going smoothly begin to fantasize that this is the way things are, and they suppose they have driven out all adversaries, who are unable to show themselves. As soon as such thoughts have settled in, the adversary immediately enters and begins fabricating vainglorious dreams from which are born self-conceit, the falling away from God’s help, and the cessation of searching and striving after this help. The result of this is deprivation. As soon as matters have reached this point, the adversary begins operating tyrannically to stir up evil inside and strong antagonism from outside, and the poor conceited person falls. Such instances are not rare. Allow me to bring this to your attention right now while you are figuring out how to order your life so that when you begin your new life and everything is going smoothly, you do not get a high opinion of yourself, but see in this instead the devil’s ambush, the most dangerous thing, and you will increase your caution and concern in this matter. Perfection, barely perceptible, comes after much 177
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE toil and after many years, not from the beginning and during the very first days.

On the other hand, the enemy attacks others with all his force and swiftness from the very start, and so such beginners are lost. No matter where such a person turns, everything is against him in his thoughts and senses. Outwardly it is apparent only that things are going contrary to good intentions and that nothing is favorable. The adversary does this to frighten the novice the first time and to make him give up his good intentions and return to his careless and inattentive life. As soon as he notices that the novice does not yield, but instead is holding his ground, he immediately backs off. A courageous stand against the adversary serves as a crown to the toilers, and he does not want to furnish them with such crowns. Keep in mind, then, that in case of heavy, antagonistic pressure, not to be frightened; know that this is a trap of the adversary that he will give up as soon as he detects your steadfastness.

You are doing very well not to see the path in front of you as being rosy. This is a realistic outlook on the matter. Prepare to be steadfast. However, you will be saved. There is still more to tell you, but that will be for another time.
