THE SPIRITUAL LIFE appearance. Self-indulgence still lives concealed within us even long after we have obviously renounced ourselves and devoted ourselves to God, even though we do not want to violate this decision by one iota. The passionate is always favorably encountered through this hidden self-indulgence or selfishness; such favor is expressed by greater or lesser sympathy toward the passionate. Thus, it is necessary to reject this sympathy and stir up anger. Both of these are encouraged by and made possible through recognition and acknowledgement of the enemy in its appearance in passionate thought or action, no matter how gratifying these are. As soon as you realize a passion’s belligerence, get angry at it in a very obvious way. This anger will arise of itself.

It does not require great intellectual labor to recognize the enemy in the passionate. It is sufficient to renew your belief that God does not favorably regard anything of the passions; that is why He does not favorably regard any who accept and cherish the passionate within themselves. You know the passionate sets God against us, and it separates us from Him. This is our final destruction. The thoughts and beliefs of someone who is attentive begin shining in the consciousness in the twinkling of an eye, and are immediately echoed in the heart as antagonism, anger and wrath toward the passionate. However, do not struggle with the passionate, devising various points of indictment against it. Success against it is very doubtful. At the same time you are sorting out your points of indictment, the accused—the passion—sits there as if in the dock, and is still defended by its lawyer—sympathy. This means you are keeping impurity within yourself willfully, which is dangerous. You cannot do this. Decide without any deliberation that as soon as you observe something passionate within yourself, you will recognize the enemy—and get angry at it. 236
57 STAGES IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF PASSIONS Various stages in the development ofpassions: passionate thoughtsy feelings and movements. The battle with them.

That the passionate shows itselfwithin us is not an inescapable misfortune. It signifies that we are impure, but it does not make us guilty. Our guilt begins from the point when we favorably incline ourselves toward a passion that has been observed; that is, we do not rush to acknowledge the enemy and do not arm ourselves against it with anger. On the contrary, we accept it and begin liking it, delighting in the impulse in which it appeared. This already shows that we have no objection to being familiar with the passionate, and, subsequently, enemies of God. Because the carnal mind—the passionate is enmity against God (Romans 8:7).

V^here assent begins, there also begins guilt, which increases by measure of its involvement in the passionate. I will describe for you how all of this happens. Our usual state is this: The thoughts wander about every which way; the emotions and desires, although they go back and forth, are without definite direction. This is how it is most of the time. Sometimes entire days pass like this. These thoughts are for the most part vain, being attached to routine everyday affairs and tasks at hand.

Among these empty thoughts or daydreams about nothing often erupt. Both empty and vain thoughts throng, for the most part, on the surface of the soul. You have been told more than once how to subdue and regulate this disarray of thoughts that impedes remembrance of God and Godly things. 237

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE But look here, some object with a certain peculiar form has come among the others and demands attention. Note this point specifically: It has come and demands attention. Never let it pass without attention. Conduct an interrogation at once: Who is this, where is it from and what does it want? You yourself will find the answers to these immediately, after a mere glance at this object that has presented itself. Let us suppose that it is a person who has offended you at some time. It is obvious that by engaging your attention with this person and the association you have with him, someone desires to create within you indignation, anger and even the desire for revenge. This is what will happen if you do not adopt the necessary measures. What measures should you adopt so as to drive away this idea? It wants to bring you to a bad state. Consequently, it is your enemy. You will treat it, then, in a hostile manner, that is, not with respect, but in the way which we spoke about last time, namely, with scornful rejection.

If you do this, you will completely thwart the enemy’s ruse or trap. If, however, you focus your attention on the person who has come to your imagination, then this idea, though ordinary and isolated at first, will become surrounded by a number of other thoughts and images that will paint a very vivid picture in your imagination as to how that person offended you, with all the details of the incident. Along with this, the previous feeling of offense, indignation and anger will arise in the heart. The passionate object or thought about it engendered the passionate feeling. The passionate became more entrenched. If you come to your senses and acknowledge that it is bad to allow yourself to be roused by passion, then, of course, you will see the enemy in this and treat it with hostility, after having expelled the wicked feeling from your heart and driven the very object that engendered it from your thoughts. The confusion will pass and peace of mind will be restored. 238

DEVELOPMENT OF PASSIONS If you do not do this, however, then other passionate feelings will gather around the one that has already arisen and exacerbate it. These feelings will begin rationalizing to you.

How dare he do that.^ Who does he think he is, someone important? I am no worse than he is. It is impossible to ignore this. I do not know how I let it slip by me. If I were to allow everyone to treat me like that, I could not live. I will certainly have to prove to him that he cannot act like that and get away with it.” Your desire for revenge, whether that vengeance is great or small, it is all the same, is at the ready. The passionate become even more deeply entrenched within. This is already the third stage. If you come to your senses at this point, you can drive off the desire. For the desire does not yet signify resolution. It arrived one moment, and it can leave the next. Ifyou take proper action, it will be for the better; if you do not, then your torment will continue further.

Observe how the thought engendered the feeling, and the thought with the feeling engendered the desire. The mind is filled with passion. All this, however, is still mental impurity and sinfulness. It is still a long way to the act. Between the desire and the act there is always the resolution to act, and premeditanon as to how to carry it out. How the resolution takes shape is not always apparent. It is already within the desire in a weak form; it then grows along with premeditation of the act, that is, with the selection of the means and establishment of methods and circumstances. W^hen everything has been premeditated, the resolution is complete. Then the act has already been accomplished within. The sin has already been committed in front of God and the conscience; the commandments have been disregarded, the conscience violated. Sometimes a lot of time passes between the desire and the resolution with the premeditation of the act. The fear of God takes hold, the commandments are remembered and the conscience is not silent. But all 239

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE of their saving suggestions are rebuffed by disregard. That is why there is already transgression and sin in the resolution. The thought, feeling and desire, although they have already occupied the entire soul, seemingly take place on the surface of the soul.
There is still no strong inclination toward sin, only an urge.
Strong inclination begins from the point when the soul begins deliberating whether it should satisfy the passion and how to do so. Here the soul has already set out on the path of sin.
While the resolution is forming, freedom is checked, the soul feels as if it were absolutely obligated to do what it intended.
But there is no such obligation. It is a sort of inner self-delusion that is taking place. What has been decided may remain unfulfilled because of some obstacle that is suddenly encountered.

Perhaps it is abandoned because the person himself has second thoughts about carrying it out, because of some consideration or because of the conscience and fear of God, if they should happen to rise up in full force.
At last, everything has been arranged and the deed done.

You got your revenge as planned. The passion has been satisfied, the sin finally committed. What more could be added to the resolution that developed at first? Seemingly nothing, for here the premeditated plan has only been carried out. That is how it seems; but, in the essence of the act, the wrongdoing increases up to the last step. First. Up to this point, the fear of God and the conscience were merely dismissed and disregarded, now they are violated. Up to now, the situation has been like a mother who is trying to persuade her son not to do something wrong, and he brushes her aside or runs away from her. Now the son answers his mother’s pleas by hitting her or slapping her in the face. Second. Now inner activity has been introduced into the course of outward events and is bound to be accompanied by the consequences, which are also outward and worldly. You will not cross it out of the circle of events by a stroke of the pen or 240
DEVELOPMENT OF PASSIONS verbal denial. It will remain there forever and will always be attached to the person who did it, forcing him to taste its fruits.
Third. The Divine grace deserts him, and the person leaves the realm of God and enters into the realm of the enemy of God.

He droops down, is burdened and feels himself crushed under a kind ofweight. The parable about the unclean spirit returning with seven more is fulfilled in him.^ Gloom, uneasiness, burden—this is the reward of any satisfied passion. Amazing! Until the passion is satisfied by the act, the person expects Paradise from its satisfaction: For you shull be us gods^ As soon as the passion is satisfied, however, the delusion falls from his eyes, the phantom dissipates, and there is left only emptiness, anguish, upset and burden; the person sees that he is impertinent. He took revenge on his enemy; he expected to be rejoicing, but things have turned out quite differently.

You see what the path is of tormenting yourself with some passion and falling into sin on account of it. Now we shall turn to you. What is possible in you from all that has been said.^ If your resolution to work for the Lord is sincere; if, having made this decision, you follow the advice given to remember God at all times with fear and reverence; if you watch after yourself strictly, then any inclination within you toward the satisfaction desire, and all that follows it, will be impossible.
However, thoughts, feelings and desires are possible. These are the objects of your inner struggle.

Passionate thought, feeling and desire sometimes pass through the soul in a single instant before we know what has happened. In this case they do not make us guilty, if as soon as we recover our senses at the appearance ofwhat is by now desire, we banish them through hostile anger toward them. Our guilt in the intention, feeling and desire is dependent on our procras1 . Matthew 12:43-45.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE tination; that is, after observing them we have failed to drive them away, and we dwell on them instead. Drive away the thought, and there will be no feeling or sympathy. Drive away the feeling with the intention, and there will be no desire. Drive away the desire, and there will be no danger of inclination toward the passionate. If, after observing a passionate intention, you dwell on it with attention voluntarily, you are guilty. Why did you occupy yourself with something that you know is God’s enemy and yours? If, however, your attention is involuntarily attracted to the intention, you are not guilty, as long as you immediately turn your attention away from it and drive it out.

If, as a result of your willful attention toward a passionate intention, a passionate feeling is engendered, then your guilt is increased a little. But if after detecting the birth of a passionate feeling, you continue to be occupied with the passionate intention, and, consequently, voluntarily provoke the feeling, then your guilt increases to twice what it was previously. If, after detecting a passionate feeling, you rouse yourself and drive it out along with the intention, then you would be guilty only in the fact that you voluntarily occupied yourself with a passionate thought; you are not guilty of the feeling, because such feelings are created through the action of the thoughts. If through the action of the passionate thought and feeling that have been voluntarily allowed, there is born within you the desire for a passionate action (for example, revenge), then your guilt is increased a little, because desire is created involuntarily from thought and feeling. Your previous guilt remains; why were you occupied with the passionate object, and when a passionate feeling was born from this, why did you allow the feeling to persist and the thought that gave rise to it? Therefore, your guilt is twofold. But if after detecting the passionate desire, you allow it to linger inside you, and do not immediately arm yourself 242
DEVELOPMENT OF PASSIONS against it, then your guilt increases by one more step; it will be threefold.
I will not go further, because I suppose this is sufficient to warn you.
You see for yourself that if you immediately drive away the passionate thought, then you put an end to the entire struggle.

There will be neither feeling, and even more, there will be no desire. Make the decision to act in this way. Why would you draw on yourself the unnecessary labor of struggle, and sometimes even danger, when you have already decided not to allow the passionate as being ungodly? If the feeling begins to stir involuntarily along with the thought, immediately chase out the feeling with the thought. If desire involuntarily clings to them, immediately chase out the desire along with them. The moment you discover a passionate impulse within yourself, chase it out.

Make this your rule: Do not voluntarily praise either passionate thought, feeling or desire, and immediately chase them away with complete hatred as soon as you detect them. You will always be innocent before God and before your own conscience.
There will be within you the impurity of the passions, but also innocence. You will carry out the act ofpurification (cleansing), laboring diligently at the purifying of your soul.
PRAYER IN THE WARFARE WITH PASSIONATE THOUGHTS The significance ofprayer in the matter ofthe warfare with passionate thoughts. Examples.

Last time I described for you the entire process of how one goes from the simple thought to the passionate desire, and from there to the act. However, the course of action is not always as drawn out as depicted on paper. Frequently, if not most of the time, all the stages go by very quickly, and no sooner does the thought appear than the act takes place, especially the spoken word. There is no need whatsoever to sort out and distinguish all these accidental occurrences. This will not give you any help in the struggle with the passions. Keep to one thing: When the passionate is detected, immediately arm yourself against it with wrath and hostile anger. This anger in spiritual warfare has the very same significance as when an evil person is assaulting you; you have to strike back at him hard. But just as striking back at an evil person during an assault does not always result in his running away, so too in spiritual warfare, the anger at the passionate thought, feeling, and desire does not always drive them from the soul and end the struggle with them. In the natural order of things, it would seem that this is how it should be, and it happens, but not always. This is because the demons often participate in the agitation of thought, and they are shameless. No matter how angry you get at them, they are all there. It is obvious that besides anger, it is necessary to resort to something else. What could that be? 244
WARFARE WITH PASSIONATE THOUGHTS What does the victim of an assault by an evil person do?

After striking the person, he calls for help. The watchmen answer his call and save him from trouble. This is also what must be done in spiritual warfare with the passions. Once you have become angry at the passion, you have to call out for help: Lordy help me! Lord Jesus Christy Son of Gody save me! O Lordy be attentive unto my helpy make haste to help me! ^ When appealing to the Lord in this manner, do not fall away from Him through attention toward what is going on inside of you, but keep standing before the Lord, imploring Him for help. Run at once from this enemy as from a blazing fire. One of the saints said, “Destroy the enemy in the name of the Lord Jesus.” No one can resist the Lord, for He is close by: The Lord is nigh unto all them that call upon LLimy to all that call upon Him in truth. He will fulfill the desire of them that fear Him: He also will hear their prayery and will save them. He Himself gave such a promise to those who call upon Him in hope in their hour of need: Because he hath set his hope upon mey therefore will 1 deliver him: I will set him on highy because he hath known my name. He shall call upon mCy and I will answer him: I will be with him in trouble; I will deliver himy and honor him? Do not forget to do this, and you will always successfully engage and drive out all passions that arise within you.

There is something others have done, and perhaps do even now. That is, once they have detected the passionate and become filled with indignation toward it, they begin exposing Its obscenity. For example, if the thought of pride has occurred, they begin reciting, “Pride is contrary to God; you who are earth and ash, are you not ashamed to become puffed up with this

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE thought; remember your sins,” and the like. They select thoughts against pride, supposing that by this they will drive out the prideful thought. Sometimes they drive it out, but this method is for the most part unreliable. By exposing the passionate thought, we are still keeping it in our mind, and in the meantime it stirs up feeling and arouses desire; that is, it continues to defile our soul. Whenever we appeal directly to the Lord with fear, reverence, hope and faith in His complete activity without entering into a verbal battle with the passionate, the passionate then moves away from the mind’s eye, which is fixed on the Lord. When it is cut off from the mind through such attention, the passionate departs of its own accord, if it has been naturally stimulated. If the enemy is involved, however, then a discerning ray of light that comes from contemplation of the Lord strikes him. It happens that the mind immediately calms down from passionate violations as soon as it turns to the Lord and calls on Him.

To make this clearer to you, I will tell you a legend. An elder lived in the desert of silence. The demons visibly attacked him, and began dragging him out of his cell so as to completely drive him out of the desert. The elder himself began fighting back at them, but they moved him and had already dragged him right up to the very door. Just a bit more, and they would have turned him out. Seeing his extreme danger, the elder called out, “Lord Jesus Christ! Why have you abandoned me? Help me. Lord!” As soon as he called out, the Lord appeared immediately and chased away the demons, and said to the elder, “I did not abandon you, but because you did not call on me and thought you could cope with the demons yourself, I did not come to your help. Call on Me, and you will always receive ready help.” After saying this, the Lord disappeared. This incident is a lesson not just to the elder, but to all of us: Do not struggle with passionate thoughts through your own spiritual altercations 246
WARFARE WITH PASSIONATE THOUGHTS them, but turn immediately to the Lord with prayer against them.

All who prudently conduct spiritual warfare with the passions act in this way. John the Dwarf said of himself, “I act as a man sitting under a tree who looks attentively around him. This man, as soon as he sees wild beasts coming toward him to eat him, immediately climbs up the tree, and the beasts, after coming up to the tree, walk around it for awhile and go away.

And I, as soon as I observe mental beasts coming toward me in passionate thoughts, immediately rise up in my mind to the Lord, and the beasts cannot get to me as they are forced to scatter every which way.”^ You will recall that I wrote you previously about the necessity for strengthening oneself through the attention of the mind in the heart, and there incessantly rising up and calling to the Lord. This is the same thing we are talking about now; that is, one must appeal to the Lord with prayer when passionate thoughts, feelings or desires arise. One must appeal to the Lord, going down with the attention of the mind into the heart and calling out to Him from there. If we were to carry out the following little rule unfailingly, that is, after strengthening ourselves through the mind in the heart and then standing before the Lord with fear, reverence and faith, then not only would passionate desires and feelings never arise within us, but neither would idle thoughts. It is our misfortune, however, that we mentally fall away from the Lord out of the heart and wander with our thoughts into a far country. It is at this time that the passionate thoughts invade, and before you know it, the passionate feelings have already commenced, and likewise the 1 . THE SPIRITUAL LIFE desires have begun to stir. You are in for a struggle! And who is to blame?! If you did not allow the thoughts to wander, there would be no battle. Let us recall, however, that, after moving away from God in our heart and having thus brought disaster upon ourselves, at least now, once the misfortune has been detected, we should rush to seek shelter once again in the heart before the Lord, so that we may call out to Him for help.

This same John the Dwarf told the following parable on this subject: “There was in a certain place a beautiful woman of questionable behavior. The ruler of this country took pity on her, that such beauty would perish, and, when he found the opportunity, he said to her, ‘Give up your immoral ways, and I will take you to my house and you will become my wife and the mistress of many treasures. Just watch that you are faithful, or else there will be such trouble for you as you cannot even imagine.' She agreed to this, and was taken to the ruler’s house.
Her former friends, seeing that she had disappeared, began searching for her, and found out that she was with the ruler.

Although the ruler was a terror, they did not despair of enticing the beautiful woman back to themselves once again, knowing her weakness. ‘YC^e have only to go up behind the house and whistle; she will know who it is and immediately run out to us.’ That is just what they did. They went up behind the house and whistled. The beautiful woman, hearing the whistle, started.

Something from her previous life stirred inside of her. But she had already come to her senses, and instead of running out of the house, she rushed into the inner chambers to the ruler himself, and immediately calmed down; she did not even hear the whistling that continued outside. Her friends whistled a few more times, and went off with nothing. The meaning of the parable is clear. The beautiful woman represents the fallen soul that has turned to the Lord in repentance and made a contract to belong to and serve Him Alone. The former friends are the 248

WARFARE WITH THE PASSIONS passions. Their whistling is the impulse of passionate thoughts, feelings and desires. Escape into the inner chambers is shelter in the depths of the heart, there to stand before the Lord. When this is accomplished within, the passion that has troubled the soul leaves of its own accord as if it had never existed, and the soul calms down.”^ Resolve to learn this story by heart and always act according to its meaning. You will see how quickly inner peace that has been disturbed by the appearance of the passions is restored within you.
May the grace of God be with you! Be saved! 59 ST. HESYCHIUS ON WARFARE WITH THE PASSIONS Citation from St. Hesychius on warfare with the passions.

You have been told almost everything about spiritual warfare now. There are many passions; but no matter how many there are, all make their approach in the manner shown, and all are driven out and overcome as we have said. Each passion, of course, has its own peculiarities that accompany it when it is aroused; there are also a very great number of contingencies in the mode of their appearance. This, however, does not change the general methods for combating them. Do as you have been told, and you will successfully drive out every passion, no matter how forcefully it has arisen within you.

So that you may better remember all this and be persuaded that you must act in no other way, I am providing an excerpt 1 . This story may be found in The Sayings ofthe Desert Fathers, trans. by Benedicta Ward, SLG (Oxford: Mowbray & Co. Ltd., 1981), pp.88-89. 249

This is what he says: ^^One who is engaged in the spiritual struggle must have at every moment the following four things: humility, extreme attentiveness, refutation (of the thoughts) and prayer. Humility, in so far as its adversaries in combat are the demons of pride, so that he will have at hand Christ s help in his heart, for ‘the Lord resists the proud. Attentiveness, so that one does not allow the heart to have any thought, no matter how good it seems. Denial, so that as soon as one has detected a thought that has come, he may repulse it immediately with anger. Prayer, so that after refuting a thought, one may immediately cry out to Christ with ‘groanings which cannot be uttered.’^ Then this ascetic will see the enemy bound or chased by the honorable name of Jesus, like dust by the wind, or like smoke that vanishes with its dreams.” (section 20) Here is a similar passage: “It is necessary to act the same way in spiritual warfare as one acts in (conventional) war. “The great lawgiver Moses, or better, the Holy Spirit, said through him. Take heed that there be not a secret thought in your heart, an iniquity’ (Deuteronomy 15:9). THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and establish in the mind, so as to entice it through the imagination, (section 6) “The thought standing (in the heart) and calling upon Christ against its enemies and running to Him is like a wild beast surrounded by many dogs and protected in a stronghold.

It perceives intellectually from afar the intellectual ambushes of the invisible foes, and praying unceasingly to the peace-making Jesus against them, remains unharmed by them, (section 8) “One who does not have prayer that is free from the thoughts is without a weapon for battle. I understand prayer to be that which is carried out unceasingly within the depths of the soul, so that the enemy who is secretly fighting may be vanquished and scorched by this invocation to Christ. For you must look with the sharply focused eye of the mind so that you will recognize what has entered into it, and after doing so, immediately cut off the head of the snake through refutation, and at the same time call on Christ with groaning. Through experience you will come to know God’s invisible help; then you will see clearly the true condition of the heart, (sections 21, 22) “Thus, the soul that makes bold concerning Christ invokes Him and is not in the least afraid, does not fight alone, but along with the fearful King, Jesus Christ, Creator of all that exists, corporeal and incorporeal, visible and invisible.” (section 40) I will limit myself to these excerpts, because if I were to copy out everything that is relevant here, it would be necessary to write out half the book. I wrote down these things to remind you what I have been telling you for a long time concerning remembrance of God and the inner struggle with the passions.
Know that in this is the entire essence of the labor over the attainment of salvation.
After reading this, do not say to yourself, “Who am I to observe such rules, some sort of hermit in the desert?” You are not alone in asking this; there are many, many others. The only 252

CLEANSING THE HEART ones who do not ask are those who, although they have the most cause to be concerned for their salvation, do not give it any thought. Those who have begun this labor cannot talk like this.

You, too, must act in the way prescribed immediately if you wish to live the spiritual life in which lies the attainment of salvation. If you want to live in a negligent manner, then of course nothing will come to you. I began writing about all this in the belief that your decision to belong to the Lord was sincere.

A person whose decision is sincere cannot bypass the path prescribed. He may perform great labors and get around things in various ways, but until he comes upon this path, it is to no purpose. I am pointing you directly to the path so that you do not wander all over the place. Be more diligent in your undertaking, and you will soon see success. However, you must labor with all your might, because without labor there will be nothing.

Do not tolerate such worldly wisdom. The desert includes those living in a monastery or in the world who are attaining their salvation. For everyone the most important rule is to cleanse one’s heart of the passions; one cannot cleanse it in any way other than that described. Do not give up at all and do not resist.
CLEANSING THE HEART How to act ifduring warfare with the passions passionate thoughts andfeelings erupt within us.

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE I suppose that you have set about things as you are supposed to, and that you diligently exert yourself to remember the Lord at all times, attend to your heart, and pursue any wrong impulse with a hostile rebuff, all the while appealing to the Lord in prayer for help. At the same time, however, I cannot in any way suppose your success to have been complete as soon as you began, that your thoughts would be pure, and feelings and desires holy. This is because it never happens like this to anybody. The thoughts will be pacified, the passionate impulses of feelings and desires will become less frequent. All the same, however, they will erupt, and sometimes with great force.

When they erupt, turn them out; when they erupt again, turn them out again. But this is already assumed. I just want to tell you now how you must react to lapses in judgment of the conscience. Should you react indifferently, or grieve over it and confess to the Lord? One should not react indifferently to these lapses, but each time after expelling them, one must grieve and confess to the Lord what happened. Even then, when passionate thoughts, feelings and desires suddenly erupt, without any participation of the assent, they signify all the same that the heart is impure and that out of it proceed evil thoughts (Matt. 15:19).

And you are obliged to have a pure heart. Therefore you should be sorry that your heart is impure, and confess this impurity to the Lord, promising to toil over its impurity and praying to Him for help in this. But the assent is in fact frequently involved here.
Because of your inattentiveness, you gave place to a passionate thought, and it succeeded in giving rise to a passionate feeling.

You are guilty; why didn’t you pay attention? Because of this inattentiveness and also because of the pleasantness of the feeling, you did not rush to arm yourself against it and drive it away, and it succeeded in giving rise to passionate desire. You are guilty; why did you allow the sinful and passionate enticement instead of arming yourself immediately? That you permit2-54

CLEANSING THE HEART ted the desire to give rise to the inclination to act, I cannot tolerate, because you may still allow the desire to persist and not arm yourself against it immediately. This makes you even more guilty. Thus, permitting the evil thought is one charge; allowing the arousal of evil feeling from it is another; permitting the engendering of evil desire from this evil feeling is the third charge; allowing evil desire to linger is the fourth charge. How many charges of guilt!

Do not try to conceal yourself, covering your nakedness with a fig leaf and hiding in the bushes from the Lord, who comes to you in the conscience and exposes you. Blame yourself completely, and ask forgiveness without placing blame on anyone else. Let us say that you got angry at the maid, and that you spoke to her with sharp words. Do not blame the maid. Even if she blundered, that is not the point, but that you got angry with her. Could you have not corrected her mistake without getting angry? Even if it is not possible to correct something, everything in general may be settled without anger. Here anger erupted, then blame, then envy, then conceit. For all of this, of course, there were external causes. It is not the causes that are to blame, however, but you, that you allowed such evil feelings. Try without concealing yourself to confess directly to the Lord and ask forgiveness. Do this each time when, having detecting an evil, you drive it out. You will make your peace with the Lord through this repentance and contrition, and through your conscience you will calmly look at the Lord again. But if you do not repent, you will not be able to look at Him in such a way; it would even be difficult and dangerous for you.

Do not put off repentance of these eruptions of the passions until evening , but cleanse yourself of them immediately, as soon as you have driven them away, in the name of the Lord. You will spend the entire day in repentance, because at first these eruptions will be frequent inside you. In the evening before 255

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE prayer, review once again all the passionate eruptions in turn, grieve over them again with contrition, and repent. This is the act of daily repentance. Here again is the teaching of St. Hesychius for you, “At every hour we must weigh our daily affairs attentively, and as much as we are able, without fail lighten their burden through repentance, if we want to overcome the passions with the help of Jesus. It is also necessary to examine whether we carry out all our outward actions through God’s will, before God and for God Alone, so that the feelings (passionate) do not deceive us like foolish people.” (section 124) If you act decisively and without self-pity as prescribed, you will soon see the fruit: Your heart will become tranquil, and joy in the Lord will begin to shine in it. Why is the heart troubled?

Because the passions gnaw at it. Kill the passions, and it will receive peace. One of the Fathers likens the heart to a pit filled with snakes. These snakes are the passions. When a passion shows itself from the heart, it is like a snake thrusting its head out of the pit. Strike at its head in the name of the Lord, and it will recoil. When another shows itself, strike it, too. Strike down each one. You will have to strike dozens of blows at this snake, the passions, before it forgets to recoil and is completely killed off. However, the snakes will also die if you block up the snake pit or do not feed them. Likewise the passions will die out if you do not feed them with sympathy for their suggestions, but poison them instead with anger as soon as they appear.

This is the shortest path for you to purifying the heart of the passions. If you have come to love this purity, rush to it by this path, for there is no other. If you do not agree to act in this way, the passions will remain in the heart. You may correct your behavior and make it irreproachable without it, but your heart will remain passionate, and will not allow you to see the Lord.
GUARDING THE HEARING AND SIGHT The guarding ofthe hearing and sight. How to destroy bad impressions ofwhat one has seen and heard.
I mentioned to you last time how the passionate thoughts, feelings and desires are aroused, and how you must not shift the blame onto them. Now I will tell you a little about them.

Passionate thoughts, followed by passionate feelings and desires, sometimes arise within the soul on their own, in some unknown way. For the most part, however, they are engendered by the influence of outward stimuli. The things, people and events that a person encounters excite ideas in him in accordance with their nature. Good thoughts are engendered from the good, while evil thoughts are engendered from the bad. Everything depends on the permanent or accidental state of the person who encounters them. Consequently, the answer also lies entirely with him. How can this be.^ First. Do not give free reign to your senses, especially the eyes and ears. Do not allow them to see everything, hear everything and be concerned with everything indiscriminately.

Your senses are like windows or doors, or better, like a pail. A person who opens the windows and lets in bad air is doing wrong. A person who opens the doors and lets all kinds of livestock come into his house is going to be reprimanded. But what would you say if someone were to take a pail (or a cup or mug) and go to a dirty, unclean puddle and draw from it and pour it over himself? Can you think of anything more stupid?
And yet, isn’t someone doing the same thing when he stops in front of some bad person out of curiosity and listens to profane 257

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE words? A smattering of evil thoughts is picked up through this; these turn into evil feelings and desires, and the person walks around distressed, as if in some sort of fog. Thus, prudence and the responsibility for maintaining one’s inner peace require that one not look at everything, listen to everything or concern himselfwith everything that he happens to come across. A thing will hardly show that it is capable of rousing the passions, so it is necessary to turn the eyes away from it, plug up the ears in front of it, or seeing do not see and hearing do not hear.

Second. Evil thoughts have been engendered from some evil stimulus. It is necessary to rush immediately to blot out the stimulus and suppress the thoughts using the method shown earlier, after the flow of stimuli has been stopped, of course. It would be foolish to remain under the influence of the bad stimulus, or to postpone blotting it out and restoring peace of mind until another time. By remaining under the influence of the stimulus willingly, we encourage the breeding of evil thoughts and feelings. By leaving the evil within ourself for a long time, we give it the opportunity to become more deeply implanted and then further oppose expulsion and cleansing, if not to take complete possession of the mind.

Third. Once a person has experienced harm from some stimulus, he should not willingly allow himself to encounter the objects that caused it again. This would signify that the person acting in this way delights in the evil thing, and consequently is impure to the bottom of the heart. If some necessity should force him to encounter it again, then he must arm himself ahead of time and prepare his heart to resist the evil stimulus and not allow it to enter himself. Attentiveness, increased resistance on the person’s part and prayer will make this possible.
Fourth. I already wrote you about reinterpreting everything you encounter and that you might encounter in the spiritual sense. It is necessary to do this also with respect to objects of evil 258

GUARDING THE HEARING AND SIGHT significance. After encountering them, you will not have bad thoughts, but good ones instead, in the same way when St. Ephraim the Syrian, having encountered an enticingly dressed woman, told his disciples, ^^You see how she takes care to adorn her body, which will soon be dust; how can we not be concerned with adorning our immortal soul?” These rules are sufficient. It would be proper to indicate those contingencies in your everyday life that are harmful to your inner life. But I suppose that there are none, either in your immediate family or relatives. You would not come across them in any way except outside the home, for example, in the society in which you spend little time, as you wrote. With regard to such contingencies, keep to what was written in the third point.

Then place all similar things you encounter in the future under this rule. Make it a general rule to measure what is permissible by the influence that it exerts inwardly. You may permit yourself that which is constructive; do not permit that which disturbs in any form. Who in his right mind would knowingly drink from a glass into which poison has been poured?!

I do not have in mind that you restrict yourself and become a recluse. I just want to guide you toward the decision to not take in what is harmful for your spirit, since you determined that the only true life is that in the spirit, and have also experienced this.

ACTIVE WARFARE WITH THE PASSIONS After guidance in noetic warfare with the passions instruction ts offered about active warfare with them. The most drastic external way oflife for battle with the passions. Conclusion ofthe discussion about warfare with the passions.

With regard to guidance for the cleansing of the passions, what has been said is sufficient, because they are within you to such a small degree. As we have already begun talking about it, however, I will add a little more which you may find useful right now, not at some point in the future.

The warfare prescribed with the passions is spiritual. It is real, because by not permitting the passions to feed on something, it exhausts them. But there is also active warfare with the passions that consists in the deliberate undertaking and carrying out of actions that are directly opposed to those passions. For example, to suppress stinginess, it is necessary to become generous; to combat pride, it is necessary to choose humbling occupations; to combat carousing, one must stay at home; and the like. It is true that one such mode of action does not lead directly to the goal, because the passion, enduring external constraint, may erupt inwardly—either that same passion, or some other in its place. When inner and spiritual warfare are united with this active combat, however, they soon defeat any passion against which they are directed together.

So it is that I offer you advice. Work at finding your primary passion and direct active as well as spiritual warfare against it. I cannot tell just what your primary passion is. Maybe it is as yet 260
ACTIVE WARFARE undefined; nevertheless, if you begin looking after the heart’s impulses more rigorously, it will make itself known to you.
Then, if you care to trust me, we will decide together how to cope with your main weakness in the quickest possible way.

I would also remind you that there are drastic methods of active warfare with the passions. There is the life of worldly renunciation, where a person who has decided to give up everything and who has placed all hope in the Lord enters a monastery to live in humble obedience, strict abstinence, diligent prayer and cheerful sobriety . There is nothing lacking here; the cutting off of one s will and not feeling sorry for one’s self soon threshes all that is passionate out of the soul. Then a peaceful disposition of the heart and purity of the heart, the ultimate goals of the life of renunciation, are established. I am not driving you away from the world. You can be saved in it, if you conduct yourself with respect to it as if you were not of it.
I am bringing this up as a way of life that is conducive to the cleansing of the passions from the heart.

I would also add that one of the laws of Divine Economy concerning us is the arrangement of each person’s life and the course of events in it so that the person, making wise use of them, may cleanse himself of the passions in the quickest and most convenient way. I am reminding you of this because when you asked why people have different fates and why these fates are so variable, it seems I did not answer you. So now I will answer you why. It is because the Lord arranges the most convenient means for people to be cleansed of passions, or because He wants in this way to grind to dust the squalid selfishness and stubborn egoism which maintain the passions that destroy a person.
I would add one more thing. Kill the passions both inwardly and externally, and cultivate your good aspects, giving them full range and exercise. The main thing is prayer. We have already 261

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE spoken of this. After this follows the labor of good deeds. We spoke about this when you were contemplating some of the broader goals of life. Along with this, spiritually beneficial reading and conversation must take place; prayers and daily Scripture readings must be carried out as well as deeds of self-mortification—renounce yourself when it is necessary, be sparing in food, sleep, entertainment and in all the pleasures of self-amusement, and conduct yourself in continual self-denial and self-restraint. We have also spoken about this.
Here is the entire course of cleansing oneself of the passions.
You already have all of this under way. May God speed.
SINGING AND MUSIG Cares around the house. Singing and music.

I hurried to bring our discussion of the struggle with the passions to an end a little sooner—although it is impossible to get to the end of it—and I left much of what you wrote during this time almost without a reply. You will find the answer to some things in what has been prescribed for you, and I will answer the other things now.
You write that you have a lot of troubles around the house.
Do everything you are told with diligence. This is your duty.

But busy yourself, do not be troubled. Troubles (cares) are accompanied by agitation of everything within, and this is something that is not proper. Perhaps it is impossible to consider each errand calmly and then go do it, not in the least disturbed, with your thoughts collected. In everyday life, if there is something hindering spiritual life, then it is not an activity or occupation at all, but a vain, intricate care that gnaws at the 262

SINGING AND MUSIC heart and at the same time is not capable of advancing (spiritual) activity forward by even a hair. These arousals, in stirring up the thoughts, are like a whirlwind. A hubbub always results, and spiritual activity gets nowhere at all. Distinguish, if you will, proper zeal from improper care and worries, and practice doing all activities soberly, without deviating from the Lord in thought, but on the contrary, maintaining the conviction that you are pleasing Him by all this.

You ask, “How should one be with regard to singing and playing on the piano and harmonium? I used to sing and play without paying attention to the content of the pieces, and now it is on my mind. As you certainly know, not all pieces have good content, although they may have a nice melody.” What should you do? It is impossible for you to change everything at once. This is immediately obvious. Sing and play as you used to, trying not to pay attention to the content. In the meantime, start selecting songs with good or perhaps just tolerable content, and sing and play these more, until finally you completely forget the others that make up your singing and playing. The harmonium is very capable of expressing church hymns in the necessary manner. Get the compositions of Turchaninov, and learn the ones you like best.

Learn “God Save the Tsar,” “How Glorious is our Lord in Sion,” and the like. If you sing these with complete feeling, I assure you that you will like this sort of singing better than the other. You will then be asked to sing and play not some frivolous little songs, but these majestic compositions. Some time ago, you said that you receive compliments on your voice and singing. They do what they do, and you do what you do. When both things are good, it would be impossible not to praise you.
But you should not pride yourself on what people say, you should be humble. Where did your voice and taste for singing come from? God gave these to you. Why did God give them? 263
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE To stir up rumors among other people, or for some other reason?
For no other reason than that you turn them to the praise of God. Have you done this? No. You have only amused yourself and others, but you have made no mention of God at all.

Therefore, you have misused your Divine gift, squandering it on frivolous things. Now the thoughts that you turned toward pleasing God should also be turned toward using your Divine gift. If one is to eat and drink to the glory of God, the more so should one sing and play music. If you were to play and sing something that made an impression on the souls of the listeners and caused them to be inspired toward the Lord and rise up to Him with praise and thanksgiving, you would be doing the same thing that a good preacher does in church. This is the fruit of your gift! Direct, as you will, the use of it toward this. Select both secular and religious songs that are edifying and sing and play only these. This is for other people; you should never sing secular songs for yourself, just religious ones. I repeat again, do not change everything suddenly, but gradually.
In general, do not show idiosyncrasies in anything. Be polite, good-humored and cheerful with everyone, as always.

Just refrain from laughter, merrymaking and any kind of idle speech. Even without this it is possible to be polite and cheerful and agreeable. Never be gloomy in any way. When the Savior told those who are fasting to smile, anoint their heads and wash their faces. He meant that they should not be gloomy.
SOLITUDE Solitude. How to avoid boredom.

You write, “I am left alone and I am bored.” This is a natural feeling. But it seems to me that you should not feel this way after the decisions you made and what you were told to do in consequence of this. If the latter took root in you at all, then you would never have realized that you were alone, even though you are by yourself. You would not have realized you were alone, because you would have realized that the Lord is close by, as is your Guardian Angel—not in thought, but in actuality. Then the feeling of loneliness would have seemed out of place, and consequently, the boredom that follows. I suppose, however, that this incursion of boredom was momentary for you, and quickly dissipated.

Always, however, in the future, each time you find yourself alone, renew as quickly as possible the conviction that the Lord and your Guardian Angel are with you, and rush to take advantage of these chance moments of solitude for undistracted sojourning with the Lord and sweet conversation with Him.
Solitude in this spirit is sweet. I hope you will taste this sweetness sometime, so that you will desire it as Paradise on earth.

Recently, while looking through a book, I came across the advice of a father (Count Speransky) to his daughter. Among other things, he tells how to avoid boredom. This is what he advises: everyone, he says, has a number of daily chores, which they work off like some sort of quitrent. There are many people. 265

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE however, for whom these quitrent chores are simple and do not take much time. There is a lot of time left over whereby, if it is not filled with anything, there is no way to avoid boredom. Here is the most reliable way for you to avoid it: Arrange things so you do not have a single idle moment and all your time is filled with suitable occupations so that, upon completion of one activity, you have another one ready to begin.

What kind of activities should these be? 1) Aesthetic occupations: music, singing, painting. 2) Some sort of handicraft: knitting, needlework and the like. 3) The best remedy for boredom, however, is to acquire a taste for serious reading and the study of subjects that you are unfamiliar with. It is not so much the reading that drives away boredom as the study.

I was going to tell you this recipe against boredom just in case, but then the need for it arose. Take it into consideration and get organized in this way. You truly will not experience boredom anymore. I know a certain person who is always alone.

He never goes out himself, and never receives any guests. When you ask him if he ever gets bored, he answers, “I have no time, because there is so much to do. As soon as I wake up, I start doing things, and never manage to get everything done by bedtime.” I would note for you that the study Count Speransky is referring to is the study of entire sciences, or certain parts of them. By this, it is obvious that one avoids any reading of frivolous books. It seems that you do not like to do this anyway.

That is good. Do not give up this habit. Read more the spiritual books (than scientific ones). This is the sphere of the most serious subjects, and, most importantly, the most necessary. In this sphere everything is new and never becomes obsolete. The more you learn, the more you will discover subjects that are as yet unfamiliar. The desire to learn these subjects and the predisposition to their cognition occurs only when one in fact sets out 266

THE GOD-PLEASING LIFE on the path of spiritual life. I consider that from the moment you decided to please the Lord, you have been no stranger to this path. Read then, as you like, books on these subjects, and become wise. May the Lord bless you!
ST. POEMEN ON THE GOD-PLEASING LIFE A passagefrom St. Poemen about the God-pleasing life, the conquest ofthe passions and the sowing ofthe virtues.

Who got you interested in St. Poemen so that you want to know more about him? No matter who it was, I am very glad of it. You will find who St. Poemen was and how he lived in the Menaion under August 27, and also in the book Sayings Concerning the Ascetic Deeds of the Saints and Blessed Fathers. You will find a number of his sayings in these places. He was from a simple family and was uneducated, but the experiences of spiritual life and grace of God so enlightened his mind that because of his astute, precise knowledge of the laws of the rising up to perfection of the spirit, it is fitting to place him among the first of the instructive Fathers wise in the ways of God.
Until you get to reading about him, I will write down for you the most important points from his teaching and sayings that relate to what I have been explaining to you for a long time.
Repentance and grieving over one’s sins is the beginning of the Divine path. This is what you encounter in St. Poemen.

He saw a woman who was sitting on a grave and weeping bitterly, and he said, “If all the pleasures of the world were to appear here, they would not free her soul from grief We too must continually grieve thus.” (saying 26) The 72nd saying also recounts a similar situation, and the words of St. Poemen are 267

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE quoted in this way, “I assure you that if man does not do away with all fleshly desires and does not thus weep, he cannot become one with God. This woman’s entire soul and life were absorbed in grief” A person who repents and grieves over his sins naturally shuns evil and does good. Until he is this way, he may not undertake to do evil by deed, but he cannot be free from wicked thoughts. That is why his shunning of evil consists almost entirely in struggle with the thoughts. This is what St. Poemen talks about.

One of the brothers went to Abba Poemen and said to him, “Father! I have many thoughts, and I am in danger because of them.” The old man took him outside and said, “Expand your chest and do not inhale!” “I cannot do that,” answered the brother. “If you cannot do that, then neither can you stem the flow of the thoughts,” said the old man. “But your job is to resist them.” (saying 28) Your job is to resist them, but how? First of all, be attentive to yourself and be sober. A brother said that when he was wi th others, he would amuse himself and return to his cell not the same as when he left it. He asked how he should act. The elder told him, “When you return to your cell, do you want to find yourself the same as when you left? Maintain vigilance over yourself both at home and outside the home. ”(137) “In general,” Abba Poemen would say, “the thing you need most is a sober mind.” (135) When maintaining vigilance over yourself, as soon as you observe the passionate, immediately pray and it will go away. A brother asked Abba Poemen about the struggle with rebellious thoughts, and the Elder answered him, “This situation is like a man who has fire on one side, and a cup of water on the other.
When the fire begins to scorch, he takes water from the cup and 268

THE GOD-PLEASING LIFE puts it out. Fire is the suggestion of the enemy (the passions), while water is earnest prayer to God.” (146) To suffer less from passionate thoughts, it is necessary to remove oneself from occasions that arouse them. One must remove himself from everything passionate, said Abba Poemen.

A man who is close to something that could engender passion is like someone standing over a deep precipice into which the man’s enemy may easily throw him. But the man who removes himself from that which is capable of arousing passion is like someone standing far from the precipice. Let the enemy drag him so as to toss him into the abyss, but while he is dragging him along by force, the man will call out to God and God will help him. (59) The most important thing is to try in every way possible not to give in to the thoughts. For encouraging this, we read the following in the sayings about St. Poemen, “A brother asked him whether a person could always suppress his thoughts without yielding to the enemy in a single one?” The elder answered, ‘There are those who suppress ten but yield one.’ That very same brother asked the same thing of Abba Sisoes, and he said, ‘Indeed, there are those who yield nothing to the enemy.’” (88, 89) Another: Abba Anoub asked Abba Poemen about impure thoughts that are engendered in the human heart and about vain desires. Abba Poemen answered him. Shall the axe boast itself against him that heweth therewith? (Isaiah 10:15) Do not give them a handy and they will not do anything (15) What then will come of this unyielding? The passions will die away. That is what Abba Poemen told Abba Isaiah when the latter asked him about impure thoughts. He said, “If a trunk full of clothing is not looked after, then in time the clothing will disintegrate. So too will the thoughts, if we do not in fact carry them out, vanish in time, as if they disintegrated.” (20) 269

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Abba Joseph asked Abba Poemen about the same thing, and he said, “If someone were to place a snake and a scorpion in a jar and close it up, then in time, of course, the creatures would suffocate. So too will bad thoughts that come from the demons die away, if we resist them patiently and do not feed them.” (21) However, in shunning evil through such ruthless opposition to passionate thoughts and desires, one must at the same time do good, cultivating every sort ofvirtue within oneself Through both these things the heart will soon be purified. There are many lessons in St. Poemen about cultivating virtue. Here are the most important ones.

He quotes a saying of Abba John the Dwarf, who said, “I desire to possess every virtue, if only to a small degree.” (46) Further on he offers his own lesson about this, saying, “When a man decides to build a house, he gathers many different materials for construction. So too must we acquire all the virtues, if only to a small degree.” (130) There are, however, virtues that are fundamental and provide guidance, and that is why one must direct all efforts toward them. St. Poemen often indicates which ones these are. For example: “Maintaining self-vigilance, being attentive to one’s self, and discernment are the three virtues that guide the soul. (35) “Fall down before God, humble yourself and reject your own will; these are the working tools of the soul!’’ (36) “In the Scriptures it is written, ‘Though these three men, Noah, Daniel and Job, were in it, as I live, they shall deliver but their own souls by their righteousness, saith the Lord God ’’’(Ezekiel 1 4: 1 4 and 20). “Noah represents poverty; Job, patience; Daniel, discernment. If a man has these three virtues, then the Lord dwells in him.” (60) “The three fundamental virtues are the fear of God, prayer and charity toward one’s neighbor.” (160) 270

THE GOD-PLEASING LIFE Abba Poemen had a layman tell the brothers the following, I do not know how to speak from the Scriptures, so I will tell you a parable. A man said to his friend, ‘I want to go see the king. Come with me.’ The friend answered him, ‘I will go with you half-way.’ He said to another friend, ‘Take me to the king.’ He said, I will take you as far as the king’s palace.’ The man said to a third person, ‘Come with me to the king.’ ‘Let’s go,’ replied the third friend, I will go with you to the king’s palace, bring you inside, announce your arrival and present you to him.’ Abba Poemen asked the layman, ‘What does this parable mean?’ He replied, ‘The first friend is asceticism, which leads you to the true path; the second is purity, which takes you to Heaven; the third friend is almsgiving, which will fearlessly bring you to the King Himself, God.’” (109) When, in this way, you do not yield to the passions on the one hand, and you cultivate the virtues on the other hand, the heart gradually softens (182), warms up and receives within itself the Divine flame, which you have only to look after to be safe. Abba Poemen says concerning this, “When a pot is warmed by a fire, then no fly or other creature can touch it; but when it becomes cold, then they perch on it. It is the same way with the soul: So long as it is in a state of spiritual activity (enthusiasm of the spirit for God), the enemy cannot affect it.” (Ill) These excerpts are sufficient for you. You will find for yourself the other things that are necessary and valuable for you in St. Poemen. I just wanted to use these excerpts to refresh your memory about all that we have talked about earlier. Here is the entire path that you have set out on. Indeed, no matter which Father you begin to read, you will find essentially the same thing in each, although they differ in their portrayal and explanation.
May you become wise! Lord give the blessing! 271
A JOURNEY TO THE ST. SERGIUS LAVRA A journey to the St. Sergius Lavra. An instruction to pilgrims.

Well, who did direct you to St. Poemen?! Someone who speaks enthusiastically about Godly things. I am guessing. I suppose it was the same person who was with you when you came to see me. I am very glad there is someone close to you who is able to speak like that. Take advantage of him, and each time he visits you, get him to talk on these subjects, drag everything out of him and acquire it for yourself.

You want to go to St. Sergius Monastery! Good. May God bless you to go. And you are going to walk! That is twice, three times as good! So that other people do not gossip, you may keep this to yourself. If that is impossible, let them talk. But you mistakenly suppose that this will be a pleasant stroll. It is fifty versts; perhaps you will cover this in a pleasant manner, but then you will begin to feel something you did not expect. You will get there in two days. You will find out the first night what walking means, if you did not already know. Do not forget to bring a little flask of vodka with salt and rub it into your legs at night. By evening they will not feel like yours. Rub the vodka in, and by morning they will be a little better. In the morning you will not set out as you did from Moscow, but get moving.

You may return by train, because you would hardly be able to endure another two days of walking. But do as your heart suggests. This is labor for the Lord; He will give you the strength. And He will give something afterwards, either perceptible or imperceptible. St. Sergius will also give something. God gives the saints the ability to see what believers do for them and 272

JOURNEY TO ST. SERGIUS LAVRA to hear what they ask of them. This is only if everything comes from the heart. Just as a telegraph in a single instant lets people in St. Petersburg and other places know what is going on in Moscow, so too does what is born in the heart make itself known in a single instant to the one to whom it is directed.

St. Sergius has been watching you since you made plans to go visit him. When you set out on your trip, he will watch after you more closely, and even more closely when you come to his monastery, having endured the journey good-naturedly and with the joy of the labor. Lord give the blessing! Just do not expect comforts, but fatigue, callouses and aching bones. Prepare to offer sacrifice, and not to get pleasure.

While you are on the road, repeat unceasingly the short prayer I have written you about; otherwise the thoughts will bring you who knows where in the wilderness. They could bring you to that which one ought not think of in general, and especially while on a pilgrimage. When you are tired of repeating the prayer, take up one of the psalms and recite it from memory with reflection. Recite Have mercy on mcy O God or O God be attentive unto helping me^ or some other psalm that you know.

Do not worry about anything except reciting it from beginning to end and thinking it over and feeling its content. Understand each word after asking questions about it, such as, why was this said and where will it lead me? Being thus absorbed, you will not even notice the versts as they go by. Reciting psalms from the memory with reflection during a journey was a rule of the first great Fathers Anthony and Pachomius. They required anyone who became one of their disciples to memorize without fail several psalms by heart. Whoever could not read learned to read after he had memorized the psalms by listening to someone else recite. After learning a few psalms, he had to recite them while sitting at work and especially when going somewhere. Do this, if you will, in imitation of the ancient monks. 273
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Do not forget to confess and take Communion of the Holy Mysteries in the monastery.

The labor of travel will be in place of the labors of fasting and attending services. Confession is not difficult for you now after the last, the one that was done thoroughly. Do not hide anything that the conscience disapproves, whether great or small. But do not overlook the most important thing, tears of contrition. One little tear, and you will be like someone who has been to the bath house.

I wanted to write you something previously concerning tears, but I forgot. I will write something now. You get books to read from the library. Get Zhukovsky and read “The Peri and the Angel.” This is in the fifth volume, it seems. It is morally edifying and long. I will tell you briefly about the content. A peri is a spirit, one that was induced to fall away from God but that came to its senses and returned to Heaven. After arriving at the gates of Heaven, however, he found them closed. The angel guarding them said, “There is hope that you can enter, but bring a worthy gift.” The peri flew to the earth. He saw a war. A valiant soldier was dying, and in dying tears he was praying to God for his fatherland. The peri caught a tear and took it to Heaven. The gates, however, did not open. The angel told him, “It is a nice gift, but it cannot open the gates of Heaven for you. This means that all civic virtues are good, but they alone do not lead to Heaven.” The peri flew back to earth. He saw a plague. A handsome man was dying. His betrothed was selflessly caring for him, but she was becoming infected herself No sooner had she closed his eyes than she, too, fell dead on his chest. There were tears. The peri caught one and brought it, but the gates of Heaven were still closed. The angel said to him, “It is a nice gift, but it cannot open the gates of Heaven for you.
This means that family virtues alone also will not bring one to heaven. Search! There is hope.” The peri returned to earth once 274

DILIGENT CONFESSION again. He found someone who was repenting. He took a tear and brought it. As he approached Heaven, all the doors were already open to him. Thus, bring, if you will, such tears to the Lord. There is joy in Heaven that someone weeps and is contrite, feeling his sinfulness. This is the most trustworthy path for us.
Repent and believe the gospel (Mark 1:15).
DILIGENT CONFESSION Consolation ofa pilgrim. Instruction about careful Confession.
Shortprayers and unceasing attentiveness to God.

You have made your journey! Thanks be to God! As you see, my prediction about fatigue and aches and pains came to pass. What was promised for your soul also came to pass. The Lord comforted you with the joy of Holy Communion, and St. Sergius drove away your disturbance.

You write, “There arose in my soul a powerful and alarming disturbance. I appealed to St. Sergius, and the disturbance instantly vanished; it was replaced by a calm and pleasant peace.” So you see, a cry from the heart is heard immediately in Heaven, and a reply to it is sent down immediately. Learn from this where you need to seek peace and understanding.

It was not good, however, that you did not admit to any sins at Confession. Be it great or small, of word or of deed; as soon as the conscience tells you something is a sin, you must cleanse it through Confession and repentance. The essence of the Mystery of Repentance consists in the resolution of the sin you have confessed. An unconfessed sin is not resolved, but remains in the soul, and will go with it to the next world. There 275
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE will be a black stain, all the more noticeable on a white garment.
Craftiness will be of no use there. It is necessary to do not what is set down by people, but by the Lord Himself. The Lord says.

Do thoufirst confess thy transgressionsy that thou mayest hejustified (Isaiah 43:26). To comfort us and facilitate this, which is not always an easy matter. He set down this law. Whatsoeveryou shall bind on earth shall be bound in Heaveny and whatsoeveryou shall loose on earth shall be loosed in Heaven (Matt. 18:18). Learn well and take into your heart that everything that is not resolved (loosed) on earth in a Godly way will remain unresolved also in Heaven. Make up this deficiency as soon as possible. Now when you repent of the sin you omitted from Confession, you must also repent of the fact that you deliberately omitted it.

You did well in maintaining your short prayer for almost the entire way. Learn never to be without it. It will focus the attention and drive out idle and vain thought, and keep you in a state to receive understanding in the hour of need from the Lord through your Guardian Angel. The little prayer is a great treasure. It has no equal among spiritual activities. Try to stand attentively in the heart with it, and not just to repeat the words unceasingly, but to keep remembrance of the Lord, combining reverential fear with this. God is everywhere. Whether we are thinking about Him oi not. He is there ail the same wherever we are, and He sees everything. Judging by human standards, how sorrowful it is for Him when we do not pay attention to Him, although He maintains everything, takes care of everyone and provides everyone with everything good. Exclude yourself from the group of those who are inattentive, and do not forget to turn your mind’s eye to the Lord, or at least maintain the feeling that He is near, just as we sense that the sun is overhead and warming us even though we do not look at it. Turning mentally to God with reverence from the heart is already prayer.
Do this as soon as there is an opportunity. Do not worry too 276

NEED OF A GOOD COUNSELOR much if there is no opportunity during the day to stand before icons and make prostrations. Render all of this to the Lord during morning and evening prayer. You should read, reflect and pray a little longer at those times. Go to sleep, and then, after waking up a little earlier, get out of bed as soon as possible and engage in sweet conversation with the Lord. Start the morning in the right way, and the entire day will go well.

Remembrance of God, reading the Gospel and Epistle and other spiritually beneficial books will reveal and confirm ideas within you about what is truly worthwhile. The conscience will take these ideas under its guardianship and begin putting them into practice by not permitting you to deviate from the actions they dictate. The blessed life will ensue. Just keep this as law: Never do anything, whether great or small, that goes against the conscience. If you should violate this, compensate the conscience at once through your own inner repentance at home.
Confess it to the priest later. The conscience is a great thing. It is the voice of the Omnipresent God in the soul. He who is in the world with the conscience is also in the world with God.
THE NEED OF A GOOD COUNSELOR Idle talk and the discussions ofmen. The need ofa good counselor. Constant dangerfrom the enemy.
Your words, “A rumor went around,” had quite an effect on my heart! Oh, how terrible and biting is the fire of speech and the suspicious eye of man! It is clear why the holy prophet David 277
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE in the psalms often appeals to God with heartfelt sorrow to deliver him from the human tongue.

Where are you to find comfort and support? In the testimony of your conscience. Keep this consciousness of the moral worth of your actions before God and all sober-minded people in your mind and heart. With this you will courageously encounter every kind of speech, no matter what is said. At the same time you should treat everyone as if you know nothing.

It is impossible in everyday life to completely ignore what people are saying or what they will say. In general, the prudent thing here should be to go only to the extent that you conduct your affairs in such a way so as not to provoke the speech and offend the eyes of men. You do not have to go any further, say to the extent where you give up doing things that are considered necessary.

Well, have you expressed any sort of peculiarity up to this point ?! Possibly the fact that you went to St. Sergius?! But this was not done publicly, and you were among your own kind. Or that you go to church for Vigil and Liturgy on Sundays and feast days?! But this is a duty that is faithfully carried out by all Christians who keep in mind that they are Christians. One may be excused from this only by extreme necessity without bringing reproach for laziness and neglect of one’s Christianity. Do not imitate the bad habits of the upper class ladies of Moscow or listen to their vain words. That is today; tomorrow is death.

Death has no regard for beautiful women; it cuts them down as well. After death, there will be an immediate accounting. The clever-mindedness of upper class ladies will by no means be taken into consideration there.

Do not be troubled by words, then. You do everything for your part so as not to provoke them. If, in spite of everything, they happen, then let them. The witness of the conscience before God is sufficient, as I said, for the comforting and 278

NEED OF A GOOD COUNSELOR strengthening of your courage. Let everyone judge you, but if God justifies you in the conscience, these judgments will amount to nothing. An observant person was telling me that as soon as men s words latch onto something that is truly unpleasant, they hang over a person like a cloud that does not give any rain, and then depart. Their traces grow cold, and no one remembers what was said anymore. It will be the same with you, I think. I wish this for you from the bottom of my heart.
Conduct yourself equably, as usual, as if you do not notice anything.
In place of the severity of asceticism, which is not applicable to your way of life, take the fear of God and remembrance of death, and they will teach you everything.
Write about everything. Secretiveness in everyday life is not a bad thing; in spiritual life, however, it is the most dangerous.
It is indispensable to have someone with whom you may consult about everything that is going on outwardly, and more importantly, inwardly. Write just as we have already discussed.

There is some sort of evil power around us and inside us, which through various illusory qualities leads us into deception and confuses our affairs, directing them to something vain or even bad. The enemy is sitting quite near and urging on everything under his hand. There is a lot to help against him and one’s own reasoning; there is the fact that you should not do every deed that seems good immediately, but examine it thoroughly before acting. When you begin your examination, the suggestions of good that the enemy uses to disguise his provocations immediately disperse. Your reasoning, however, does not always work, because the enemy often confuses it with his own advice (our elders have nicknamed this “add-vice”). Write, then, and tell everything. If you should find someone there, you will be able to discuss everything more often. 279
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE May the Lord save you and have mercy on you! Live and learn.
Take care of yourself. Mistakes will show you how to act more correctly next time.
DEPRESSION AND FEAR Depression andfear. Innocent distraction. Severing relationships with immoralpeople. An English apostlefrom a Pentecostal sect. Hisfalsehood.
Your depression mixed with dread is somehow unclear.

What is wrong with you? You have everything in order, both at home and in your soul. You will overcome it; what should you do?! Pray to God and entrust your fate and that of everyone close to you to Him. This is the most reliable way to peace! You should have but one concern—to not do anything that would anger God. You will have firm hope and lasting peace from this.
That you have some family amusements is fine. Just do not get carried away. Do not forget God, and thank Him for every comfort, accepting such comfort as coming from His hands.

Then when you seclude yourself, fall down before the Lord more diligently, asking Him to give you understanding and indicate life’s paths. It happens that amusements, especially pleasant ones, give rise to depression, because while they are not sinful, they are unable to content the heart. Generally speaking, the inconstancy of the emotions is characteristic to us. It is necessary to discard and overcome this, being concerned that one thing does not change; that is, that your most important decision, the goal of life you chose for yourself, always remain in force. 280

DEPRESSION AND FEAR Can this really be?! God is asking your heart once and for all, and the heart desires God. For without God it is never satisfied, it is bored; examine yourself from this aspect. Maybe you will find the door to the peace of God there.
