That you broke off with one of your friends is a very good thing; her charming and ingratiating manner influenced you in a way that was not at all conducive to the goal oflife you planned for yourself. You made mention of this influence at one time, but I neglected to tell you to watch out. Well, thank God! Now the affair has taken a turn for the better of its own accord. It is not proper, of course, to allow all kinds of hostile things into the heart. Do not permit yourself to trust her any more or take her pleasant words to heart. Behave equably, and do not upset peaceful relations.

You ask what to think about some English apostle. Your own words tell what to think about him. He says that the Lord appears to him and tells him what to do and what to say. Don’t you and those around you see that he is a deceiver? If the Lord appeared to him. He would not have sent him to us, but to the Turks, for instance, or some other nonbelievers. The Lord Himself has repeatedly confirmed and continues to confirm our faith through miracles, the holy relics and the appearances of Him and His saints. There is nothing to teach us. On the contrary, he needs to learn from us. This apostle does not come from the traditional English church, but from some peculiar one that appeared recently. A pentecostal sect recently appeared there. Someone got it into his head that what happened to the Apostles must be repeated in the church of Christ; that is, that the Holy Spirit descended visibly and acted visibly in believers.

Some people, mostly women, believed him. They began thinking how to achieve this. They thought and thought, and came up with something. Somehow they became convinced that the Holy Spirit was acting in them specifically, and they began 281

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE convincing others of this. They fabricated a few suspicious miracles and began trumpeting them everywhere. Quite a few gullible people gathered around them. They separated from the others and established their own ecclesiastical order. There is a sect for you!

This is the sort of milieu your apostle comes from. He explains everything about the Holy Spirit, that everyone must without fail be filled with His grace to be saved. This idea is totally correct. I revealed this to you in our first letters. But this is the only true thing he says. Everything else is a lie! He cries, “Believe, believe, and the Holy Spirit will come.” This is his biggest lie! Faith is indeed an indispensable condition for receiving the gift of the Holy Spirit, but the very receiving of the gift comes about not from faith alone, but from faith through the Divinely-established Mysteries. This is how it was even in Apostolic times. Here is a case in point. St. Paul arrived in Ephesus, and after encountering several believers there, he asked them whether they had received the Holy Spirit. They answered they had not even heard of the Holy Spirit. It turned out that they were baptized only with the baptism ofJohn. St. Paul then baptized them with the baptism of Christ, and after that he laid his hands on them and they were filled with the Holy Spirit.

They had been renewed by Baptism, but they had not received the gift of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit was imparted to them through the apostolic laying on of hands, while Baptism only gave them the ability to receive the gift and made them worthy of it. As it was with these believers, so has it been for all believers, both during Apostolic times and afterward. That is how it has been to this time in the Divine Church, namely through Holy Chrismation, which the Apostles themselves introduced in place of the laying on of hands.
All of us who have been baptized and chrismated have the gift of the Holy Spirit. Everyone has the gift, but it is not active 282
DEPRESSION AND FEAR in everyone. How to make it active is explained in my first letters. Take some time to review this again. I would add here only that there is no other way to this gift.

Go and ask the English apostle whether he himself has the gift of the Holy Spirit. He does not. For the English do not have the Mystery of Chrismation, without which, just as without the laying on of the Apostolic hands, the Holy Spirit never descended and never will. The English have only two Mysteries, that of Baptism and Communion, but they have no Mystery of Chrismation. Consequently, they also do not have the gift of the Holy Spirit. This apostle also does not have it. Therefore, he is talking about something he does not know about and preaches about receiving something that he himself does not have. You will not observe in his speeches even a mention of how the Holy Spirit is received. He talks only about the necessity for having Him, and then cries out, ^Telieve!” This is like saying, “Open your mouth and the Holy Spirit will fly in.” How appropriate are the Lord’s words for this apostle, “Physician, heal thyself!” Be convinced, if you will, and pass my ideas on to others, especially that fellow who enjoys discussing religious subjects at your place. Such a view of the apostle may become more widely dispersed through him.
ON THE READING OF SPIRITUAL AND SECULAR BOOKS On the reading ofspiritual and secular books.

I am sending you the writings of St. Anthony by your request. Read and be absorbed. You will be surprised. He was not educated and did not read books of learned men; he only sang the Psalter and read the Gospel along with the Epistle. The grace of God revealed contemplation in his mind, and you see how wise his words are. There are eyewitness accounts of him that tell how when he began to speak, his words flowed like a river and, coming from his heart, they filled the hearts of his listeners. Sometimes they spent entire nights in conversation, and neither he nor those listening to him became tired or felt like going to sleep. We had Father Seraphim of Sarov who was also uneducated, but his experiences in spiritual life, absorption in the word of God and writings of the Fathers made him a wise man among the wise. Books are only for guidance in the spiritual life. Knowledge itself is acquired through deeds. Even that which is known from reading, clear and detailed though it be, presents itself in an entirely different light when experienced through deeds. The spiritual life is such a realm into which the wisdom of this world cannot penetrate. You yourself will experience this or are already doing so. Work on yourself and be attentive to yourself. Little by little, you will reach the point where you will begin holding wise conversations which you should sit down and write out! May the Lord bless you! 284

THE READING OF BOOKS You write, “I read a lot; is this bad?” It can be bad and it can be good, depending on what you read and how you read it. Read with discrimination and verify what is being read through the genuine truth of our faith. What agrees with it, accept; what does not agree with it, reject as thought against God, and throw out any book that expresses such thoughts. You have begun studying spiritual life. This is a subject that encompasses a lot, is lofty and sweet for the heart, in which you cannot help but see the uttermost good. You have taken this up and are learning, both from books and even more from deeds. You already know what kind of books you should read, and you also understand how to arrange your personal life. If you seriously desire to set out on this path, then you will never take up the study of other subjects. You went to school and have a general knowledge of everything; this is sufficient for you.

You will say, “I will become backward!” What kind of misfortune is that! Backward in one thing, well-developed in another—and in the other, quite superior. If at the same time that you were lagging in human wisdom you were not v/ell-developed in Divine wisdom, it would be a detriment. But as you will become well-developed in the latter if you undertake things in a proper manner, then you will not suffer detriment, but rather, acquire superiority. This is because human wisdom cannot be compared with spiritual wisdom.

Speaking in this way, I do not want to say that it is forbidden to read anything else, but only that without anything else one can get by without detriment; whereas if one is inclined to other things, it is possible to suffer detriment in the most important area. If you pursue two different goals, you will not accomplish even one.
The question still remains unresolved as to whether one may read anything besides spiritual things. I would tell you with reservation, in a low voice: You may if you like, but just a little 285

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE and not indiscriminately. Take this as a sign: When you are in a good mood spiritually and begin reading a book containing human wisdom, if the good mood begins to desert you, get rid of the book. This is a general rule for you.

Even books containing human wisdom may nourish the spirit. These are the books that indicate to us the vestiges of wisdom, benevolence, truth and solicitous Divine industry in nature and history. Read books such as these. God reveals Himself in nature and history in the same way as in His Word.
Nature and history are Divine books for those who know how to read them.

You will say, “It is easy to tell me to read such books, but where do you get them?” This I cannot tell you. There are presently more books on scientific subjects coming out. Most of them, however, have a very bad direction; namely, they attempt to explain the origin of the world without God, and all moral-religious and other manifestations of the spiritual in our lives without the spirit and soul. Do not get those books. There are books on scientific subjects without such false wisdom.

These you may read. It is good to understand the structure of plants and animals, especially man, and the laws of life as they are manifested in them. Great is the Divine wisdom in all of this! Unknowable! Ask the fellow who enjoys discussing religious subjects which books are of this sort.

What about stories and novels? There are good ones among these. To find out whether they are good, however, you must read them, and after you have finished, you will have acquired such tales and images that—God have mercy! You will soil your clean little mind. Afterward, go get cleaned up. Why would you want to bring such labor upon yourself? Therefore, I think it is better not to read them. When a benevolently-minded person who has read some story recommends it, you may read it. 286

ON COLDNESS IN PRAYER There are good descriptions of geography. You may also read these. However, do a little of everything, and then only for variety. Keep up your spiritual wotk, and do not turn your attention away from it.
May the Lord bless you! 71 * ON COLDNESS IN PRAYER On coldness in prayer. Careless and hurriedprayer.
How to flee this. The reading ofprayersfrom memory.

You write, “My prayer is somehow going poorly.” That prayer is going poorly is not the fault of the prayer, but of the one who is praying. Try to pray as you should, and the prayer will go well. If someone writes carelessly, people reprimand and threaten him, and he begins writing meticulously. Reprimand yourself, if you will, and threaten yourself with Divine judgment, and you will begin praying properly. We conscientiously correct human affairs, because people would see and reproach us. We conduct the affairs of God haphazardly, however, because the Lord is silent and does not reproach us immediately, presenting His children, as many as are benefitted by Him, the opportunity to serve Him. Oh, how bitter it will be when we will have to plead guilty in this to the Lord Himself Where has your prayer gone? It was going well, you know, and you were already feeling its beneficial action in your heart.

I will tell you where it has gone. You prayed once or twice diligently and with warmth, and received such quick help from St. Sergius as a consequence of your prayer, that you therefore thought your prayer was already established, and there was thus 287

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE nothing more to worry about concerning it, that it would go along well on its own. By permitting this kind of thinking, you began reciting the prayers carelessly and hurriedly, and ceased guarding the thoughts. Because of this, your attention became dispersed, your thoughts were scattered in different directions and there was no prayer in the prayer. This happened a couple of times, and then prayer totally vanished. Acquire prayer from the beginning again, and ask for it from the Lord yourself You will learn from this that you must never consider any spiritual activity to be established, especially prayer; always go about it as if you were going to carry it out for the first time.

First zeal is given to something that is done for the first time. If when undertaking prayer, you go about it as if you have never prayed properly yet, and only now do you want to do so for the first time, you will always carry out your prayer with first zeal.

I suppose you began carrying out your prayer rule hurriedly—and haphazardly—^just so you would finish it. Make it a rule for yourself from now on, that you will never pray haphazardly. Nothing offends the Lord more than this. It is better to recite a few of the set prayers with the fear of God and reverence than to recite all of them haphazardly. It is better even to fall down on your knees and recite one of the prayers or even use your own words than to do the other thing. You began praying that other way, and there are no results. Scold yourself thoroughly for such carelessness. Know that no one who prays attentively and diligently ever departs from prayer without having received its action. Oh, what good we deprive ourselves of when we permit ourselves to pray carelessly!

How does haste come about in prayer? It is not clear. We spend hours on other tasks and it never seems long; but hardly have we begun our prayer than it seems we have been praying for who knows how long. We hurry ourselves along in order to 288

ON COLDNESS IN PRAYER finish sooner. There is no benefit from the prayer. What should be done, then? Some people do the following, so as not to be subject to this self-deception: They set aside a quarter of an hour for prayer, or half an hour, whatever is more convenient for them, and thus adjust their prayer time so that when the clock strikes, whether on the half hour or hour, they will know when it is time to end. While they are at prayer, they do not worry about reading a certain number of prayers, but only that they rise up to the Lord in a worthy manner for the entire set time.

Others do this: Once they have established a prayer time for themselves, they find out how many times they can go around the prayer rope during that period, moving the beads in an unhurried manner. Then when they are at prayer, they move the beads unhurriedly for the set number of times, and during that period they pray to the Lord in their mind, speaking to Him in their own words or reciting set prayers, or bowing with reverence to His boundless majesty without either of those.

There are others who get so accustomed to praying that the times they spend at prayer are moments of delight for them. It rarely happens that they stand at prayer for the set time only; instead, they double and triple it. Select whichever method pleases you best. Maintain it without fail. We cannot get along without a precise prayer rule for you. Those who are zealous about prayer do not need any rules.

I have already written you about memorizing the prayers and reciting them by heart when you are at prayer, without opening your prayer book. This is nothing to boast about! When you are at prayer, recite the prayer or psalm from memory, and embrace each word, not just with thought, but with feeling. If during this your own prayerful cries arise from some word of the psalm or prayer, do not cut them off, but let them come.
You see, you will not worry about reciting a certain number of prayers, but only being at prayer for the allotted time, which 289

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE will make itself known either by beads on the prayer rope or by the clock. Rush through the recitation of prayers and nothing will happen. Recite one psalm or one prayer for the whole time.

Someone was saying that often he will recite a single prayer, the Our Father^ for the entire allotted time. This is because he considers each word in the entire prayer. Another person said that when someone explained to him that one could pray in this way, he stood in reverential prayer for the entire Matins, reciting Have mercy on mey O Gody and did not manage to complete the entire psalm.
Get accustomed to praying in this manner if you will, and, God granting, you will soon cultivate prayer within yourself.
Then you will no longer need any rules. Labor, for nothing will come from you otherwise. If you are not successful in prayer, do not expect success in anything else. It is the root of everything.
THE VOW OF RENUNCIATION OF THE WORLD The vow ofrenunciation ofthe world. How to conduct oneselfafier pronouncing the vow until its fulfillment.

Your last letter furnished me with unspeakable joy. So you made up your mind once and for all to dedicate your life to serving the Lord and to not have anything to do with worldly life. May God graciously accept this sacrifice and bless your good decision! You have spoken before the Lord; keep to it then, and from here on be so formed in your thoughts as to be already cut off from the world. You told me previously that you have a 290

RENUNCIATION OF THE WORLD distaste for worldly life, both because of the weakness of your health, and because of your fear of such misfortunes as you have had occasion to observe in other families. But that was one conversation; this is now another. The matter is one and the same, but the spirit is completely different. First there was reluctance toward worldly life for worldly reasons, but now you are cutting yourself off from worldly life so that you may belong entirely to the Lord. Few people live without a family, but those who do live in the same way as those with families, with the very same cares and expectations. Your decision is not in this spirit.
It is from an entirely different source, belongs to a different way of life and is preparing you for it. Kindle that very spirit in which you voiced your decision.

The decision has been pronounced, but now it is necessary to wait patiently as to when and how it will be carried out. It would be impossible to dash off suddenly in your situation, when everything, as you expect, would be against it. Parental blessing is the first condition. You must wait for it. Wait and pray. The Lord Who instilled such a good intention within you will carry it out Himself in an inconspicuous fashion, and then it will be downhill the rest of the way. “Speak” without talking, keeping in your soul one thought; pray wholeheartedly to the Lord that He carry out your intention, as His wise will knows.

At the same time, adapt to that kind of life within yourself, not by a change of outward ways, but of the inward order. By this I do not mean anything in particular other than that which we have previously written a lot about and that you have already begun. Set about everything that you have been told to do a little more decisively now. The foundation has been laid; construct the building.
You have turned your back on worldly things; do not turn your face toward them again. 291

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Now you may completely refuse all amusements and stay more at home in solitude, engaged in some occupation, to include studies according to your frame of mind. Now you may act more decisively in this respect.
I would also remind you about the most important thing.

When renouncing the world, it is necessary to become closer to God. You know this, but you will be blessed if you begin doing it. Think more about God and things of God. Fix in your mind the remembrance that God is everywhere, both outside you and within, and never be separated from this remembrance as you go about; work, sit, sleep and be awake with it. Join to this another remembrance, that of death, and that upon death, there will be the judgment and determination of eternal fate. These two remembrances are the two powerful guards of everything good. They themselves will teach you everything, kindling inside you the saving fear of God, the source of divine wisdom.

You already know all this. I am reminding you of everything in passing, so you will set about it a little more decisively. What you need to hear especially now is. Lord give the blessing! But await the fulfillment with patience. 73 THE VOW OF CHASTITY Continuation. Once a vow ofchastity is given one must keep it steadfast. The heights ofvirginity.
I rush to add still more for you on the same subject.

St. Paul, who imparted the Christian faith in Corinth, fearing that the Corinthians were headed down the wrong path, wrote them. For I am jealous over you with godly jealousy: for I have espousedyou to one husband, that 1 maypresentyou as a chaste 292

THE VOW OF CHASTITY virgin to Christ. But Ifear, lest by any means, as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtlety, so your minds should be corruptedfrom the simplicity that is in Christ{2 Cor. 1 1:2-3). I have been jealous with godly jealousy over you since the beginning of our correspondence, as you see. This is what you will present to Christ the Lord—a chaste virgin, giving yourself to Him! But your betrothal to the Lord was not accomplished by me. It was arranged apart from my efforts or even my intentions. When writing to you, I had nothing in mind other than to set you on the way of the true Christian life, a reliable way in which, just as in a life boat, you would be able to sail the uncertain sea of this temporary existence. However, something more happened.
Thank God for that! Your predisposition toward it came naturally; God gave it to you. God breathed His grace into it, and here is the decision!
The decision! It is, however, still far from the actual deed.

How many things might be encountered by accident that could turn everything upside down. That is why it is impossible not to fear lest your mind be corrupted from naivete, and the enemy beguile you through his subtlety, as the snake did Eve so long ago. May the Lord deliver you from such circumstances. However, if it is always necessary for the servants of Christ to be sober and awake, vigilant and prayerful, it is even more necessary for you now with the intention you have. I can say nothing definite to you other than to be on guard. The enemy may attack at any moment. Write as soon as possible if that should happen, and in the meantime stand fast.

Implant a little more deeply the conviction that the way of life you have chosen is blessed by God. It was sanctioned by the Savior in the saying about eunuchs for the Kingdom of Heaven’s sake. He gave it preference when he said of Mary, who had sat down by His feet and was listening only to what He said, that I. Matthew 19:12. 293

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE she had chosen the better part, that she had chosen the one thing needful.^ You see clear examples of it in the person of many Prophets and Apostles, the Holy Forerunner of the Lord, and especially the Most Pure Lady Theotokos, the friend and source of purity. May Her motherly protection always be upon you.
Virgins are the special object of Her protection.

Female and male virgins were a fixture in Christ’s Church even in apostolic times; they have been in the Church ever since and will continue to be in it, as long as there is a Church, that is, until the end of time. For this kind of life is not alien to our nature, and is favored by the spirit of belief in Christ. Many young women in Corinth did not want to get married, having fallen completely in love with Christ the Lord, the only husband to all. Their fathers turned to St. Paul, asking how they should act. St. Paul advised them to give their daughters freedom to work for the Lord and not oblige them to get married. The fathers listened to his advice, and the young women remained virgins.^ The other churches followed the example of the Corinthians, and virginity blossomed everywhere. In other places, virgins gathered together to live in the same house; they worked for and prayed to God, not having anything to do with other people. Many of these virgins received the crown of martyrdom, as their lives attest.

You see what an assembly you are joining, in whose footsteps you are following, under whose protection you will be—and whose words you are heeding. See then, having put your hand to the plough, that you do not look back.^ Learn by heart the words of the Apostle on renunciation of marriage, and bring them to mind frequently: He that is unmarried careth for the things that belong to the Lordy how he may please the Lord... The

YEARNING FOR MONASTIC LIFE UTiTnciTTicd ivoTTiciTi ccLvcth Jof thc things oj^the Lord, that she Tnciy he holy both in mind andspirit {\ Cor. 7:32,34). There is nothing to say about the holiness of the body. But it is impossible not to say a lot about the holiness of the spirit, and not to take a lot of care concerning it. It is possible to lose it in an instant. See then thatyou walk circumspectly (Ephesians 5:15). How do you walk circumspectly? You guard the heart, so that it does not become attached to anything, not just to a person—nothing needs to be said about that—but not to any object at all. Any such attachment would be a violation of your betrothal to the Lord; it would be unfaithfulness to Him. For He is jealous, and He will exact severely from the hearts of those who are unfaithful.

I am not writing this to frighten you. How powerful fright is! But I do remind you that it may sometimes be necessary to admonish yourself with the fear of God, if you will understand in no other way. It is not possible to rely on our zeal and constancy. Here today, but tomorrow who knows what will be.
You should know how bad it will be if you deviate from your intention.

May the Lord bless you! 74YEARNING FOR MONASTIC LIFE An instruction for those who yearn for monastic life. Various aspects ofthe ascetic exploit ofthe celibate life. The patient waiting andpreparation at homefor monastic life.
You write, “Oh, if only it could be sooner! I would fly off now somewhere to the desert, so that I would see and hear 295
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE nothing.” No, no. It is quite impossible to rush. When there is haste and zeal like this to hurry things along, it is not of God.
Confused desires are well-meaning, but they are not good and do not lead to the good. Godly things move along slowly and imperceptibly, but steadfastly.

Pray and wait with patience, looking to see whether a door will open. The Lord will arrange things in such a v/ay as you cannot guess. You may become engaged to the Lord by yourself now, before Him Alone. As to when you will enter the chorus of His brides, let Him decide. Put yourself completely in His hands.

You are selecting a monastery. A monastery is not the only place for those who do not want to be bound by family ties. In the beginning there were no monasteries at all. Those who made up their minds to serve the Lord without being bound by everyday cares set up solitary cells for themselves within their homes and lived there, estranged from everything, fasting, praying and reading the Divine Scriptures. Then, after some time had passed and it became inconvenient to live in homes, some began leaving the cities and settling and living in natural caves, catacombs or cells that were constructed for that purpose.

After that, monasteries were set up so people could live in a community and maintain themselves and carry on the activity of salvation in deliberate deeds through mutual efforts. Even as this was going on, there were others who decided to devote their lives to the Lord in an unmarried state but who did not enter monasteries, instead giving themselves to the service of their brothers and sisters in hospitals, poor-houses and hospices.

None of these ways of life that came to be in the very beginning ofGhrist’s Ghurch have ceased, but have always existed and exist to this day. Even today unmarried people seek salvation at home, some go to monasteries, while others undertake to look after the sick as sisters of mercy. 296
YEARNING FOR MONASTIC LIFE You, too, may select any of these ways of life. As to which one precisely, you must wait and see which one God indicates.

You were correct in saying that it is easier to find salvation in a monastery. There one may achieve purification of the heart sooner and that state which is joy in the Holy Spirit. Everything there is directed toward this. I think that because of the state of your health, a monastery would be the most suitable place for you. That is why you should not abandon the idea of entering one in due course. The Lord will indicate to you when. Until that time, you must be patient. Until then, follow the first way of life, that is, living at home. Have your own room; have it like a monastic cell, and conduct life there as if you were in a monastery. Let your father and mother be in place of the Mother Superior for you; your family in place of the sisters of the monastery; and you, a devoted novice for all.

You are hastening to a monastery as if to freedom and to Paradise. Indeed there is complete freedom for the spirit in a monastery, but not for the body or external activities. With regard to this, there is the absolute spiritual prohibition, which is immutable law, against having one’s own will. You will indeed find Paradise there, but not always as if you are strolling down a flowery path. It may truly be found there, but it is obstructed by thorns and barbs through which you must go to reach it. No one achieves it without getting bruises and scratches. Keep these things in mind, and you will attain your hoped-for freedom and Paradise in the monastery.
Wait patiently, then. Remember the governess!^ She waited seven years, testing the firmness of her intention. She turned out to be a fervent nun because of this.
Why rush? The monasteries will not go away. You will have time to enter one someday. In the meantime, you should reinforce your intention and strengthen your body. You have

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE devout ways at home. Your parents and relatives are God-fearing people. There is nothing that would be adverse to your decision that you cannot endure. Devote yourself to the Lord. Mature in this atmosphere and seed-plot.
Thus, it has been decided: Wait. Adapt your life and your habits to those of the monastery.
Lord give the blessing! 75 THE TRICKS OF THE ENEMY The tricks ofthe enemy can dissuade onefrom the life ofrenunciation ofthe world. How to repel them.
You write, “I am sad. I cannot find peace anywhere. Something is burdening me; my heart was suddenly seized by heaviness and darkness.” The power of the Cross is with us! Take heart and stand fast.

The enemy has guessed that you want to flee completely from that society around Christians where he conveniently profits, so he greets you with these difficulties and languor. You are not the only one; everyone experiences such attacks, but no one experiences them in the same way. You are tormented by difficulties; another person is overcome with fear; another person’s thoughts are so full of obstacles that they are like mountains; another person’s mind depicts the absurdity of some enterprise he has undertaken. He approaches each person in the way most suited to that person, directs torrents of thought, alarms the heart, disturbs him inwardly. This happens all at once, like the sudden rage of a storm. Such are indeed the ploys of our enemies. They are a disorderly bunch and lounge about 298
THE TRICKS OF THE ENEMY everywhere. They see the doors are open and there are no guards; they burst into our home and turn everything upside down.

They create a fuss and then they’re gone. You are left to cope as you can. But, you see, there is still no harm from this. We are not guilty because our enemies attack; we are also not guilty of the disturbance of our inner state that they cause; we are also not guilty of those thoughts and feelings that are stirred up because of this. God, too, does not blame us. Just do not consent to anything that is not necessary. Suffer along, and all will pass.

You did not describe any characteristic of your languor, I suppose, because it is in fact not definite; more than anything else, this vagueness indicates that these are enemy attacks. Be steadfast! The Lord sent you this trial so that you could show that you were not joking when you made your vow. Treat your trial as something sent by the Lord, and say like Job, Blessed be the name ofthe Lord!^ It was peaceful, and now there is difficulty!

Thanks be to the Lord for peace, and thanks be for this difficulty. Tell the Lord, “I will endure if that is Thy will! Just help me to suffer. Lord.” Throw yourself down before the Lord with your whole being. Call on the Mother of God.

You are suffering attack. This is an opportunity to show your ability to stand for the good, like a soldier; a chance to distinguish yourself when the enemy attacks. Let the enemy strike. You stand steadfastly for the good. You should even say this aloud, “I will not change my intention and decision one iota. I want nothing of what the enemy is suggesting.” Name each thing he suggests one by one, renouncing and cursing it.

Call on the Lord for help. Everything will pass. A layman was overcome by the spirit of abuse and disbelief. He related that, “I heard the enemy murmuring in my ears that there is no God, 1 . Naked came I out ofmy mother i womb, and naked shall I return thither: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name ofthe Lord (Job 1:21). 299

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE no Christ. But I just said over and over, ‘I believe, Lord! I believe, believe, believe!’ And I drove away the enemy.” That is what you must do, too. Cry out, “I do not want this, that or the other thing; I want nothing suggested by the enemy. I want one thing, to speak solemnly before the Lord in my heart.” You are saying, “I suppose I am a great sinner, and the Lord is punishing me.” It is good that you feel yourself to be a sinner.
And if you come to feel yourself to be a greater sinner, do not go too far with this feeling.

All of us are great sinners and irredeemable. If you will make out of your sinfulness an opportunity, there will be no misfortune. Just do not reverse your inner decisions, and do not fall into despair. No matter what the Lord sends, whether inwardly or outwardly, all is directed toward our cleansing and salvation. He Himself is close by and helps. He is close by you, too. Resurrect such hope and prayer while enduring.
The Lord sends this and things like it for the sake of crowns.

He who stands his ground will win a crown. We, and those around us and those whom we encounter, are all capable of obtaining crowns. You just need attention and courage, so that you receive everything from God and attribute everything to Him.
Enjoy yourself peacefully, and do not tolerate any hesitations. Lord give the blessing! 300
TEMPTATIONS FROM UNBELIEVERS Temptationsfrom unbelievers. A refutation oftheir vain wisdom and reasoning.
Well, now! No sooner did you manage to escape the first burden than another one, more subtle and more dangerous, was already waiting. W^ho is there with you who was carrying on so?

Did he not tell you a bunch of stories and cram a lot ofworthless garbage into your head? His aim was to make your faith waiver, and not only to divert you from Christ the Lord, but also to make you a total atheist. It was the enemy who sent you such a clever wise man.

You made the right decision; that is, to avoid in every way conversations with nonbelievers and any meetings with them. Evil conversations spoil the good attitude of the soul. Try to struggle and restore order afterward. But you know, you will not be saved from danger. These evil creatures stick their noses into everything.

Look around, and there they are. In the future act in this way: When by chance you happen to overhear something that goes against your religious faith, try in every way not to let what you have heard into your heart, but let it go in one ear and out the other. At the same time, while drawing the attention away from the words of a nonbeliever, take care to occupy the heart with the conviction that our faith is indisputably true in all aspects, and if it has enemies, this is from a misunderstanding of the matter. It is possible to dispute everything, even whether we exist. The restored conviction in our heart, accompanied by thought about the narrow-mindedness and bad-heartedness of the speaker. 301
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE will make your soul like a rock against which foolish words, when striking, will bounce off, leaving no traces.

If, in spite of this, something bad sinks into the soul, turn to prayer in the very same way that you did at St. Sergius Lavra: Appeal to your Guardian Angel, the Mother of God, and especially the Lord our Savior Himself, and they will disperse the gloom hanging over you. Afterward, either do your own examination of the bewilderment that befell you, or seek refutation of it in some book, or talk it over with someone; but without fail do something so that no trace of it is left in your soul. Any remaining trace would stir up the soul and be like stones cast at the feet of a pedestrian.

Write to me, too. You did well to write everything you heard. Although you did not waiver and have recovered from everything that lodged in your thought from this conversation, I would say, however, another word or two to counter it.

“Religious faith is instilled through education.” It does not instill it, but develops it and gives it a definite form. Every newborn enters into a society that has faith. The faith of society becomes his faith, as with other things. An aptitude for faith, however, and its requirement for the soul, are not instilled by society. It is the same with respect to knowledge. Society, through education, transmits the knowledge it has accumulated to its new member, but does not give him the aptitude for knowledge. This aptitude is an inherent property of the mind.

Likewise, faith in the existence of God and His almightiness is an inherent property of the spirit, which is found with vitality in each person as soon as his aptitudes are developed. Society transmits only the way of its faith, but not the power of belief itself or aptitude for it.
Society instills faith through education. But just how did belief come into society? Everything that goes into society comes from the mind. Society is the repository, like a reservoir, into 302

TEMPTATIONS FROM UNBELIEVERS which everything that comes from the mind flows. It is in this way that there is faith in society, because it is first of all in the mind. It went out from the mind and settled in society. Faith is older than society and the family, and, consequently, older than education. Education gives back to the newborn that which was previously introduced into the family and society from the mind.

Therefore, establish, if you will, a little more firmly the conviction in your mind that education develops only what is in human nature, but adds nothing new to it. If it develops faith, that is because it is in human nature. No one would be able to teach you to know God and to worship Him if this were not in the human spirit on account of some unassailable law. Here we see that there is religious belief in every race of man. The most primitive men have it, because without it, man is not man. Ifsome well-educated men do not believe, this does not mean they have made great strides forward or have achieved great heights; instead, it means that they have departed from human nature; they have distorted and mutilated themselves like someone who puts out his own eyes or cuts off his nose. If everyone has faith, it follows that the norm of human life undeniably includes faith. Consequently, he who does not have faith departs from this norm, and is a moral freak. All nonbelievers are of this order.

“A man fell among the wild beasts since childhood, and now he does things the same way they do.” This was discussed in French narratives and novels at the end of the last century. Whether this happened in reality I do not know. Man 1 . There were several celebrated cases of children who were allegedly raised by wild animals in eighteenth century Europe. Here, St. Thcophan is probably referring to that of Victor, a boy of about eleven years who was found in the Caune Woods in France in 1799. Dr. Jean-Marc-Gaspard hard studied Victor and tried to enculturate him, with limited success, hard wrote about his experiences in The Wild Boy ofAveyron, which has been translated into English by George and Muriel Humphrey (New York: Gentury, 1 932). 303

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE is man everywhere. Let him grow up among animals, and he will take after them without fail. But let it happen as they were saying: Take this man who grew up among the animals, take a couple of these animals, and place them in your own society.

After a week you would see how much the man differs from the animals. Why is this? Because there is the embryo of higher life in him. His faith will also emerge; it is within him already, but it is vague and obscured. It will awaken and soon come to full power beginning from the first indications. The animals, however, will remain animals.

Nonbelievers who have departed from the norm of human nature usually turn to abnormalities in confirming their skepticism. So do they point to congenital idiots, besides the alleged cases of man being raised by animals. What kinds of slander do they not make up in all this! There is no God and no soul. At the same time, to be fair, it is not proper to draw any conclusions from these cases, because they have still not been explained.

Think of congenital idiots in this way, if you will. They have a soul as well as all people, but it does not have the opportunity to manifest itself completely; it is bound. Apply this then to certain actions of Divine Economy. It happens that someone would be threatened by danger from a combination of circumstances, if he were to become involved in them. God sends him an illness, and while he is lying in bed, the circumstances pass without his getting involved. Thus is he saved from misfortune.

If he had been healthy, he certainly would have become entangled in matters, and would have fallen into misfortune. This is how you should think concerning congenital idiots: Their souls are bound for the entire period of their earthly existence, because if they were to be unbound, they would cause misfortune for others and for themselves. In the other life it will be revealed that this is so. Their souls will be transferred there like the souls of babies who have died. We will see whether such 304

3. bclicF turns out to be true or not in that world. But the controversy over this circumstance will be settled in that world, if it may be settled. It is not possible to come to any sort of conclusion concerning it in this world.
I will not touch upon the other absurdities you heard. You understood them well yourself. See then that you walk circuTnspectly, not asfools, but as wise 5:15).
May the Lord bless you! 77 OBEDIENCE TO PARENTS Temptations due to domestic unpleasantness. Obedience to parents as a preparation for monastic obedience.
What next! The enemy wanted to make you a nonbeliever; now he is making you a rebel, and against whom.^ Against your parents.

My God, do you whine in your letter! What could have happened? Most likely you were stung by a flea. I would guess that in all probability it is some trifle, and there is nothing to be said about it. But in your head it grew into such a big thing that you could not see it all at once. It is all the enemy’s doing. He is driving you from your parents’ home in order to muddle your brain. In this way he swells every trifle into a mountain that overwhelms you. You cannot describe what it is. I would tell you up front that there is nothing to describe. The enemy is up to his usual trick of confusing our mind with uncertainty. He knows how to cover the griefs he causes and his foolish comforts with illusions so that people, not seeing anything definite in them, nonetheless think they are something great. Once one 305

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE begins analyzing it, everything shatters; there is neither grief nor comfort where there once seemed to be mountains. All is deception. And now he has arranged to cover you with deception. Cross yourself, if you will, and spit on these counsels of the enemy.

Troubles, troubles! I am certain they do not exist at all. But suppose they do indeed exist; is it really necessary to run from them? One must bear them with good cheer and thankfulness to the Lord. It is the enemy, you see, who is rousing you. When a soldier encounters the enemy, is it necessary for him to run away? It is necessary to fight. You, too, must fight. What were you about to do? The enemy, raising some troubles, merely showed his horns, and here you are, ready to run. You are not thinking. After making the decision to not get married, you have only two ways to leave your parents’ home: Either you go to a monastery or to the sisters of mercy, one or the other, with the blessing ofyour parents themselves. Until one thing or the other has come about, stay at home and endure everything. Stand before the Lord at this very moment, if you will, sincerely repenting your evil confusion, and utter the firm decision, “I will wait, I will bear everything with good cheer. Thy will be done in everything. Lord!” I am unable to understand how you could disagree in something with your parents. If it concerns judgment in something, why don’t you yield in it each time with humility? They are older than you and more experienced. You must admit that you are more likely to make a mistake in judgment than they are. Assume that you probably understand poorly, and agree with their opinion. If it concerns the way something is done about the house, again do it their way, and that will be the end of it. If you cannot do something, explain that you cannot, and that will also be the end of it. If in spite of that they insist that you do it, do what you can. I cannot conceive ofany disagreement 306
INJUSTICE AND FALSE ACCUSATION you could have in anything that you could not cope with. The enemy makes a mountain out of a molehill in everything.
Do you want everything to go as you desire? Proud woman!

Not only is this wrong with respect to something around the house, but even with respect to you yourself. Give yourself up in total obedience to your parents and do everything to please them and put their minds at rest. This will be preliminary preparation for future monastic obedience or diligence in a community of sisters of mercy. Thus did St. Paul lay down the law for everyone: Submit yourselves one to another in the fear of God (Eph. 5:21). You, however, undertake to contradict your parents without fearing God. Where did this come from? In which school did you learn such wisdom?
I repeat to you once again. See then that you walk circumspectlyl May the Lord bless you!

INJUSTICE AND FALSE ACCUSATION Temptations due to the injustices andfalse accusations ofother people. The patient endurance ofthese. A warning to those who desire to leave their parents* houseforfreedom.

Again?! Injustices, wrongful accusations! It is probably from somewhere outside. No matter where it is from, I would say to you, do not just be courageous and stand fast, but rejoice. It means that things with your soul are going well. The Lord is leading you to cleansing, and the enemy is encountering strong opposition within you. Stand your ground firmly. There is no need to be troubled by injustices and wrongful accusations. He who causes them will be judged by God, and he who suffers 307
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE them must bear them with good cheer and give thanks to God.

Do not forget that there is another life in which everything here will be answered for, in praise or in remorse. What if you endure injustice? There will be praise. If you do not? Remorse. Do not say, “That is not right.” Whether something is right or not is not your business to judge, it will be said. Why did you not bear it?

Injustices do not come from God, but are permitted by God for the good of the person to whom they are permitted. Truly for the good! These are not just words, but a real thing. I cite for you the example of the martyrs, who were tormented in every way possible. And where was the Lord? He was there indeed, and appeared to them, comforted them, lessened their suffering, but all the same left them to bear their torments to the end, so as to receive the full crown. Similarly, each falsehood and wrongful accusation prepares a crown. But the person to whom they befall must endure them. Now you have encountered this necessity. Endure them with good cheer, if you will, as if they did not exist. God wants this from you for your own benefit.

You say it is terrible to give yourself over to falsehoods for an entire lifetime. Of course it is terrible. That is why it is said of those who follow the Lord, Be thou strong and of good courage! Ahead of you is a cross. It appears that nothing more remains other than to give yourself over to sorrows, privations and wrongful accusations. This self-denial, I would say to you, is the beginning ofthe true path. Give yourself over to that! The very moment you deny yourself, you begin following the Lord.
Think over all this and decide, if you will, in a Godly manner.
All success is from the necessary resolution of this point.

You would flee your parents’ house, but where to you still do not know. Apparently you feel some sort of constraint from them. Your parents’ duty is to guard you, and they cherish you, I. See Deuteronomy 31:6 and numerous other places in the Old Testament as well as I Corinthians 16:13 in the New Testament. 308

INJUSTICE AND FALSE ACCUSATION I think, as the apple of their eye. If this goes against your desires in some way, you must resign yourself It is not right to see this as their desire set against yours, but more natural to see here the desire to protect you and keep you safe from everything. This is the fruit of love. You may sweeten the bitterness of obedience with the certainty of this, if you feel that way on account ofyour disobedience.

You must pay back for what you receive in your family with this obedience. Food, clothing, shelter, while not insignificant, are not the most important things. The most important thing is security from outside troubles. You are protected by the wings of an eagle. The eagle s beak and talons are ready to defeat anyone who would attempt to cause you harm or trouble. This blessing is irreplaceable. Without the eagle to protect you, you would be pecked to death. No one is attacking now, but everyone would pounce on you later. You cannot imagine how terrible it is to be defenseless, because you have not experienced it. May God grant that you never experience it. Do you have protection? Keep it, and do not give it up. From the safety of your home you must find safety elsewhere, either within the walls of a monastery or inside the community of the sisters of mercy. Do not undertake the struggle alone! You would be pecked to death, and would not find your place.

What does such a strongly expressed impulse toward freedom for you mean? This evil impulse is of evil. Your path is already defined. Where is there to break away to? Did you not specify what you wanted, by so desiring freedom for yourself?

There is no reason to seek inner freedom, because it already exists, since it is an integral part of the soul. No one can take it away. It appears that you find external freedom desirable. But consider, if you will, to what extent such freedom would be permissible and attainable. No matter where you would turn, you would be surrounded everywhere by the same sort of 309

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE freedoms as yours, all equal to your freedom. No matter what we intended to do, we would always have to weigh the pros and cons of our actions with those of other people, and be restricted by them, and, consequently, they would hinder our freedom.

No matter what step you would take, there would be interference with freedom, and a reasonable one, too, which, you have to admit, you could not object to. If this is what it is like, then the impulse to freedom is like chasing a rainbow, or even worse, desiring to catch shadows. Thus, in general, it is a thousand times unthinkable for you.

Curb your impulses, if you will. Children listen to their parents and their advice and then arrange their own affairs and lives, obeying them voluntarily. That is the law of freedom for you! But what are you thinking of doing? “I will run off here or there, and no one will dare to tell me where to go.” You are a real emancipationist! Just look at yourself, are you not afflicted with pride? All emancipationists are proud women.
What is wise about dashing off somewhere? We constantly see such people; we just do not see happy ones among them.

What sense could there be in such impulses? None, just fanfare; step aside! “I want this or that! Who tells me what to do?” These lawless people rush along without turning their heads back or looking under foot, while they are throwing themselves into the abyss from which there is no way out. Keep this in mind, if you will, when you are feeling such impulses as “I will do this, I will decide on that.” One of your intentions is very good. Perhaps it could be an indication of what to do with your life? I understand it to be to enter the sisters of mercy. But because it is observed among such disorderly impulses, it is not good. There are still no indications as to what you should do. Wait, as I have already written you several times. The Lord will arrange everything. 310
FINAL ANXIETIES AND DISTURBANCES You have a cozy home. Stay there until the time comes to leave.

May the Lord bless you! 79 FINAL ANXIETIES AND DISTURBANCES Final anxieties and disturbances. Entrusting one's welfare into God's hands with prayer and courage against the enemy who brings temptations upon us.

You still have anxieties. Tell me, where could they have come from? Everything external is in order for you; you have reexamined and adjusted everything internally, and have confirmed your decision. Where could these anxieties come from, then? Everything is from the enemy, everything. From nowhere else.

What else could it be?! Are you not thinking of arranging your life on your own, through your own efforts and abilities, as if that was what you were told?! Take a look, and if this is indeed the case, rush to correct it. With this attitude you will not get rid of your confusion. So there you are. Reexamine yourself, if you please, or mentally repeat everything you were told to do and what has occurred in you, and how you, at last, resolved the matter of your own life; and direct this reexamination in such a way that the end results in a firm decision to place your fate irrevocably in the hands of God. Then begin praying, and after praying fervently, speak before the Lord from your heart, “I place my fate in Thy hands. Lord. In the way Thou knowest, arrange my life, with all its contingencies and happenstances. From now on I cut off every care about myself, having but one care, to always do what is pleasing before Thee.” Speak

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE in this way, and by the very action you will have already placed yourself completely in God’s hands, not concerned about anything, but calmly accepting every sort of situation, as being arranged for you on purpose by God, whether it is pleasant or unpleasant. Your sole concern should be to act according to God’s commandment in every instance. This is all that is required for you.

As soon as you have arranged things in this way, there will be an end to all your worries. You are concerned about yourself at present, and want to arrange and turn all happenstances toward your own end. Since it is not all working out, you are troubled that this is not like this, and that is not like that. When, however, you commit everything to the Lord and accept it as coming from Him and as needed by you for your good, then you will have no troubles, but will only have to look around you to see what the Lord sends, so you may act in accordance with what is sent. Any situation may come under the commandment.

Place it under, and act according to, the commandment, trying to please God instead of exerting yourself to satisfy your own desire. Try to understand well what I am saying, and resolve to achieve this state of mind. It is necessary to learn it; it cannot be done suddenly. Pray about this.

I pray to the Lord that He will deliver you from that situation which you consider unpleasant for yourself, adding, however, if that is in accordance with His holy will and necessary for your salvation. He will deliver you, in His own time, of course. Clothe yourself with faith and be patient. Simply looking at current conditions, we see that they continually change.

Nothing stays the same. Even that which burdens you will change. There will come the day when you will breathe freely, and not only breathe, but flutter about like a butterfly among the flowers; you only have to endure the allotted term with patience. The housewife places a pie in the oven and does not 312

FINAL ANXIETIES AND DISTURBANCES take it out until she is certain that it is baked. The Master of the world has placed you in an oven and is keeping you there, waiting until you are baked. Be patient, then, and wait. As soon as you are ready, you will not have to sit another minute in the oven. You will be taken out immediately. If you rush out of there on your own, you will be like a half-baked pie. Arm yourself with patience. I would also say to you that by our faith, he who endures the troubles he encounters with good cheer, accepting them as being from the hand of the Lord, is a partaker of martyrdom. Imprint this well in your thought, if you will, and draw comfort from it for your heart.

It is impossible to live without emotions, but it is not proper to yield to them. It is necessary to refresh and moderate them through reason, and give them a proper direction. You are impressionable, and your heart spills over into your head. Do as I have already written to you: Think out ahead of time where the possible stimulus for each emotion is and enter into situations keeping yourself on the lookout for disturbances of the heart, or keeping your heart in steady hands. You must practice this, and through practice it is possible to gain full control over yourself.
But everything comes from God. It is necessary to turn to Him. You write, however, that you do not pray. Clever girl!

Have you become an atheist or something? What do you mean, you do not pray? You do not recite all the set prayers, but you tell God in your own words what is on your mind, and you ask for help. “See, Lord, what is happening with me? This thing and that. I cannot cope with myself Help, All-Merciful One!” Tell every little part of your need, and ask personal help for everything. This will be the most genuine prayer. You may always pray with your own prayer, without reading the prayers in the book; just as long as you do not indulge yourself in laziness. 313

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE Why do you listen to the person who suggests that you give up prayer? Or do you not see that this is the enemy? Obviously it is. He murmurs in your ear, “Give it up,” and sometimes, after seizing your entire body, he drags you quickly off to bed. These are all his tricks. But he is going about his business, distracting you from your good work. We, however, must go about our business, not abandoning our good work until we are finished.
Thus, be armed with courage, if you please, and do not listen to the enemy, and do not pay any attention to his murmuring.
It is even better to get angry. Anger at the enemy is the same thing as striking someone in the chest. He will fly off immediately.
I wish with all my heart for you to find peace at last.
REST AFTER A STORM Rest after a storm oftemptations raised up by the adversary.
Well, glory be to God! Through prayer, you have begun to defy your anxieties, or better, to defy the enemy who caused them, and peace has come to you. May the Lord help you to continue such activity.

So you see what a storm you have endured! The words of the Lord have been fulfilled in you: Satan has desired to haveyou, that he may siftyou as wheat} Where did he not toss you?! The Lord allowed it, and he sifted you, first tossing you one way, then the other. What a grace of God that at last you were

REST AFTER A STORM delivered! You are learning. The science of life is accjuired through experience. Remember well what it was like for you during the entire course of this terrible condition, and you will understand it more precisely. All this time you were under attacks of the enemy. You know now what these attacks are like, how they appear and how they depart. Henceforth you will know when you are undergoing an enemy attack. The enemy always masks himself with the illusion of righteousness. Do not look at that, however, but at what is going on inside the soul; that is, the continual anxiety and vague uncertainty. In this way you may guess immediately that the enemy has come; drive him away with harsh admonishments and through prayer. Godly influences, however, are always light-bearing. Your Guardian Angel has put a word of comfort in your ear concerning this.
Get accustomed to listening to him, and he will teach you everything.

I am very glad that you have set out on your path. Labor over yourself, if you will, preparing yourself for what you intended. The enemy is compelling you to hurry. He always alarms us so as to confuse matters. Godly things, however, are peaceful and quiet. It is impossible except with waiting. Everything in its own time. The time will come when it will be downhill the rest of the way, as I have already written.
You intend to move to the village and are dreaming about the pleasures of village life. Good, good! That life has truth.

In cities, especially in capital cities, there is no truth. There, everyone is playing a comedy. May God grant you to reach that place where you quietly grew up and were educated as soon as possible, and safely. How will you remember your sojourn in the ancient capital? It afforded you all kinds of useful learning; especially at the end it duly scorched you in the frying pan. 315

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE You will be an anchoress in the village. I will add something of my own to your dreams. Find a natural cave, or dig one out with your own hands. On one side, if possible, let there be a small fountain; on the other side, some sort of fruit tree; in front, a small garden. Train a few songbirds for the tree and flowers.
Arise early, and, secluding yourself here, sing together with the birds to the glory of the One Who created all!
Oval portrait of St. Theophan from Russky Palomnik [Russian Pilgrim) , 1894

THE Saint Theophan the Recluse Society of America FOUNDED ON ST. PHILIP THE DEACON DAY, OCTOBER 11/24, 1992, AT ST. PAISIUS ABBEY, FORESTVILLE, CALIFORNIA It is important for us 20th Century American lovers of Truth to be “plugged into” the Orthodox Tradition—not only the texts of Orthodoxy, but the context in which the Holy Fathers lived and do live even now. To bring this context to the contemporary “pepsi generation” is to follow in the spirit of St. Theophan who labored so that the average person could understand what spiritual life is all about. This is how he preached the Gospel “to the poor”: he made spiritual life accessible to everyone regardless of education or background. St. Tikhon of Zadonsk in an earlier generation had also labored for this cause.
In fact, it was he that St. Theophan consciously sought to emulate in his apostolic work.

Another attempt of this kind in pre-revolutionary Russia was carried out by Nikon of Vologda. He went to the tomb of St. Sergius of Radonezh and wept over the saint’s relics, seeking an understanding of what to do. There he was inspired to write the Trinity Leaflets, a simple four-fold leaflet which came out weekly. Inside were short homilies and talks that delivered a very simple but powerful message to the spiritually impoverished common man. The Trinity Leaflets became so popular that they 318
ST. THEOPHAN SOCIETY were printed by the millions and distributed throughout the Empire. This same Nikon, after once again weeping at St. Sergius tomb, began to publish a larger weekly. Trinity Word.
Helen Kontzevitch, the wife of the well-known theologian Ivan Kontzevitch and accomplished author and theologian in her own right, considered it the best periodical ever published in Russia.

Desiring to follow in the footsteps of these recent Holy Fathers, a small group ofAmerican Orthodox converts founded The Saint Theophan The Recluse Society OfAmericaXzx.^ in 1992 for the promotion of true Orthodox Enlightenment in the English-speaking world.” Like St. Theophan, we hope to present people with Orthodox Tradition in its fullness, yet in a form which is accessible, and thereby to ignite many souls with zeal for the Lord. Working to accomplish this end, the Society encourages the creation of small groups (local brotherhoods or fellowships) dedicated to studying and practicing Christian spiritual life as taught and understood by St. Theophan, taking his writings (and similar literature by other Holy Fathers) as a sure guide to Christian life in our own times. In addition, the Society has undertaken the publication of important spiritual literature in the English language for distribution throughout the English-speaking world via leaflets, pamphlets and monographs, and via its bulletin: The Path ofSalvation, named after one of the saint’s major works.

There are three types of literature related to St. Theophan available for such study: 1 . literature about him (biographies and memoirs); 2. literature by him (of which there are two categories—original writings by him and the writings of others which were edited and revised by him); 3. writings similar in nature to his that reveal to us the context in which he lived and wrote.
Several good, full biographies exist in Russian, but none in English. To help fill this need, the Society has begun the 319
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE serialization of the saint’s biography written by Fr. Nicholas Deputatov in its Bulletin. Installments appear as they are translated from the original Russian.

Catalogues of the saint’s own works show that he was truly a prolific writer. Not only did he write commentaries upon all the Pauline letters (with the exception of Hebrews) and works on theological subjects, but he carried on a voluminous correspondence during his years of reclusion at Vysha. In addition to these works of original composition, he labored as a translator and editor of other important spiritual texts. Among these are a Russian edition of the Philokalia and the well known classic.
Unseen Warfare, both of which he not only translated, but also reedited to include additional material that would be profitable for the edification of Orthodox readers of his (and our) time.
As to literary writings which are similar in spirit to St. Th eo ph an, there are many. The works of St. Ignatius Brianchaninov, St. John of Kronstadt and St. Tikhon of Zadonsk are all good examples.
